title: Engineering Mechanics
description: Equivalent Force Systems: Basic concepts of force-couple systems. planar firce systems: parallel force systems; simplest equivalent for general force system- "wrench". distributed force systems.Equations of Statics and its Applications: Simple frictionless rigid body assemblies; two foce members: machines: trusses: cables: rigid body assemblies including friction.Virtual Work and Potential Energy Principles: Application of these principles as replacement of equations of statics for real life problems.Vibrations: Equations of motion for single degree-of-freedom systems and rigid body assemblies: free vibration (simple harmonic oscillator): concepts of dampling and cirtical damping: damped free vibration: equations of motion for harmonic excitation: transient and steady-state vibrations: illustration of MDOF systems concepts with two degree-of-freedom systems.

reviews.body:
Course Name: CE102
Review By: Harshit Varma
Name of the Prof: Prof. Sai Vinjanampathy
Year the course was taken in: 1
Attendance Criteria: 0.8
Difficulty of evaluation(How difficult the test and assignment were and how difficult was the grading): 4
Grading Scheme: Quiz-1 10% Quiz-2 10% Midsem 30% Endsem 50% The course has, more or less, a binary grading scheme as partial credit is quite rare, so even a minor mistake can cause a significant loss of marks. Grading statistics can be found on ASC
Time spent on course(hours per week): 4
Pre-requisites(if any): No official pre-requisites. JEE concepts related to Mechanics may be helpful.
Overview(Expect to learn and future prospects): The main objective of this course is to make students understand fundamentals of mechanics and its application to real-life (Natural and Man made) problems. It will also prepare you for higher level courses like solid mechanics, strength of materials. The professor is very enthusiastic about teaching and even solves tutorial problems completely on the board. A lot of time is spent on discussing doubts and solving problems rather than plain theory.
Course Rating: 4

Instructor

Prof. Mandar M. Inamdar

Section

S2 (BC student so had the privilege to choose the section :) but in general first half of the batch was in S1 and other half in S2 )

Semester

Spring

Course Difficulty

Moderate (builds on JEE-level Mechanics)

Time Commitment Required

The initial half of the course is comparatively easy as it involves just the revision of JEE stuff. In the second half though, you will get to learn new (and interesting) concepts. Overall the course tests your problem solving skills, so once you have read the topic, performance boils down to problem solving. Solving the tutorial sets and the additional problems (provided by the instructor) would suffice. So if one is attentive in the class and attends the tutorial sessions regularly then two hours per week would help you sail through the course easily.

Grading

Absolute (80+ AA and so on) but chill

Attendance Policy

80% attendance compulsory, else DX grade

Pre-requisites

None. It builds the foundation for the third sem core course - Solid Mechanics

Evaluation Scheme and Weightages

The evaluation was based on two quizzes (one in each half semester) accounting for 10% each, mid semester examination (accounting for 30%) and end semester examination (accounting for 50%). Good performance in the end semester exam, therefore, was crucially important for a good grade.

Topics Covered in the Course

Introduction and Fundamentals of Mechanics, Equilibrium of 2D and 3D systems, Truss, Friction, Methods of Virtual Work(you’ll surely love this) and Potential Energy, Vibrations.

Quality of Lectures

The instructor has a good command over the subject and explains the concepts well. Though the course is purely based on problem solving, the instructor makes sure that you can qualitatively draw conclusions from the problem. The classes are very interactive and probably you won’t sleep during the lectures (:p). After each sub-topic the instructor solves a problem to give an insight into the applications of the theory learnt because at the end that is all that matters in terms of the evaluation in the course. In general, you won’t regret attending his lectures ;P

We thank the Mechanical DAMP team for this review

Course Name: CE102
Review By: Arjun Agarwal
Name of the Prof: Abhishek Gupta and Shashikanth
Year the course was taken in: 1

Overview(Expect to learn and future prospects): Attendance was a bit strict, prof won't let you use your phone or zone out, will keep asking everyone questions and will keep looking at your notebooks regarding what you're solving. Quizzes and exams can be solved easily if you can solve all tutorials and assignments yourself.
Course Rating: 4

Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Prof. Yogesh M. Desai

Well to begin with the course is largely an extension from the basics of mechanics that all of us have had to learn over 11th and 12th. In fact in the first class, Yogesh sir listed down the basic formulae on the board and stated ‘You now know everything you need to know about this course!’. So the reason why we do have the course is not so much content building as it is learning how to apply it in more real-life situations. It is very numerical/practice oriented, with minimal theory as such.

It has largely been constant across years, repeating what was said in an earlier review here for convenience :

Planar and parallel forces, Equivalent Force couple systems, Wrench, Distributed forces, Equation Of Statics, Friction, Trusses and cables, Virtual Work Method , Potential Energy Method, Vibrations-equation of motion, degrees of freedom, Damping, Harmonic Oscillators.

None, it is the DIC after all.

Well personally I found it to be a very enjoyable course, largely because the content was something we all had studied before, and because the Professor was really enthusiastic about the course and understood the class well. While he is very strict about certain issues (like carrying the course booklet and calculator to every class), it never is a burden, and over time everyone understands why it’s important. The classes had solving sessions incorporated, and tutorials at times for added practice. The TAs were really helpful too, and that made things a lot easier. Attendance rules do apply (>80% I guess), though I believe this is one course where attending every class/tutorial session would go a long way to understanding the problem solving techniques required to solve the problems in the quizzes and exams. It also is the first course offering some sort of insight into what the department has to offer, which is why among the courses in the semester this is the one with most long term significance and hence do give it the required amount of attention and effort.


Click here to continue reading

description: Introduction to Plane & Geodetic Surveying, Fundamental Principles, Earth Ellipsoid, Geodetic Datums and Coordinate Systems, Control Surveys: Horizontal & Vertical: Triangulation, Traverse, Leveling, Instrumentation: Total Stations, EDMs, Digital Levels; Engineering Surveying, Data Processing, Legendre302222s Theorem, Theory of Errors and Adjustment Computations, Introduction to Space Geodetic Techniques: GPS, Introduction to Photogrammetry, Remote Sensing, Maps & Map-making.

Professor who took the course (2019-20)

Prof. Rao E P and Prof. Indu J

Motivation behind the course

This course focuses on the importance of surveying and on the various techniques, simple to complex, that are used for surveying in Engineering. This course aims to build a foundation in the Remote Sensing Specialisation of Civil Engineering and thus is largely different from other solid and fluid mechanics core courses.

Course Content

Course starts with simple concepts of Plane and Geodetic Surveying and then techniques that are used in surveying like Compass Traversing, Chain Surveying, Theodolite, Levelling, Plane Table surveying, Triangulation, Tachometric Surveying, GPS surveying etc are discussed. Usage of various Instruments that are used in these different techniques is also taught in the course.

Motivation behind the course

Course Content

The practical topics are same as taught in CE204-Geodesy:


Click here to continue reading

Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Prof. Jayadipta Ghosh

This course is perhaps the first step towards civil engineering. All the second-year students are expected to take this course as per the regular curriculum. It is an 8 credit course that includes the study of the stresses, strains, deformation, and motion of solid materials under the action of loads or forces. It is very interesting and important in order to understand the fundamentals of other structural courses. 

CE 102 (Engineering Mechanics) is a prerequisite for this course. Most of the concepts learned in CE 102 are applied in this course and having done that course properly and understanding it well will help you perform well in this course too.

For me, this course was the most interesting course among all the courses that I had done from 1st to 3rd semesters and I can clearly say that it was to a large extent because of the way our Prof. Jayadipta Ghosh taught us. He explains each and every topic very clearly and you will not feel bored in his class at all. If you attend all the lectures and tutorials, you can do very well in the course. The course content as a whole is also interesting, we need to solve numerical questions applying some come concepts of physics and mathematics and I personally enjoyed it. This course is very important in order to understand the courses in the upcoming semesters such as CE 222 and CE 317. So, try to perform well in this course as it is an 8 credit course and matters a lot not only CPI but also for understanding the upcoming structural courses.

There will be 2 quizzes each of 10% weightage, a mid-semester examination of 30% weightage, and an end semester examination of 50% weightage. You need to be very clear with the concepts as there are some questions that require the use of multiple concepts put together in order to solve them. It will be very helpful if you practice problems from the tutorials and the lecture notes. Also, practicing problems from recommended textbooks and past year papers will be very beneficial.

You will find the course very easy if you attend all the lectures and tutorials and are attentive during the classes. But, you may find it difficult if you lose track during the semesters as topics are interrelated to each other. Also, this course needs a lot of practice to solve the problems and to apply the concepts correctly. You need to be consistent to perform well.


Click here to continue reading

title: Structural Mechanics I
description: Analysis of Statically Determinate Structures - Determination of forces in trusses, frames, arches, and cables; Drawing bending moment, shear force and axial force diagrams; Computation of displacements using principle of virtual work; Moment-area method, Conjugate-beam method; Energy Principles; Maxwell’s and Betti’s laws; Analysis of Statically Indeterminate Structures - Concept of static indeterminacy; Concept of compatibility conditions; Method of consistent deformations for statically indeterminate trusses, beams, frames, arches; Matrix formulation of force method; Influence Lines - Concept of influence lines using equilibrium methods, and by using Muller Breslau principle for both statically determinate and indeterminate structures

In future on whatever project you work on your base should be strong. Similar is the case for Civil Engineering if you are going to construct a multistoried building or built an Expressway, a bridge in the ocean the base which will support the structure for years should be stable. And the base of all structures in soil. If soil is not stable then its movement in centimeters can lead to disasters. So, from the Civil Engineering perspective it is one of the important courses about which you should know if you are entering the field of Engineering. This makes the course interesting in itself.

title: Engineering Law
description: The Indian legal system from an engineer’s perspective. Socratic-method analysis of statutory and case law. Contract, patent, corporation, antitrust, property, and environmental laws. Development of law, courts, and ethics; law on contracts, agency, sales, property, and patterns; specifications; preparation of contract documents. Coverage of following topics: 1. Overview of Indian legal system, 2. Contracts: Definitions and essentials, 3. Conditions: 3rd parties and law, 4. Construction: Performance/Breach/Damages and Contracts, 5. General Conditions: Proposals, advertisements and applications, 6. Financial Considerations: Loans and leans, 7. Specifications: Workmanship, material, drawings, 8. Agencies: Partnerships and corporations, 9. Torts: Limited coverage, 10. Professional liability: Insurance, bonds, property, 11. Litigation: Evidence, expert witness, mediation, ethics.

reviews.body:
Course review


Details

  • Reviewer name: Vansh Chandarana
  • Roll number: 23b0659
  • Department: Civil
  • Year of study: 3rd
  • Course: Engineering law
  • Professor: Milind wankhede
  • Quizzes/exams: 0
  • Assignments: 0
  • Additional components: Presentations


Prerequisites necessary (if any)

None, beginner-friendly


Course difficulty

Assessment format: Closed-book.

Typical quiz/exam questions: Direct from lectures.

Grading style: Lenient.


Overview

Course focus: Theory-heavy.

Most emphasized topics: Law.

Best suited for: Who can rote learn.


Personal opinion

Teaching style: Slides.

Additional resources: Rarely.

Advice for future students: Rote learn the small portion, nothing will come outside from there

Instructor

Milind Wankhede

Semester

Spring ‘20

Course Difficulty

Quite chill, a very different kind of course, so it’s quite tough to compare with regular engineering courses.

Time Commitment Required

Not more than an hour a week in addition to regular lectures.

Grading Policy and Statistics

Exams are quite high-scoring, so it may be difficult to get an AA. With very minimal effort one can end up with a grade >= BB.
13 AAs, 10 ABs and 20 BBs out of 90.

Attendance Policy

None

Pre-requisites

None

Evaluation Scheme

Midsem - 30%
Endsem - 50%
Mock Arbitration - 20%

Topics Covered in the Course

The Indian Contract Act, 1872
Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996

Teaching Style

Classes were live on Microsoft Teams, the professor would explain the sections with real world cases. Lectures were interesting and certainly worth attending.

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

The mock arbitration is a novel idea. Pre-assigned groups of six were assigned a real-world case and had to don the hat of a defendant, respondent or an arbitrator. While this would’ve been more fun offline, it was still quite engaging in the online mode. It gave a brief idea of how legal arguments need to be posed.

Feedback on Exams

The exams were based on interpretation of various sections. This may involve a fair bit of memory, but the professor is very considerate and provides a list of sections from where questions will be asked, as well as model question papers. This makes it a lot easier than it would’ve been otherwise.

Motivation for taking this course

To try something different, a Law course at IITB, whoever thought about that!

Course Importance

Not really relevant to anything else in the institute, just a fun glimpse of what law is all about.

How strongly would I recommend this course?

Very strongly, it provides a very different experience compared to any other course you would ever take at IITB!

When to take this course?

6th Semester. The course runs every semester, so you can take it anytime, no particular preference!

Going Forward

Nothing really, although the professor does offer some BTP topics for further study.

References Used

www.indiacode.nic.in contains all the required sections and acts

CE 324 Review By: Aditya Iyengar

We thank the Mechanical DAMP team for this review

Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Professor who took the course (2019-20)

Prof. Arghadeep Laskar

Motivation behind the course

The motivation behind the course is to introduce students with the concepts of design and analysis of Reinforced Concrete (RC) structures, familiarize with Indian Standard codes, and general behaviour of RC structures such as beams, columns, slabs, staircases etc. on multiple stress applications.

Course Content

The course starts with the introduction to material properties of steel and concrete such as their stress-strain curve, critical failure point etc. The professor also teaches how to properly use IS codes (mainly IS 456 & SP-16) for analysis & designing of various structures along with various design philosophies, bending theory and serviceability conditions.s
Before directly teaching about designing of structure, professor familiarizes the concepts and formulas to be used with the analysis of existing structures with examples such as the maximum load which can be subjected to the structure etc.
The first part of the course deals with the analysis and design for shear forces through beams and one-way slabs; while the second part deals with compression forces through columns, two-way slabs and staircases.


Click here to continue reading

Course Instructor :- Milind Wankhede


This course isn't meant for those who wanna learn LAW just for learning. The course took off pretty smoothly but was way too much to bear after the midsems, like you can't even compare the dissimilarities it possesses. Also, you'll have to learn all the numbers of the particular section of the law (pretty much like the IS codes for Construction).

In the end, it also depends on how you manage your time but one thing I'd say is don't fall for the previous stats. Undoubtedly, this is one of the best topics to voyage through your interests.

title: Numerical Techniques in Hydraulic Engg
description:  Mathematical Modeling: Mathematical tools and techniques, Advanced modeling applications to water resources and environmental engineering problems.  System of Equations & Solutions– Linear equations; Iterative methods, direct methods, conjugate gradient method; Newton Raphson method; sparse matrices and compact storage schemes; Inversion of matrices; Non-linear equations and solutions; Gauss Jordan method; Gauss Siedel method; Eigen Values and Vectors & applications;  Numerical Solution of Ordinary Differential Equations: Single step method, multi-step method; Runge-Kutta methods; Numerical differentiation and integration; boundary value and initial boundary value problems – Applications in water resources/ ocean/ environmental engineering.  Partial differential equations & Solutions: different types and solution approaches.  Finite Difference Method: Various Finite difference schemes, implicit and explicit methods, ADI method; Method of Characteristics - Applications in water resources/ ocean/ environmental engineering.  Approximate Methods – Method of weighted residuals (Sub-domain, Collocation, Least squares, Galerkin, Raleigh-Ritz methods), Applications to solve linear and nonlinear differential equations.  Finite Element Method: Discretization of domain – Grid generation, Interpolating polynomials, Integration of shape functions over the domain; Formulation of element matrices and its global assembly - Applications in water resources/ ocean/ environmental engineering.  Introduction to boundary element method and Meshfree methods - Applications in water resources/ ocean/ environmental engineering.  Applications of various computer software based on finite difference method & finite element method – Applications in groundwater flow, Fluid dynamics, Pollutant transport in surface and subsurface water

title: Chemistry
description: Organic Chemistry MO and photochemistry: Importance of bonding and anti-bonding molecular orbitals involving sigma, sigma*, pi, pi* and non-bonding MOs. Applications in 1) Understanding property and reactivity; 2) Rationalising pericyclic reactions (Electrocyclic and Cycloaddition), Basic concepts in organic photochemistry and its applications. Chemistry in 3D: Visualization of tetrahedral carbon, representation of molecules using projection formulae (Fischer, Newman, sawhorse, flying wedge) and their interconversion. Significance in drugs and materials application. Factors affecting rotational barrier, stability and reactivity of alkanes, cyclohexane, cyclohexene and decalin systems. Inorganic Chemistry HSAB and Non-Covalent Interactions: Introduction to Hard-Soft Acid-Base concept and their applications in chemical reactions with a suitable experimental demonstration. Introduction of polarizability, weak intermolecular interactions such hydrogen bonding, p-p, cation-p and Anion-p interactions. Experimental demonstrations of weak intermolecular interactions and relative strength of various intermolecular interactions using liquid nitrogen. Chemistry Behind Separation of Metals from Ores: Basic principles of extraction of metal from its ore and purification. Various separation methods, Electrochemical displacement with experimental demonstration. High-temperature reduction methods (Ellingham diagram) and their real-world applications. Coordination Compounds and their Applications: Chemistry of Transition metal, General properties of transition metal ions, theories of bonding that explain the physical properties of transition metal complexes (UV-Vis absorption, magnetic properties, other spectroscopic aspects with suitable experimental demonstrations. Crystal field stabilization energy and their applications, Jahn-Teller Distortion and its influence on the orbital splitting pattern. Molecular orbital theory of M-L sigma and p- bonding (p-acceptor and donor ligands). Application of Coordination compounds in Magnetic and Nano Materials (Basic Building Blocks, Principles): Introduction to Magnetism, Magnetic properties of transition and lanthanide metal ions/complexes, Zeeman effect, Curie-law, Curie-Weiss law, Exchange interactions (application of exchange interaction in real-world application with experimental demonstration). Mechanism (qualitative) of exchange coupling between the transition metal ions, first-order orbital angular momentum and spin-crossover complexes. Relationship between coordination compounds and their application in Nanotechnology and related area. Application of Coordination compounds in Biology: Introduction to bio-Inorganic Chemistry, Hemoglobin, Myoglobin, pH-dependent O2 binding (with possible experimental demonstration), cooperative effect and CO poisoning. Physical Chemistry Schrodinger equation, Origin of quantization, Born interpretation of wave function, application (module 1) of PIB to understand band structure of materials. Hydrogen atom: solution to -part, Atomic orbitals, many electron atoms and spin orbitals. Chemical bonding: MO theory: LCAO molecular orbitals, Structure, bonding and energy levels of diatomic molecules. Application of (module 2) MO theory towards predicting molecular properties. Concept of sp, sp2 and sp3 hybridization; Bonding and shape of many atom molecules. Molecular spectroscopy - rotational, vibrational, electronic. Application (module 3) of molecular spectroscopy to understand chemical and biological systems.

reviews.body:
Name: Gautham Mallya
Roll Number: 200110041
Year of Study: to-be 4th Year
Section: S1
Course: CH 504: Computational Chemistry
Professor(s): Prof. R.B. Sunoj, Prof. Rajarshi Chakrabarti
Course Type: Institute Elective
Semester: Spring (Even)
Mode of Course: Offline
Frequency of the Course: Every Year
Pre-Requisite: No prequisites whatsoever
Learning Outcome: This course introduced us to softwares used in Computational Chemistry like, Gaussian for micro-level analysis and LAMMPS for macro-level analysis. These can prove useful for applications like drug discovery, new material development, proteomics, quantum chemistry, among many others.
Pre-Midsem Course Content: Concept of Potential Energy Surface, Molecular Mechanics, Quantum Mechanics in Computational Chemistry, Ab-initio Calculations, Levels of Theory and Basis Sets
Post-Midsem Course Content: Lennard-Jones Fluid, TIP4P Water Model, Polymer-Solvent (Good, Bad and Theta Solvent) Interactions
Slides [Ease of Understanding]: 5
Slides [Completeness]: 4
Slides [Depth]: 3
Slides [Usefulness for exams]: 4
Teaching method: Every week there is one lecture and one lab session. The professors teach only in the lecture and the lab sessions are conducted mainly by TAs. The professors explain the concepts broadly in the lecture(they do not provide slides; Prof Sunoj writes on the board and Prof Rajarshi explains verbally) and in the lab session there is an assignment implementing them. The assignments consisted of interesting practical problems requiring the application of Computational chemistry.
Grading Scheme: Exact weightages are not known as there was a Group Project/Activity component that was removed. Roughly 30% for endsem, 20% for lab exams, rest for lab assignments.
Feedback on Evaluation: One lecture and one lab session every week of 1.5 hours each. Lab sessions involved assignments in Gaussian or LAMMPS and step-by-step procedure or guidelines were given in the assignment itself on how to complete it. TAs helped in case of any doubts. Assignments were therefore easy to complete. Endsem exam was slightly difficult, however the evaluation was very lenient.
Nature of the Course: Time Needed: Light
Nature of the Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding: Moderate
Course Grading: Very Lenient
Course Grading Review: Grading is very chill. Scoring AA/AB is quite easy, due to the relatively less amount of effort that has to be put in overall.
Reference Texts: 1. Computational Chemistry: Introduction to the Theory and Applications of Molecular and Quantum Mechanics by Errol G. Lewars, 2. Introduction to Modern Statistical Mechanics by David Chandler

title: Introduction to Chemical Engg.
description: Historical overview of Chemical Engineering: Concepts of unit operations and unit processes, and more recent developments, Features of organized chemical processing- from chemistry to chemical engineering. The Chemical Industry-scope, features & characteristics. and scope. Principles of balancing with examples to illustrate differential and integral balances, lumped and distributed balances. Material balances in simple systems involving physical changes and chemical reactions; systems involving recycle, purge. and bypass. Properties of substances: single component & multicomponent, single and multiphase systems. Use of Compressibility charts, vapour pressure correlations/charts & Psychometric charts. Ideal liquid and gaseous mixtures. Energy balance calculations in simple systems. Introduction to Computer aided calculations-steady state material and energy balances.

Review 1:

Instructor: Prof. Sharad Bhartiya (2019)

Overview and Content: The course is intended to give students a basic understanding of probability and statistical data analysis and their applications in Chemical Engineering. The course was divided into several modules, namely probability, random variables and expectation, distributions, parameter estimation, hypothesis testing and regression. The course also used R as a computational platform, and tutorial problems the final part of the course were to be solved using R.

Feedback on Lectures: Lectures were interesting, although only around 50% of the class would be present on non-quiz days. The instructor explained the concepts in detail with a lot of relevant examples (he continued to upload normal 1.5 hour long lectures even after the semester was interrupted). One or two lectures were also R demonstration sessions and tutorials and lecturese were often swapped. Attentiveness during the lectures and tutorials was sufficient to get a high score in tutorial quizzes.

Attendance: Not compulsory

Course: CL202: Introduction to Data Analysis
Professor(s): Prof Santosh Noronha
Course Type: Core Course
Semester: Spring (Even)
Mode of Course: Offline
Frequency of the Course: Every Year
Pre-Requisite: nan
Learning Outcome: The course content is fairly easy while also being highly relevant. It covers probability, statistics, and data analysis. The professor also delved into the fundamental concepts of machine learning.
Pre-Midsem Course Content: The course content starts with random variables and expectations. This is followed by learning about some special random variables (Bernoulli, Poisson, etc.) and the distributions they follow. The course then covers parameter estimation and finally, hypothesis testing. The initial topics overlap with those covered during JEE preparation, which builds momentum for the rest of the course content. A lot of emphasis is laid on hypothesis testing, which is an important concept taught in this course.
Post-Midsem Course Content: Post mid-sem, another week was spent completing hypothesis testing. Subsequently, non-parametric hypothesis testing was taught. This was extended to a multi-variate analysis. At this point, the course gets slightly challenging. The next topic covered was regression, and the course finally ended with ANOVA (analysis of variance). Additionally, the professor also introduced us to information theory and pattern classification in the last few classes. Post-midsem content required more effort compared to pre-midsem.
Slides [Ease of Understanding]: 4
Slides [Completeness]: 4
Slides [Depth]: 4
Slides [Usefulness for exams]: 3
Teaching method: The professor taught the course really well. He would make us aware of the relevance of every topic covered, in daily life. He would give sufficient time in class to solve problems and would discuss them in depth. We were given lengthy problem sets as take-home assignments and subsequently provided with hints/solutions. Quizzes and exams could be aced if these were done well. The classes were interesting. We were asked to estimate the number of deaths caused by covid-19 as a class participation assignment. We made multiple submissions for the same, as more concepts were taught in the course.
Grading Scheme: Attendance was not compulsory Surprise quizzes and class participation: 20%, Midsem: 30%, Endsem: 50%
Feedback on Evaluation: Both the midsem and endsem exams were fairly easy if the tutorials were solved well. Quizzes were more challenging and not announced beforehand. Questions from these quizzes repeated in the midsem and endsem exams. Tutorials were take-home problem sheets, given at the conclusion of each topic.
Nature of the Course: Time Needed: Moderate
Nature of the Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding: Moderate
Course Grading: Moderate
Course Grading Review: Of the 148 grades that were given, most were CC and BC. The grading was moderate, and a fair distribution of the number of students was done for each grade (although AAs and ABs were given to a small number of students)
Reference Texts: Introduction to Probability and Statistics for Engineers and Scientists - Sheldon M. Ross (very helpful!) The professor also gave his own notes for each topic that were quite comprehensive. Many tutorial problems were taken directly from the reference book.
Photo: nan
Comments: This is an 8-credit course, which is both interesting and relevant. A little effort and consistency are sufficient to ace the course.

title: Introduction to Transport Phenomena
description: Introduction: Vectors/Tensors, Viscosity, Shell balance: Falling film, Circular tube; Equations of Change for isothermal systems: Continuity, Motion, Energy, Substantial derivatives; Unidirectional flows: Pipe flow, Variable viscosity falling film, Couette viscometer, Rotating Sphere; Unsteady flows: Startup Plate flow, Parallel plates, Oscillating plate; Thermal conductivity and mechanism of energy transport; Shell energy balances and temperature distributions in solids and laminar flow; The equations of change for nonisothermal systems; Diffusivity and the mechanisms of mass transport; Concentration distributions in solids and laminar flow; Equations of change for multicomponent systems; Introduction to the concept of heat and mass transfer coefficients.

reviews.body: You will be confused if this a chemical course or a maths course. Intensely mathematical, won't lie it made my life hell. One good thing was cheat sheets were allowed in all the exams. There were 2quizes, 1 midsem and 1 endsem. You'll have to deal with a lot of linear algebra, vectors and tensors. Grading? Dont even ask.

Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Section: S1

Professor: Prof. Venkat Gundabala

Motivation: This course creates the foundation for the majority of chemical engineering courses. As the name suggests, it deals with how energy, fluid and mass are transported and revolves around Navier Stocks equation. This course also gives an insight about vectors and tensors use in differential mathematical equations.

Prerequisites: None

Feedback on lectures: The professor follows the prescribed book very closely. He takes the book examples and derivation to explain the concept and its use. Tutorials, quizzes and papers are completely based on the back exercises of the book. One should read the book along with the professor teaching to excel.

Evaluation Scheme:


Click here to continue reading

Instructor

Prof. Hemant Nanavati

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Autumn

Pre-Requisite

There is particularly no hard/soft pre-requisites for this. The mathematical concepts (for eg. vectors, Einstein notation, RTT etc) which needs to be clarified for this course will be covered in detail by the professor himself in the starting of the course.

Learning Outcome

As the name suggest, it is understanding how any matter transport in real time, be it a energy, fluid, solid or anything. This course involves the basic physics of many of the other courses and even daily life problems. In the 4th semester you will encounter a course CL254 – Process Fluid Mechanics in which the concepts taught in Transport Phenomenon is rigorously used. And even in other courses like CL246 – Heat Transfer the concepts studied in this course are used.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

The course commences with the concepts of mathematics which will be used in the entire course. Those concepts include:
1. Vector and their properties
2. Different operators like gradients, curls etc.
3. Introduction to tensors
4. Some Matrix properties
2. Einstein notation of vectors
3. Integral theorems
After these concepts the course will dive into microscopic balances. This includes energy balance, mass balance and momentum balance for solving different pratical application of life.
According to me, the stress tensor and the normal tensor part was the toughest among everything covered before the midsem. Except that nothing was tough. Mostly every stuff covered was similar to the concepts studied in JEE.

Post-Midsem Course Content

After midsem, prof. majorly covered macroscopic balances with many of its apllications. Some basics concept like Bingham fluid, Navier Stokes equation, Bernoulli eqaution etc.
At this point you will start exploring how the concepts learnt in the whole course is applied to the pratical life problem, by analysing the complete view of the problem, and balancing eqautions to get to the required solution.
According to me, after midsem was much rigorous and tougher than that of before midsem. Therefore, I strongly recommend you all to be regular after midsem, and follow the professor. Try not forgeting things that were taught earlier, because there might be time when the prof. assumes it by deafult that the concepts that are taught earlier need not to be mentioned everytime. So you should try to understand all the things that taught in the lectures after the lecture gets over.

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 1

Completeness: 2

Depth: 2

Usefulness for exams:1

Teaching method

Prof. Hemant Nanavati taught the course very well. The basics concepts of mathematics were covered entirely in the lectures. Just a small thing was that his pace after midsem increases exponentially, therefore I recommend all off you to go parallel to the prof even after the midsem too.
All the lectures and tutorials were regularly conducted with no postponement or delay. The professor was strict in examination, and ensured that no malpractices occur.

Grading Scheme

Midsem = 35
Endsem = 45
Quizzes = 20

Feedback on Evaluation

There were a total of 4 quizzes conducted in the complete semester and they were all conducted uniformly in the entire semester. The midsem and endsem exams of this course have a bit different patterns. It is conducted in parts i.e. part 1 of 45 min then 10 min break, 45 min part 2 and then 10 min break, and so on. So students freak out because of this pattern but there is nothing to freak out about. Even I find it useful as a sufficient amount of breaks in between the exams helps us stable our mind, and recompile our thoughts for the next part. So just try to chill and try your best in the exam.
Quizzes are easy, just that you have to go through the lectures thoroughly and try the tutorial problem.

Nature of the Course: Time Needed

Moderate

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Diffficult

Course Grading

Harsh

Course Grading Review:

In our batch, the performance was not that good. The overall average of the class was less than 50%. And since the exams were low-scoring papers, a BB grade was attained even on 40 marks. So if the paper was low scoring or tough, don’t freak out. Everything will work good.

Reference Texts

Transpost Phenomenon by R. Byron Bird, Warren E. Stewart, Edwin N. Lightfoot

We thank the Chemical DAMP team for this review

Instructor

Prof. Guruswamy Kumaraswamy

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Autumn (Odd)

Mode of Course

Online

Pre-Requisite

None

Learning Outcome

This course introduces you to the fundamentals of many Chemical Engineering topics like Fluid Dynamics, Heat Transfer, and Mass Transfer and hence will form your base before taking third and fourth year courses. It takes a deep-dive into explanations behind a lot of physical phenomena you see in the real world.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Week 1: Scalars, Vectors, Mass, Momentum, and Mechanical Energy Conservation
Week 2: Continuum, Stress Tensor, Einstein Notation
Week 3: Velocity Gradient Tensor, Constitutive Relation, Cylindrical Coordinates, Continuity Equation
Week 4: Eulerian and Lagrangian Frames, Substantial Derivatives, Reynold’s Transport Theorem, Navier Stokes and Euler Equation
Week 5: Fluid Statics, Unidirectional Flows, Couette Flow
Week 6: Dimensional Analysis, Non-Dimensionalization and its Applications

I think you might take some time wrapping your head around vectors and tensors, their operations and related Einstein notation. However it is necessary to be clear with them for understanding further topics. Also, Non-dimensionalization of equations is where things might start becoming slightly complicated or abstract. A little effort needs to be invested in getting this concept right so that you do not feel lost in related problems.

Post-Midsem Course Content

Week 9: Energy Equation, Conduction, Convection, Heat Flux, Heat Transfer Coefficient
Week 10: Forced Convection, Free Convection, Non-Dimensional Numbers
Week 11: Radiative Heat Transfer, View Factors, Radiation Networks
Week 12: Mass Transfer, Species Balances, Diffusion in Binary Systems, Fick’s Laws
Week 13: Different Cases of Diffusion, Mass Transfer with Flow

The topics of Convection and Diffusion are equation-heavy. You would find yourself navigating through many differential equations. Practice is required to get yourself comfortable with these topics.

Along with the course material for every week a problem set is given, typically consisting of some problems from the prescribed textbook BSL and some from other sources. Some questions in the problem sets can be extremely challenging and not have a simple solution. A deep understanding of fundamentals is required to be good at solving these problems. Often you might need to refer to the textbook solution manual.

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 3

Completeness: 4

Depth: 4

Usefulness for exams: 3

Teaching method

The professor gave us recorded lectures to watch on a weekly basis. Also, he conducted a separate live lecture every week wherein he would solve doubts regarding the recorded lectures, solve additional problems and also give some more information. In addition to this a weekly tutorial session was taken where we had to present solutions to some problem set questions in groups of 4. This tutorial session was usually followed by a weekly quiz of 4% course weightage. These weekly quizzes were great in the sense that we had to keep revising every week and this reduced our load for midsem and endsem. Teaching-wise Prof. Guru was amazing and he was approachable for any doubts. He also conducted guest lectures to introduce us to CFD software and its uses in practical world.

Grading Scheme

1. Tutorials : Group will solve problem set and email the written solution (these will not be corrected/graded) 10% for regular, on-time submission.
2. During tutorial: Each group will present solution to a problem (5%). Every member from the group needs to present. Short viva.
3. 9 Quizzes. Best 7 will be considered. 4% each (28%).
4. Midsem (20%)
5. Endsem (30%) – Open notes
6. Research paper presentation (7%, after midsem)

There was no strict attendance policy for the live lectures as it was an online semester.

Feedback on Evaluation

Tutorials were weekly and problem set questions were presented by students. Evaluation for this component was lenient.
Research Paper presentation was a small course project with 7% weightage which was also evaluated leniently (class avg. nearly 5/7).
Weekly Quizzes were easy to moderate and were a significant chunk with 28% in total. This component was relatively much easier to score in than midsem and endsem.
Midsem and Endsem were the toughest to score in. Midsem average was 6.3/20 and Endsem average was 8.9/30.

Nature of the Course: Time Needed

Moderate

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Difficult

Course Grading

Harsh

Course Grading Review

The number of people scoring AA/AB was quite low. Majority of the class was in the grade range of CC to BB. These were the grading stats:

AA 2
AB 5
BB 22
BC 26
CC 17
CD 4
DD 2
FF 3
FR 1
II 6

Reference Texts

Introductory Transport Phenomena by Bird, Stewart, Lightfoot (commonly referred to as BSL): Main textbook followed by the Professor, helpful in terms of reinforcing whatever was taught in class and for problem practice

Additional Remarks

Be regular in solving tutorials and studying for weekly quizzes, as it will keep your overall load less and help you for the midsem and endsem.

We thank Gautham Mallya for sharing the course review.

We thank the Chemical DAMP team for this review

title: Introduction to Numerical Analysis
description: Interpolation by polynomials, divided differences, error of the interpolating polynomial, piecewise linear and cubic spline interpolation. Numerical integration, composite rules, error formulae. Solution of a system of linear equations, implementation of Gaussian elimination and Gauss-Seidel methods, partial pivoting, row echelon form, LU factorization Cholesky’s method, ill-conditioning, norms. Solution of a nonlinear equation, bisection and secant methods. Newton’s method, rate of convergence, solution of a system of nonlinear equations, numerical solution of ordinary differential equations, Euler and Runge-Kutta methods, multi-step methods, predictor-corrector methods, order of convergence, finite difference methods, numerical solutions of elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic partial differential equations. Eigenvalue problem, power method, QR method, Gershgorin’s theorem. Exposure to software packages like IMSL subroutines, MATLAB.

Review 1:

Section: S2

Professor: Prof. Sarika Mehra (2019)

Aim and Motivation: Concisely speaking, the aim of this course is to learn numerical methods to solve equations that cannot be solved analytically. The equations considered include linear and nonlinear algebraic equations, linear and nonlinear ordinary differential equations as well as some simple partial differential equations. The course runs parallel to the Computational Methods Lab, where these methods are utilised to solve equations pertinent to the chemical industry. 

Prerequisites: None

Name: Sharvil Dayal
Roll Number: 210020125
Year of Study: to-be 3rd Year
Section: S1
Course: CL 244
Professor(s): Ratul Dasgupta
Course Type: Core Course
Semester: Autumn (Odd)
Mode of Course: Offline
Frequency of the Course: Every Year
Pre-Requisite: There were no pre-requisites as such. Knowing basic calculus, linear algebra as taught in the first year courses was enough for the start
Learning Outcome: The course helped students understand numerical methods which involve root finding, interpolation, integration, and solving higher order differential equations. Moreover, students gained insights into the selection of appropriate numerical methods based on the specific problem at hand, thereby enhancing their proficiency in utilizing these techniques effectively.
Pre-Midsem Course Content: The topics taught before mid-sem were : round-off and truncation errors, Taylor series for errors, bracketing methods and open methods
Post-Midsem Course Content: The topics taught after mid-sem were : Eigendecomposition of a matrix, power method, inverse iteration method, Root finding methods, finding roots of multidimensional equations, interpolation, error estimation
Slides [Ease of Understanding]: 4
Slides [Completeness]: 5
Slides [Depth]: 4
Slides [Usefulness for exams]: 5
Teaching method: The professor taught the course quite well. Lectures were interesting and both students as well as prof enjoyed the course.
Grading Scheme: 20% Quizzes, 30% Midsem and 50% Endsem
Feedback on Evaluation: The prof took like 1 quiz a month, there were no assignments or projects. The midsem and endsem exams were of moderate level with balanced easy and difficult questions
Nature of the Course: Time Needed: Moderate
Nature of the Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding: Moderate
Course Grading: Moderate
Course Grading Review: The prof did absolute grading by considering the toppers' marks to be 100% and then adjusting the marks of other students proportionally. From my point of view, the grading was done quite logically because it set a clear standard for the highest performance in the class and allowed for differentiation among the rest of the students based on their relative performance.
Reference Texts: The book suggested was Numerical Methods by Chapra and Canale
Photo: nan
Comments: Please don't keep backlogs. Though the concepts are easy to understand, they can frustrate you in the end since there are many of them. You will have to understand many methods and keep them in mind. Tbh the course is quite enjoyable.

Name: Krishna Baldwa
Roll Number: 210110066
Year of Study: to-be 3rd Year
Section: S2
Course: CL 244
Professor(s): Prof. Sarika Mehra
Course Type: Core Course
Semester: Autumn (Odd)
Mode of Course: Offline
Frequency of the Course: Every Year
Pre-Requisite: Basics of Matrices, Differentiation and Integration
Learning Outcome: Topics from this course will be helpful in the course CL202: Introduction to Data Analysis, which usually is a core course in 4th semester.
Pre-Midsem Course Content: Topics covered before Midsem include: Interpolation by polynomials, divided differences, error of the interpolating polynomial, piecewise linear and cubic spline interpolation. Numerical integration, composite rules, error formulae. Solution of a system of linear equations, implementation of Gaussian elimination and Gauss-Seidel methods, partial pivoting, row echelon form, LU factorization Cholesky’s method, ill-conditioning, norms. Solution of a nonlinear equation, bisection and secant methods. Newtons method, rate of convergence, solution of a system of nonlinear equations. Power Method. The topics which maybe a little tough among these are partial pivoting and LU factorization.
Post-Midsem Course Content: Topics covered post-midsem include: Eigenvalue problem, power method, QR method. Numerical solution of ordinary differential equations. Euler and Runge-Kutta methods, multi-step methods, predictor-corrector methods, order of convergence, finite difference methods, numerical solutions of elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic partial differential equations. Post-midsem topics are in general tougher to understand than the pre-midsem ones.. Here multi-step methods, predictor-corrector methods,finite difference methods needs to be focused properly
Slides [Ease of Understanding]: 4
Slides [Completeness]: 4
Slides [Depth]: 3
Slides [Usefulness for exams]: 3
Teaching method: Regular tutorial graded quizzes are conducted. It helps students to keep with the course but you can't be in the light to prepare just before exams.. The professor provided us the slides before hand so that we may read the concepts before the class for better understanding..Concepts are taught in the class and students are required to refer to example questions from reference book.. There are weekly tutorial sessions, where tutorial questions are discussed, doubts are cleared and quizzes are conducted..
Grading Scheme: Midsem:25% Endsem:50% Tutorial/Quizzes/Assignment:20% Class Participation/Attendance: 5%
Feedback on Evaluation: Tutorial happens weekly with quizzes atleast bi-weekly.. The assignment include you need to submit one of the tutorial's question solution before the solutions to that tutorial are released.. The assignment is not that tough, provided you had to submit only one question's solution(correctness carries marks) among all tutorial sheets. Midsem level is okayish but endsem was tougher than all quizzes and midsem.
Nature of the Course: Time Needed: Heavy
Nature of the Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding: Heavy
Course Grading: Moderate
Course Grading Review: Grading is not too good, not too bad..
Reference Texts: Numerical Methods for Engineers by S.C. Chapra A very helpful book, this book provides detailed explanation of concepts taught in class.. It contains good example questions on all topics which helps to grasp idea on how questions may be asked in exams

Instructor

Prof. Supreet Saini

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Autumn

Pre-Requisite

No hard pre-requisites, but some understanding of basic linear algebra from MA 106 will help

Learning Outcome

As the name suggests, this course covers numerical methods of solving different kinds of equations – linear algebraic equations, non-linear algebraic equations, linear ODEs, non-linear ODEs, and some exposure to PDEs. This course is meant to run in tandem with the CL 249 Computational Methods Lab, where you’re supposed to learn the methods in this course and apply them in the lab (However, the profs of these courses don’t coordinate with each other so it’s possible they’re out of sync)

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Pre-misdeem, we mainly studied solving linear and nonlinear equation systems along with eigenvalue theorems. The content here is not a lot, but it is heavy on the concepts side.

Post-Midsem Course Content

Post-midsem a lot of new topics are introduced and a lot of it is applications of the linear algebra you know. We studied different kinds of optimizations, numerical methods for integration, and solutions of ODEs and PDEs. There is a lot of new content here and it is important to keep up.

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 3

Completeness: 3

Depth: 2

Usefulness for exams: 3

Teaching method

Prof was pretty irregular with classes, sometimes just uploaded last year recordings. Attendance wasn’t mandatory but towards the end the prof took bonus quizzes.

Grading Scheme

3 quizzes (15% each)
Midsem (25%)
Endsem (35%)

Feedback on Evaluation

All the exams were heavily based on the notes, or something that was discussed in class but was not present in the notes. A couple of times during class the prof had raised some questions for us to think over, that he didn’t discuss, and they ended up coming in the quizzes. If someone had covered the class content thoroughly and understood the concepts, these should have been easy.

However, the evaluation for all our exams was very shady. There was no mark scheme, or step wise or even sub-question wise grading that happened. We simply got the total marks for each question and the test, and it was up to us to figure out what had gone wrong with the lack of solutions of discussion. We did not get feedback for our cribs either, the prof just updated the marks according to him.

Moderate

Course Grading

Moderate

Course Grading Review

10AAs, 13ABs, 24BBs, 39BCs

Scientific Computing by Michael T. Heath

Prof’s notes and lectures were essentially a condensation of this book.

Instructor:

Prof. P. Sunthar

Course Type:

Core Course

Semester:

Spring (Even)

Pre-requisite:

Soft Pre Req : CL 203 Introduction to Transport Phenomena

Learning Outcome:

The course deals with different heat transfer phenomena ( Conduction, Convection, Radiation) in detail with questions designed to represent the practical application of various heat transfer concepts. As a course project, we were asked to apply the concepts to solve a real-life heat transfer problem.

Pre-mid sem Course Content:

Pre – Midsem content covered the basics of modes of heat transfer, Conduction: 1D, 2D steady state and Transient Conduction, Temperature Profiles, A brief introduction of CFD using OpenFoam, Basics of Radiation

Post-mid sem Course Content:

Post Mid-sem content covered: Radiation View Factors, Convective Heat Transfer coefficients, types of Convection, Dimensionless Numbers, Boundary Layers, Heat Exchangers

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 4

Completeness: 3

Depth: 3

Usefulness for exams: 4

Teaching method

There were no live lectures. Instead, the weekly content for the course was uploaded on Moodle as ~1 hour worth of video content comprising pop-up questions. Along with this there was a self study test based on reading material for the week. A live tutorial session in hybrid mode was conducted every week in which the professor solved sample problems followed by a compulsory tutorial set and a weekly assignment or Quiz (offline – post mid-sem). Students were divided into groups of 8 to pursue a semester-long Capstone project. End Semester was covered as weekly quiz. Overall the course was well-designed and structured..

Grading Scheme

H5P Video 5%
Chapter study 10%
Tutorial Problems 15%
Assignment 10%
Weekly Quiz 30%
Project work 20%
End term Quiz 10%

Feedback on Evaluation

Due to distributed evaluation, it was easier to score marks if proper effort and time commitment were given to the course.

Heavy.

Moderate

Course Grading

Lenient

The Grading stats were good with 24 AAs and 57 ABs so it was fairly good grading..

Instructor

Prof Ateeque Malani

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Autumn

Mode of Course

Online

Pre-Requisite

General programming skills (CS101) would be good enough. Knowledge of MATLAB can be handy.

Learning Outcome

This course is complimentary to CL244 and is basically the implementation of the numerical methods covered in CL244. These methods include for

– Linear equations (Gauss Elimination, Jacobi method etc)
– Non-linear equations (Newton-Raphson method, Fixed point method etc)
– Interpolation (Cubic Splines, Polynomial fit)
– Integration (Euler Integration, Trapezoidal method)
– ODEs. (Runge-Kutta method, Finite difference method)

The professor also covered partial differential equations, but this was excluded from the syllabus for endsems. The methods taught in this course are extremely useful in future courses (particularly in lab courses such as CL 232).

Pre-Midsem Course Content

The first part of the course was about solving systems of linear equations (with and without pivoting), non-linear equations and interpolation. The implementation of cubic spline and pivoting while solving system of linear equations are perhaps the most challenging topics in this part of the course

Post-Midsem Course Content

The second part of the course was calculating numerical integrations, solving ODEs and boundary value problems. This part is not complicated since all these methods have similar structures.

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 1

Completeness: 1

Depth: 1

Usefulness for exams: 1

Teaching method

Attendance was compulsory for the course. All students were instructed to keep the video on at all times. Every class was divided into two parts. The first part involved discussion of the theory part for the week. The professor was exhaustive in covering content and even asked some thought-provoking questions to stimulate the class. The second part was implementing the methods covered during the day where the students could take help from the professors and TAs to debug their code.

Grading Scheme

EndSem – 35
Quizzes – 25
Assignment – 25
Attendance – 5
Class participation – 10
Attendance was compulsory and assignments were graded based on the submission and viva

Feedback on Evaluation

There were two quizzes, the professor had announced in the beginning that these would be unannounced. There was no midsem. Though the evaluation was strict and difficult. However, there were some interesting applications of the course content in the endsems.

Heavy

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Moderate

Course Grading

Harsh

Course Grading Review

The grading was not good with just 6 AAs, 8 ABs and 32 FRs

Reference Texts

1. Numerical Methods for Engineers, S C Chapra and R P Canale
2. Linear Algebra and its Applications, Gilbert Strang
3. Computational Science and Engineering, Gilbert Strang
4. Numerical Methods for Engineers, S K Gupta

Additional Remarks

This course requires regular efforts and good concept clarity in order to excel. Programming skills are also important since all assignments and endsems were based on the MATLAB implementation of the numerical methods.

Prof. Jyoti Seth

This course runs parallel to the Numerical Analysis course (CL 244) and involves implementing the algorithms learnt theoretically in that course using programming in MATLAB. It is important because real life chemical industry systems involve solving a lot of linear and non linear equations, differential equations etc. which are often not feasible to solve by hand. This course will involve problems actually related to chemical industries and solving some set of equations by programming in MATLAB.

None

Instructor

Prof. Jhumpa Adhikari

Course type

Core Course

Semester:

Spring

Pre-Requisite

CL 255: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics I

Learning Outcome

Topics like VLE, LLE, VLLE and supporting concepts taught in this course have real-life applications in industry in various separation processes of chemicals.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

1. Revision of Basic Concepts, Laws of Thermodynamics
2. Concept of Equilibrium, Phase Equilibria, Gibbs Phase Rule
3. Fugacity of Pure Species and Mixtures
4. Excess Gibbs Free Energy and Models
5. Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium
6. Liquid-Liquid Equilibrium
7. Vapor-Liquid-Liquid Equilibrium
8. Distribution Coefficient
9. Osmosis
10. Solid-Fluid Equilibria
11. Adsorption

Understanding the fugacity relations and derivations clearly is key to understanding Gibbs Free Energy Models and all the different types of Equilibria that follow. It is also important to understand how activity coefficients are derived from the Gibbs free energy models. Prof. Jhumpa is known to give derivations directly from the slides in all of the quizzes/midsem/endsem. So it is highly recommended that you practice ALL derivations in the slides.

Post-Midsem Course Content

12. Chemical Reaction Equilibrium
13. Thermodynamic Analysis of Processes
14. Introduction To Statistical Thermodynamics
15. Introduction to Electrolyte Solutions

Chemical Reaction Equilibrium has a few lengthy relations, however are easily derivable if you have understanding of the fundamentals. The topic Thermodynamic Analysis of Processes can be a little tricky sometimes as it involves applying different kind of balances: Mass Balance, Energy Balance, Entropy Balance to solve problems. Separate practice of questions in this topic shall help you a lot in the exams. In Statistical Thermodynamics you’ll find a lot of relations and derivations. However, it is an easy topic if you do written practice of it. Again, ALL derivations are important from an exam point of view.

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 5

Completeness: 5

Depth: 5

Usefulness for exams: 4

Teaching method

Prof. Jhumpa was excellent in her teaching. There were 3 one-hour lectures in a week. She taught under the assumption that all students were well-versed with CL 255, however, when doubts arose she solved them by going right to the basics. She entertained all kinds of doubts and in fact, encouraged us to ask her doubts. You need not hesitate to approach her for the same. She discussed almost all of the problem set questions in class just after the related topic had been completed by her. This was really helpful in developing a knack of problem-solving.

Grading Scheme

End Sem : 40%
Mid Sem : 30 %
Quizzes : 30 %

Feedback on Evaluation

Quizzes, Midsem and Endsem exam questions were largely based on derivations in the lecture slides or problems from the problem set, most of which were discussed during the lectures themselves. Prof Jhumpa sets all exams too lengthy to complete fully and accurately. However if you have practiced the derivations from the slides perfectly, the exams can be scoring as a lot of questions are directly from the slides or class discussion.

Prof. Sandeep S Roy

This course is a continuation of the course CL255, covered in the third semester. It provides an insight into practical applications of thermodynamics in chemical engineering. Compared to the previous course on thermodynamics, this course requires more conceptual depth and thorough practice of numericals.

CL255 – Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics I


Click here to continue reading

Instructor

Prof. Sandip Roy

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Autumn

Section:

S2

Pre-Requisite

The hard pre-requisite of the course is CL255 Thermo-1 which you all had been studied in your 3rd semester. So in this you will be studying about the topic you studied in extension of the topics you were taught in CL255 i.e. fugacity, Gibbs energy, Partial Properties etc .Apart form these there is no soft pre-requisite for the course.

Learning Outcome

Leaning these concepts will let you discover the thermodynamical aspects for every system. you will learn how the reaction in the chemical reactors are thermodynamically stable and spontaneous. The factors and aspect that governs all the process and improving a process’s efficiency.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

In the first half of the semester, concepts like:
Fugacity
Vapor Liquid Equillibrium
Liquid Liquid Equilibrium
Solid Liquid Equilibrium
Vapor Liquid Liquid Equillibrium
Solid Vapor Equilibrium
Saturated vapor pressure
Henry’s Law
Activity coefficient

According to me, VLLE was the toughest among all of them, because it is not easy to recognize whether the system is VLLE or VLE or LLE

Post-Midsem Course Content

Post Midsem, sir touched upon the use of thermodynamics in the chemical reactions:
Reaction Equilibria
Gas phase Equilibria
Multiphase reaction
Heat of solution
Osmotic pressure of electrolyte solution
thermodynamics of surfaces
Molecular and Statistical Thermodynamics
Bio thermodynamics

According to me the toughest parts from the above were Multiphase reaction and heat of solution.

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 2

Completeness: 3

Depth: 1

Usefulness for exams: 4

Teaching method

Prof taught the course very well. He covered each and every aspect of the course and also explained each concept with the relevant examples. Overall you have to continuously followed his lectures and solve some tutorial problems.

Grading Scheme

Midsem = 26
Endsem = 50
Quizzes = 24

Feedback on Evaluation

The quizzes were conducted randomly in any of the lectures. So only students who actually attended the lectures can only access the quiz. The quizzes were very simple, if you follow his lecture everyday, you wouldn’t need any more help for doing the quizzes. There were a total of 11 quizzes conducted.
Midsem and endsem requires more efforts as compared to the quizzes, but still the they easy.

Moderate

Moderate

Moderate

Before the release of the grades, the class performance was expected to be low since the average of the class in the endsem was less then 40% of the total marks in the endsem. But apparently the grades seemed to be good when they were released. Professor was very fair in grading students.

Chemical and engineering thermodynamics – Sandler
introduction to chemical engineering thermodynamics – Smith

Teaching method: Sandip Roy Sir was a very involved instructor, and his way of teaching was really nice. His voice is really calm and his class was always a treat to attend. If you manage to grab the first seat in the class then that would be the cherry on the cake. He would also be warm enough to entertain students during office hours regarding the doubts we had in tutorials before conducting the class tests, and in problem sets before the midsems and endsems respectively. Apart from that he was very kind and understanding, hence the environment was never troubling. He also made sure to try to accommodate everyone's requests.

It is highly advised to attend all classes and not be late for the lectures, as he is notorious for conducting surprise tests and yes, these tests will contribute to the final grade as well. Referring to the notes and solving tutorials before class tests, solving problem sets before midsems and endsems will prepare you decently well enough for the exams. Midsem and endsem exams have very interesting good questions and decent involvement and practice of problems will give you an edge in the exams.

title: Process Fluid Mechanics
description: Basic Fluid Concepts: Dimensions and Units, Velocity and Stress Fields, Viscosity and surface tension, Nonnewtonian viscosity, Dimensional Analysis (Buckingham PI theorem), Types of flows, Methods of Analysis, Fluid Statics; Differential Analysis: Review of NSE, Potential flows, Velocity potential, Boundary Layer Theory (Laminar), Viscous flows (past sphere), Integral Analysis: Mass, Energy (Bernouli), Momentum (Impinging jet, pitot tube, Orifice meter, rotameter, pipe flow: f vs Re charts); Chem Engg Equipment: Piping systems (K factors, networks), Flow past immersed objects (Packed beds, Fluidised beds, sedimentation, Centrifugal separation, filtration), Pumps, Agitation and Mixing, (Power consumption, mixing times, scale up), Particulate solids, characterisation, Other topics: Introduction to Turbulent Flows (Reynolds equations), Compressible flows, Compressors.

Instructor

Prof. Rajdip Bandyopadhyaya

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Autumn (Odd)

Mode of Course

Online

Pre-Requisite

Hard pre-requisite for CL250 Thermo-II

Learning Outcome

This course involves a lot of in depth derivations, concepts elaborating on the Laws of Thermodynamics learnt during JEE Preparation. It additionally introduces the students to certain new and important topics including fugacity and partial molar properties. CL 250: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics II requires knowledge regarding all concepts taught in this course.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Understanding the concepts taught pre midsem was relatively easy. Focusing on solving thermodynamic questions (like the ones in Problem Solving sheets) and on all derivations taught in lectures is sufficient.
1)Prominent names in the development of Thermodynamics
2)Example-applications of Thermodynamics in Chemical Engineering
3)Basic definitions, conservation relationships (mass/ energy) and Cv, Cp
4)Entropy balances and both statements of the second law
5)Heat engine and Carnot engine
6)Equilibrium criteria for open and closed systems
7)Thermodynamic identities, evaluation of changes in U, H, S and departure functions

Post-Midsem Course Content

Fugacity’ taught in the post midsem syllabus was introduced to us for the first time and was tough to grasp initially. A few topics were rushed towards the end, but the topics are just as important and applicable in themodynamics II. Again, solving various questions on thermodynamics (like the ones in PS), being clear with derivations and applications of all concepts is sufficient.
1)Molar Gibb’s Energy and fugacity, fugacity coefficient (new topics)
2)Gibb’s phase rule
3)Antoine’s equation
4)Property changes on mixing and Gibb’s Duhem Rule
5)Equations of change and thermodynamic equilibrium state of a multicomponent multiphase system
6)VLE, Isothermal flash calculation, azeotropic mixtures

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 4

Completeness:

Depth: 3

Usefulness for exams: 3

Teaching method

Prof was regularly involved, taught the topics on slides and revised concepts as well. In the online mode, he shared the slides on the meet and taught things on the whiteboard. He rushed through a few topics towards the end due to which Thermodynamics II felt like a challenge to most students.

Grading Scheme

2 quizzes- 10% per quiz, 1 midsem- 30%, 1 endsem- 40%, 1 assignment- 10%

Feedback on Evaluation

Frequency of assignments/ tests was comfortable, tests were more on JEE level topics than on topics related to the slides. Midsems, endsems and assignments were on topics taught in the course and were slightly difficult (primarily because the quizzes’ difficulty was misleading and people prepared accordingly)

Nature of the Course: Time Needed

Moderate

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Moderate

Course Grading

Harsh

Course Grading Review

Final grades were slightly worse as compared to the marks the students got. I remember S1 students complaining about the stats when they saw the S2 stats. [S1: 0 APs, 3 AAs, 5 ABs, 9 BBs rest below BB] [S2: 2 APs, 3 AAs, 10 ABs, 16 BBs, rest below BB]

Reference Texts

Sandler. While slides were enough to understand and score, this book can be referred in case of doubts in understanding topics or to solve additional problems. Studying from slides as much as possible and referring to this book occasionally can help.

title: Process Control
description: First Principles model development; Process dynamics for first, second and higher order systems: linearisation, transfer function models, effect of poles, zeros and time delays on system response; Empirical models from data; control system instrumentation; introduction to feedback control: objectives, PID control; analysis of closed loop systems: stability, root locus, frequency response using Bode and Nyquist plots; control design techniques: design criteria, time and frequency domain techniques, model based design, tuning; advanced control strategies: cascade and feed forward, introduction to multivariable control; controller implementation through discretisation.

reviews.body:
Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Prof. Bharatkumar Suthar

This course is about how processes such as chemical reactors are controlled in actual chemical plants. So, it starts from the basics of process control (laplace transform, transfer functions, etc.) and then goes deeper into PID control modeling and tuning. By the end of this course, you would be able to design a controller for any basic process, analyze its stability and simulate it in Simulink.

None


Click here to continue reading

Instructor

Prof. Ravindra Gudi

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Spring (Even)

Pre-Requisite

Soft pre-requisite: EE 101

Learning Outcome

The course is highly utilitarian from an industrial operations perspective in the sense that it teaches one about design of system parameters to ensure its stability. It should be a breeze for those pursuing SysCon minor and can also be supplemented by an Advanced Process Control course in the Chemical department (CL 686)

Pre-Midsem Course Content

SYSTEMS: Introduction to control variables, manipulable (and non-manipulable) variables, dynamics of systems in chemical engineering, linearisation of dynamics, transfer functions in the Laplace domain, stability (poles and zeros), first and higher order systems, graphs of response. Relatively easy to understand with an EE101 foundation

Post-Midsem Course Content

CONTROL: feedback, feedforward, cascade controls, P, PI, PID, stability analysis during Bode plots, Nyquist plots and parameter tuning, multivariable control. The post-midsem part is more difficult as it is novel to most students.

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 4

Completeness: 3

Depth: 2

Usefulness for exams: 3

Teaching method

The professor was a very experienced and learned teacher. He was regular with the classes and finished the expected syllabus on time, although he was a little slow in the start.

Grading Scheme

Best 2 (out of 3) quizzes: 10% each
Midsem: 30%
Endsem: 50%
Prof. insisted on attendance and stated that attendance counts in the grade but weight was unbeknownst to us

Feedback on Evaluation

The frequency of quizzes was high post-midsem. The exams are quite difficult and the lecture material often does not prepare one for them. However the quizzes were easy and based directly off class interactions or from the content: straightforward and concept-based.

Really important course from industry perspective. Controls have wide applications across all disciplines. If you want to something more of maths side in chemical then this course is really helpful. Useful in design lab course in 4th year.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Intro to process control, Feedback control and it’s
dynamic response, Laplace transform, Energy balance,P, PI, PD, and PID controls, interacting systems, MIMO

Intrstrumentation, close loop stability analysis, controller design, frequency response analysis, feed forward control, multi loop multi variable control, optimisation

Teaching method

Prof had a plan for entire course and stick to it till the end. He was neutral towards class participation but attendance was compulsory. Attendance was not compulsory in tuts. Teaching was really good he explained everything clearly and very eager to repeat concepts if you find it hard to understand at first time. Used really descriptive slides. All quizzes, assignments, exams were highly relevant to whatever taught and main textbook, Exams and quizzes were open notes and open book(we were allowed to use soft copy in exams but this year he may ask for hard copy so confirm it in start). Assignments were simulations and programming heavy also time consuming. Quizzes and exams were moderately difficult on average but it depends on how much practice you have done. Course Grading Review

25% students got AA and AB. Also around 20% got FR and II. So grading was unevenly distributed. Prof highly rewarded the ones who scored well and also highly penalised mistakes. He is serious about his course and don’t tolerate any kind of dishonesty and malpractices.

III: (Process Dynamics and Control)

❖ Luyben, W.L. Process Modeling, Simulation and Control for Chemical
Engineers.
❖ Stephanopoulos, G., Chemical Process Control: An Introduction to Theory
and Practice.

Additional Remarks

It’s an intresting course for the ones who want to do some maths in chemical engineering. Practice problems it’s really helpful in exam. Tutorials are important must solve atleast once before exam. Main text is really good and enough for exam perspective. No need to follow additional resources until you want extra knowledge.

title: Solid Mechanics
description: Rigid and deformable solids; Method of sections for evaluating internal forces in bodies - review of free body diagrams; Concept of stress - normal and shear stresses; State of stress; Concept of strain - normal and shear strains; State of strain; Hookes law; Constitutive relations; Axially loaded members, force and deflections; Indeterminate systems and compatibility conditions; Simple indeterminate systems and lack of fit problems; Generalized Hookes law; Stress in cylindrical and spherical shells; Thin-Walled Pressure Vessels; Torsion of circular shafts - determinate and simple indeterminate systems. Elastic theory of bending of beams; Shear force and bending moment diagrams; Bending and shearing stresses inbeams of symmetrical cross-section; Concept of shear flow and shear centre; Principle of superposition and its limitations. Transformation of plane stress and strain; Principal stresses and strains; Mohrs circle. Bending deflection of beams by direct integration method; Application of direct integration method to simple indeterminate systems. Elastic buckling of compression members.

Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Professor when you took the course:

Prof. Arindam Sarkar

Motivation: This course acquaints you with the importance of mechanics of materials in chemical engineering. The basics of this course are formed by the mechanics studied during JEE, but this is only at the very start. Later, after about 2 weeks, this course becomes complex with the application in advanced three-dimensional problems and introduction to various progressive topics such as strengths of structural materials.

Feedback on lectures: The lectures in this course were not that interactive(depends on the prof) but the concepts taught were interesting as it was something we have already been accustomed to during JEE prep – Mechanics, although not a complete overlap. So, going to class and visualising the simple FBDs, discussing with friends and prof easily cleared the concepts in the class itself and helped to have sufficient prep for the quizzes which had elementary level problems related to each concept taught after the last quiz.

Feedback on tutorials/assignments and exams: Regular Tutorials for the topics discussed will be uploaded(about 8-9 tutorials) having 15-20 questions each. Most of the questions were from the book itself. If you solve the tutorial questions religiously, then the quizzes will be a cakewalk.

Pro Tip – Make(read as Arrange for some) solutions of the tutorial questions discussed in the class so that you can have a look at them just before the quizzes/exams.
