Professor when you took the course

Prof. Madhu Vinjamur

Motivation behind the course

The course helps us understand the various reactors and conditions used for chemical and biological processes and why, which is useful in process design. How chemical processes are set up is the crux behind this course

Course Content

Pre midsem part: Various reactors and their conditions were taught. Also, there were 6 processes: Methanol, Sulphuric Acid, Formaldehyde, Ethylbenzene, Ethylene oxide and Ammonia


Click here to continue reading

Instructor

Prof. Madhu Vinjamur, Prof. Rahul Nabar

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Spring (Even)

Mode of Course

Hybrid

Pre-Requisite

No official prerequisites.
Concepts from Thermodynamics, Mass Transfer 1 are useful.

Learning Outcome

This course gives an idea about how chemical and biochemical processes for production of bulk and fine chemicals are carried out in the industry. The main learning outcome is: Ability to come up with a complete and economical Process Flow Diagram (PFD) for any chemical based on its properties and available starting materials. One gets to learn about a wide variety of reactors, catalysts and downstream separation methods.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Broad topics covered pre-midsem:

1. Production of Bulk Chemicals (Heterogeneous Catalysts)
1.1 Sulphuric Acid
1.2 Nitric Acid
1.3 Methanol
1.4 Formaldehyde
1.5 Ethylene Oxide

2. Production of Bulk Chemicals (Homogeneous Catalysts)
2.1 Homogenous vs. Heterogeneous catalysts
2.2 Acetic Acid
2.3 Butaryldehyde (Hydroformylation)
2.4 Terepthalic Acid

All these topics are taught by Prof. Madhu Vinjamur

Post-Midsem Course Content

Broad topics covered pre-midsem:

1. Production of Fine Chemicals
1.1 Introduction to Fine Chemicals
1.2 Menthol
1.3 2-phenyl ethyl alcohol
1.4 Citral

2. Petroleum Refining
This section contains a large number of processes (to be listed here) from extraction from earth to coking and more. Toughest part of the course in terms of volume of memorization.

3. Biotechnology
2-3 lectures on fermentation based chemical processes for production of Lactic acid, Ethanol, etc. Simple to memorize and understand.

Topics under 1. Production of Fine Chemicals are taught by Prof. Rahul Nabar.
There were some guest lectures on Soap production taken by professionals from HUL (not in syllabus).

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 4

Completeness: 4

Depth: 3

Usefulness for exams: 5

Teaching method

Both, Prof. Madhu Vinjamur and Prof. Rahul Nabar, taught the concepts very well. The lectures by Prof. Madhu were pre-recorded and uploaded weekly (typically for each chemical) with live sessions for doubts and discussion only which very few of us attended. Attending live lectures did not add a lot to the content already present in pre-recorded lectures. The slides and notes are full of information and very relevant as far as exams are concerned. The instructor also uploaded ‘Homework Questions’ for each topic which were very useful.
Whereas, reading slides for the fine chemical part (Prof. Nabar) is not enough. All the lectures were live sessions with a lot of interaction with students. Questions in the exam were asked from the content taught as well as discussed in live lectures and hence, attending these is recommended.
Overall, the teaching part could not have been better.

Grading Scheme

Quizzes: 20%
Midsem: 30%
Endsem: 50%

Since the semester was online, there were no attendance related requirements.

Feedback on Evaluation

There were no graded assignments in this course. A good number of questions for each topic were uploaded on the moodle as a self-study exercise.

Quizzes were extremely short and easy to score. Midsem and Endsem had few questions that required understanding of the processes and simple memorization won’t help. I would consider the Endsem to be tough due to the vast amount of syllabus that was asked.

Nature of the Course: Time Needed

Moderate

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Easy

Course Grading

Lenient

Course Grading Review

None

Reference Texts

Chemical Process Technology by Annelies van Diepen, Jacob A. Moulijn, and Michiel Makkee

Additional Remarks

This course requires regular efforts and good concept clarity in order to excel. Programming skills are also important since all assignments and endsems were based on the MATLAB implementation of the numerical methods.

We thank K Adithya Krishna for sharing the course review.

We thank the Chemical DAMP team for this review

The course will have significant component of visits to most of the sectors.

This course is offered as a department elective aimed at imparting practical knowledge on topics learned in the curriculum of Chemical Engineering which is otherwise not possible in the classroom. title: Mass Transfer II
description: Perspective on Unit Operations; Liquid-Liquid extraction; Leaching; Adsorption and Ion-exchange; Simultaneous Heat and Mass Transfer: Humidification and Dehumidification, Design of cooling towers, Drying, Crystallization; Membrane processes: Ultra-filtration and reverse osmosis.

reviews.body:
Instructor

Prof. Venkatesh K.V.

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Spring (Even)

Mode of Course

Online

Pre-Requisite

Hard Pre-requisite: CL 319 & CL 255(Basics)

Learning Outcome

The course gives you a good insight into various chemical operations commonly used in industries. Apart from the real-life application part of the course, it also equips you with a decent understanding of factors/criteria to consider while selecting a chemical operation.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

The course did not have a mid-semester exam. It had six quizzes, each based on a certain operation and an end-sem. The professor had conducted quizzes for two out of six operations by mid-semester. The following were the processes covered: –

1. Drying
2. Cooling

Amongst the two, I believe drying was a bit tough as it had a lot of formulae, and with the exams being closed book, it becomes a little challenging to remember everything by the time end-sems arrive.

Post-Midsem Course Content

The topics covered post-midsem had more course content than those covered before mid-sem. Hence, the course load increased a little post-midsem. The following were the topics covered: –

1. Adsorption
2. Solvent Extraction
3. Leaching
4. Membrane Separation & Reverse Osmosis

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 3

Completeness: 2

Depth: 3

Usefulness for exams: 4

Teaching method

Professor’s Involvement: The lectures were primarily one-sided and not much on the interactive end. The professor teaches well and is quite welcoming when it comes to doubts. However, the professor was a little irregular with the classes; there were times when he had left the lectures in between and hadn’t utilized his 1.5-hour slot thoroughly.

Content: Some of the operations were covered in reasonable detail, and the content taught was good enough for the students to be able to solve questions by themselves. However, some of them were rushed through and were taught on a surface level, which compromised both the students’ problem-solving ability and the course’s learning outcomes.

Grading Scheme

Six quizzes and an end-semester exam: –

Weightage of each quiz: 10%
Weightage of the end-semester: 40%

Attendance wasn’t mandatory.

Feedback on Evaluation

There were 6 operations covered in total. The instructor did not have tutorials as such but he provided us with some homework problems(non-graded) for each operation once he was done teaching it.

The instructor conducted six quizzes and one end-semester exam. The questions asked(both for quizzes & end-sems) were of moderate difficulty at max, most of which were directly taken up from past year papers.

Nature of the Course: Time Needed

Light

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Moderate

Course Grading

Harsh

Course Grading Review

The class performed pretty well in the course, and most students had a decent total. However, the grading was not very rewarding. Only 3 out of 77 students were given an AA(which is significantly less compared to standard grading stats)

Most of the class(~70%) ended up with a 6, 7, or 8, with each having nearly the same number of students. Only 40% of the students got an 8 or above, which seemed a little unfair based on the class’s performance.

Reference Texts

N.A.

Additional Remarks

NA

title: Mass Transfer I
description: Principles of Mass transfer: Constitutive laws of diffusion; unsteady state diffusion; Convective mass transfer. Interphase mass transfer and mass transfer coefficients; Mass transfer theories/models; Effect of chemical reaction on mass transfer. Equilibrium stages and transfer units: number and height of transfer units; stage efficiency. Gas absorption: plate and packed column design; reactive absorption. Distillation: batch distillation, continuous fractionation, other types of distillation (eg azeotropic); introduction to multi-component mixtures.

Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Professor: Prof. Ganesh Vishwanathan

Motivation: This course develops the Pillars of Chemical Engineering. This said, highlighting the importance of the fundamentals developed in this course is redundant. Unarguably one of the most important courses it also develops a critical yet creative way to approach problems.

Prerequisites: None

Feedback on lectures and tutorials: Lectures are intellectually very stimulating. The prof takes a lot of effort to keep the entire class in the loop and ensures complete understanding. Hence, missing lectures isn’t suggested (unless absolutely necessary)  because following up would be difficult and also because they are extremely helpful. Personally, one of the best course and the best course instructor I’ve encountered.

There are computational tutorials which aren’t graded but attendance is enforced. This is the only place where he will be solving questions and illustrating the concepts taught. Hence, highly recommended if you want to understand the way the prof approaches problems which in turn provides insights into the questions one can expect in the exams.

The structure is well developed and the instructor informs the students of the text to be referred to (alternates between Levenspiel and Foggler)  each time he starts a new topic/concept. He has 5 major topics that he has divided the course in:


Click here to continue reading

Instructor

Prof. Swati Bhattacharya

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Autumn (Odd)

Pre-Requisite

There are no pre-requisites for this course, however, one might need some coding skills for certain questions/problems.

Learning Outcome

The concepts of Reactor Design maybe useful in some courses in the later semester, such as Design Project or if one is taking the SLP-IDP course.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

The pre-midsem part involves covering the basics i.e. kinetics, reaction rates, rate constant estimation, material balances, followed by the use of these in designing reactors (eg. batch, CSTR, etc.) under various constraints (such as variable volume, variable flowrates, etc.)

Post-Midsem Course Content

The post-midsem content is slightly more rigorous and covers non-elementary rate laws, multiple reactions, catalysis and catalyst deactivation, shrinking core model, residence time distribution, internal mass transfer limitations and a case study on Cumene. The content covered is mostly theoretical so application in problems needs practice.

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 4

Completeness: 4

Depth: 3

Usefulness for exams: 4

Teaching method

The professor had divided the class into three groups, and would conduct classes separately for the three groups initially, she was fairly involved in the course and conducted classes regularly. However, the course does require a lot of self-study and practice, especially if you wan to score well in the exams.

Grading Scheme

The grading scheme that was decided initially was Quizzes – 5%, Tutorials – 25%, Midsem – 35%, Endsem – 35%. However later it was revised to a weightage of 15% each to Quizzes and Tutorial Assignments.

Feedback on Evaluation

The tutorial assignments are usually assigned in groups and they can be solved easily. The quizzes are of varying difficulty, depending on the topic being covered and a total of 6 quizzes were conducted throughout the course. The lecture slides do contain practice problems, so those combined with the tutorials and quizzes, are good, if one is looking to practice questions relevant to midsems and endsems. However, they are relatively easier than midsems. The midsem also involved solving a question that required coding. The endsem exam had a high level of difficulty, with the paper being slightly lengthy.

Nature of the Course: Time Needed

Heavy

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Difficult

Course Grading

Moderate

Course Grading Review

The overall grading followed the typical bell curve, with most people getting grades between AB and CC (AB – 13, BB – 13, BC – 18, CC – 22). If one has performed well in the midsems and endsems, then getting a good grade should not be difficult.

Reference Texts

Elements of Chemical Reaction Engineering by H. Scott Fogler, Chemical Reaction Engineering by O.Levenspiel

title: Advanced Transport Phenomena
description: This course will teach students how to think about problems in transport phenomena. Given a physical problem, students will learn how to formulate a consistent mathematical model based on first principles, and how to simplify and analyze the equations to obtain useful physical insights and make quantitative predictions. The scope of the course is limited to single-phase, laminar flows with transport of a scalar. However, we will consider nonlinear problems, including situation where fluid flow is coupled to heat/mass transport. Key Topics: Introduction and motivation - Continuum approach - Elementary kinematics - Reynolds Transport Theorem - Governing conservation equations - Cauchy stress tensor - Navier-Stokes equations - Brief history of the stress tensor and continuum mechanics - Non-dimensionalization and O(1) scaling - Perturbation methods - Domain perturbation - Singular perturbation - Illustrative problems - Thin gap flows - Taylor-Aris dispersion - Lyapunov Schmidt reduction – Instabilities. Illustrative problems: Electroosmotic flow in microchannels, Reaction in a porous catalyst, Pulsatile pipe flow, Curved channel Dean flow, Wavy-wall flow, Settling sphere, Active flow in the cell cortex, etc.

reviews.body:
Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Prof. Jason Picardo

Advanced Transport Phenomena is a requirement for honors. I chose to do honors since I find core chemical engineering interesting and it was a good way to explore that interest further.

Transport phenomena [CL 203]

Basic principles and governing equations, non-dimensionalization and Scaling, perturbation methods, longwave approximation, averaging

35% Project
30% Assignments
10% Moodle quizzes
25% Written quizzes

The professor uploaded video lectures before class and we had to watch them as well as attempt a short quiz on moodle based on that lecture. The class hours were for discussing doubts from the lectures. While the course content itself is a little challenging and math-heavy, the professor did an excellent job in explaining the concepts through the lectures.
Assignments were long and challenging but we got sufficient time to complete them. Quizzes were largely based on content covered in lectures and were not that difficult to solve if one had followed the lectures.
There was a project assigned as well which required additional reading and solving of a transport problem.


Click here to continue reading

Instructor

Prof. Jason Picardo

Course Type

Honors Course

Semester

Autumn (Odd)

Pre-Requisite

Hard pre-requisite: CL 203 Introduction to Transport Phenomena
Soft pre-requisite: some programming language for simulating velocity, pressure fields, etc (basically for solving ODEs and graphing the solutions) like Mathematica, Python, MATLAB

Learning Outcome

The course is the successor to the core transport phenomena course and provides an excellent insight into the integration of the three kinds of transfer: heat/momentum/mass. One gets a good qualitative idea of competing phenomena in a system and how they qualitatively affect our fields (v,P) of interest.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Overview of CL 203: Reynolds Transport Theorem, mass/momentum/energy conservation governing equations, O(1) scaling, perturbation methods

Post-Midsem Course Content

Long wave approximation, lubrication theory, Taylor-Aris dispersion, averaging

Slides
Ease of Understanding: 5
Completeness: 5
Depth: 5

Teaching method

The professor is a fantastic teacher and is great at providing clarity to concepts you may have done already in CL 203 but want a revision of. He teaches the course from a very foundational and detailed but practical perspective and that makes the experience of learning quite enjoyable.

Grading Scheme

15 Short quizzes based on lecture videos: 10%
3 Written, subjective quizzes: 25% (open-book)
3 Assignments: 30%
Final project (in pairs): 35%

Feedback on Evaluation

The quizzes were interesting and medium in difficulty level. The project must be started early and is easy if thoroughly thought through. The short quizzes based on YouTube video lectures were to be attempted on Moodle almost before every class and the longer quizzes were held at around every 2-3 weeks. Project submissions were post-endsem with a final report submission and live presentation to the professor. The assignments were given at every 2-3 week intervals with around 10 days of time to do each of them.

Nature of the Course: Time Needed

Heavy

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Easy

Course Grading

Moderate

Course Grading Review

It is difficult to get an AA/AB, but no student was awarded an FR grade.
AA 2
AB 2
BB 1
BC 2
CC 1
DD 1

Reference Texts

L. G. Leal, Advanced Transport Phenomena, Cambridge 2007
Quite useful for the course

Comments

The lectures were uploaded as a playlist in advance in the online mode. The live classes were held once/twice weekly for video discussions and doubts. The long, written quizzes are similar (conceptually) to the assignments, if done religiously. Another advantage is that this course has no big midsem/endsem!

title: Process Equipment Selection
description: Design variations, Selection criteria, process calculations and representative industrial applications for the following will be covered. (1) Pumps: Types of pumps and selection criteria, Typical calculations of a pumping circuit and pump rating, Pump characteristic curves, Cavitation and NPSH. (2) Compression and Expansion: blowers and compressors, Single or multistage compressing, Typical multistage compressor calculations. (3) Furnaces: Types of furnaces, simple 1-D, 2-D heat transfer models of furnaces. (4) Gas-Solid Catalytic Reactors: Fluidized Bed. (5) Evaporator: Multiple Effect Evaporator Design, Optimum number of effects; Plant Design: PFD, P&ID, Plant Layout, Safety

reviews.body:
Name: Shreeya Athaley
Roll Number: 190020106
Year of Study: to-be 5th Year
Section: S2
Course: CL409: Material Science
Professor(s): Prof. Rajdip Bandyopadhyaya
Course Type: Core Course
Semester: Autumn (Odd)
Mode of Course: Offline
Frequency of the Course: Every Year
Pre-Requisite: No pre-requisites
Learning Outcome: This is an introductory course on material science. It provides us information on the composition, structure, synthesis and processing of various materials that can be used to invent new devices or allow us to improve existing material properties. This course is an inch deep, mile wide introduction to the developments in the field of material sciences.
Pre-Midsem Course Content: Properties of Materials, Nano materials, Bio materials
Post-Midsem Course Content: Structure of Unit Cells, Polymers and their applications, Crystal Structures, Diffusion in Solids, Imperfections in Solids, Phase Diagrams, Corrosion
Slides [Ease of Understanding]: 2
Slides [Completeness]: 2
Slides [Depth]: 2
Slides [Usefulness for exams]: 3
Teaching method: The course is divided into several sections, each covering the properties and applications of different types of materials. The professor utilises slides, which usually have several screenshots/pictures from various sources. He does not stick to any particular textbook. He provides a black and white print out of the lecture slides to circulate within the section after completion of a particular topic (Slides are not uploaded on Moodle). The professor also uses the board to teach some new concepts. It is advisable that you attend classes and take notes as the professor teaches, as the exams cover everything and anything discussed in class. Although the course content is not difficult and may seem logical, it is tough to recall details like scientific terms discussed in class or trends in the diagrams while giving your examinations. The professor appreciates attendance in class, although it is not compulsory. He is willing to repeat concepts till everyone is convinced and on board with what is being taught.
Grading Scheme: Attendance was not compulsory. Weightage: 2 Quizzes - 20-25%, Midsem - 25-30%, Endsem - 40-45%, Project - 25%
Feedback on Evaluation: The course had two quizzes(one before and the other after midsem), a midsem, an endsem and a project. The written exams had all portion taught till the date of the exam. The numericals in the exam were fairly logical and were based on concepts taught in class. Majority of the paper was based on memory. It was important to carefully go through each slide and remember important terms, trends in graphs, names of equipment, etc. It was an added advantage if you had taken notes in class. It was necessary to stick to the answer key to earn marks. Papers were very lengthy. The project was based on surveying literature.
Nature of the Course: Time Needed: Moderate
Nature of the Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding: Light
Course Grading: Very Harsh
Course Grading Review: The grading was harsh. No AAs were awarded, with only 2 ABs. There were only 10 students with BC or above.
Reference Texts: William D Callister, Material Science and Engineering: An Introduction, Wiley

Name: Khyati Jain
Roll Number: 190020059
Year of Study: Graduated
Section: S1
Course: CL409 Intro to Material Science
Professor(s) Prof. Jhumpa Adhikari
Course Type Core Course
Semester Autumn (Odd)
Mode of Course Offline
Frequency of the Course Every Year

Learning Outcome Advanced applications of material sciences in bioscience, forensics, biodegradable medical applications all emanate from the basics of this course. A number of professors from the department work along these lines such as Prof Majumder, Prof Bellare, etc.
Pre-Midsem Course Content The course is relatively simple and easy to understand. The contents cover lattice points and a number of allied concepts of symmetry, defects, uses, and most of it seems familiar to what is taught in the chapter of solids in class 12th, just a slightly advanced version
Post-Midsem Course Content The contents post midsem take a slight thermodynamical direction where the concepts of solid lattice and defects meet their thermodynamic concepts and applications. Plots drive a major proportion of this course. It requires more of common sense and vigilance to sail through.
Slides [Ease of Understanding] 4
Slides [Completeness] 2
Slides [Depth] 2
Slides [Usefulness for exams] 3
Teaching method Mam uses the traditional chalk and duster method of teaching and completely helps understanding from the basics. It becomes necessary to attend classes since she stops using slides after a while. Although, what you understand in class is enough to give a good understanding and sail you through exams. No extra effort is needed provided you attend classes. She brings models and 3D simulations to make understanding more fruitful.
Grading Scheme 40% quizzes, 30% midsem, 30% endsem. No projects or assignments. The prof takes full account of the fact that many of the students are in their placement semester and so the attendance was relaxed.
Feedback on Evaluation 4 quizzes (45 mins in class hours), 9-10 tutorials (each having around 6-8 questions. Each question is properly discussed in the class) no assignments or projects. The exams have one thing in common: everything on the exam has definitely already been taught in the class. Try attending as much as possible. It is not easy to understand from slides or notes alone.
Nature of the Course: Time Needed Light
Nature of the Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding Moderate
Course Grading Moderate
Course Grading Review The grading was fair. With very few FRs, and a lot of populations of 9s and 8s, it was a well-defined grading
Reference Texts Mam teaches majorly from her slides and using the blackboard. The text suggested did not add a lot of value in addition to what was covered as she picks topics herself and then covers them in detail.
Photo nan
Comments Attending classes would definitely help. Make your own notes and you will not have to give extra time to study for this course. Not very difficult but just logical and a little tricky at times

Name: Abhi Manjunath Dasari

Course Type: Core Course
Semester: Autumn (Odd)
Mode of Course: Offline
Frequency of the Course: Every Year
Pre-Requisite: MATLAB was necessary for two process control experiments and was also helpful for other experiments. Soft Pre-requisite: Controller design, Mass transfer (particularly concepts taught in the Mass Transfer II course), and Reaction engineering.
Learning Outcome: This course helps to get a better understanding of process control, mass transfer, and CRE concepts and their practical applications. Also, it gives laboratory work and equipment handling experience.
Pre-Midsem Course Content: A total of eight experiments were conducted, with four taking place before the midsem and the remaining four after the midsem. Out of the four experiments our group had in the first half, three experiments focused on process control (controller design, sensors & actuators), and one experiment was related to mass transfer (liquid-liquid extraction).
Post-Midsem Course Content: In the other four, three experiments were related to mass transfer (Crystallization, Drying, and Distillation), and one was related to reaction engineering.

Slides [Completeness]: 3
Slides [Depth]: 3
Slides [Usefulness for exams]: 4
Teaching method: The following routine was followed each week: 1. Pre-quiz: After going through the lab manual, a pre-quiz was supposed to be attempted before coming to the lab. 2. Lab session: TAs explain the concepts behind the experiment and the experimental procedure, then the experiment is performed. Initially, marks were allotted only for lab participation, but later a small viva session was also taken during the lab. 3. Report: The report has to be completed and submitted in about 2.5 days. Most professors asked for handwritten reports. 4. Viva: Each viva was scheduled for 30 minutes. The results, hypotheses, and conclusions were to be presented during the viva. Questions were asked on the theoretical concepts, experimental setup & procedure, results, and hypotheses.
Grading Scheme: Pre-quiz, lab participation, report, and viva had the most weightage. Endsem also had a significant weightage.

The overall difficulty of the exam was moderate.

title: SLP-IDP
description: The only course in the chemical engineering curriculum that offers professional experience at the undergraduate level along with mentoring from institute mentors is SLP-IDP. Because the corporation was required by the agreement to supply all relevant information under a confidentially agreement signed by us, it gave an experience similar to an internship but in a more organised manner. Additionally, because SLP-IDP is a group project, as opposed to other SLP projects, it will generate an environment that is akin to a working one.

reviews.body:
This course has been reviewed by Divyashree Tambade

Awareness and Motivation

The course is quite popular within the Chemical Department and runs every semester. Some of the seniors and even people from my batch had taken previous offerings of the course, so that’s how I came to know about it. Prof. Sanjay Mahajani, who leads the course also floats a form, along with conducting an information session, before each semester gauging the interest of students in the course for that particular semester.
This course is one of the unique offerings from the chemical department. IDP stands for Industry Defined Problem and the course essentially covers groups of students trying to solve an actual industry problem. There are no lectures involved and the course is entirely project based.
The reason why I wanted to enroll for the course was that it was an opportunity to apply all that I had learned in the classroom on a practical problem. There is immense learning involved in the process as you understand the differences between theory and practice. Also, the ratio of professors to students is exceptional. We have two faculty mentors and two industry mentors for a group of three students. So, the interaction is very positive.

Course Curriculum

There is no defined curriculum. Chemical industries all over India define some problems that IIT Bombay students work on. They could be on any of the concepts that you have learned uptil now in your courses. And even if you haven’t, the professor's guide you enough to figure it out on your own. Finally, the kind of work and concepts you work on depends on the project.
Specifically, for my project involved developing the process of Reactive Chromatography for an esterification reaction. The industry has a batch process currently, so we explored all the intermediate steps of a continuous process and a reactive distillation process.

Rigour and Time Commitment

This too depends on the project you choose / get. Some of my classmates had much lighter weeks. In our case, the reaction was very specific and barely any literature could be found on it. So, we had to do a lot of experiments to figure out the technical details for simulations and other work on the project. We used to spend no more than 9 hours per week per person.

Pros and Cons

The pros as I mentioned are that you get to work on a practical problem, which in itself is an extraordinary experience. I would urge everyone to try this course at least. It would be helpful if other departments did the same, but sadly they don’t have any such offerings. There is also an opportunity to visit the industry you are working with (more so in an offline sem :) ) which is quite rare in undergrad. You also get to design and conduct experiments (a bunch of them in our case) in a lab which again is quite interesting ( and also tiring XD). The best part is that the industries actually use the solutions that the students develop, so it is quite fulfilling to know that your contribution had some value. There is also an award that Aarti Industries gives for a few top projects. A bonus is that you get to choose the kind of problem you want to work on to a fairly large extent, so if there is a particular field in chemical engineering that you wish to explore, this is a very good opportunity. Additionally, if you want to dip your toes into the core and see if it suits you, I would urge you to take this course.
There are no cons as such but I would suggest being prepared for a steep learning curve and long working hours sometimes.

Relevance of the Course to the Career Path

I personally wanted to work on a chemical industry problem to see if I would like core and see how all that I had learned is relevant and applied in the industry. Also, since this is one of the unique courses in the department and I had heard many good things about it, I wanted to try it out.

title: Chemical Process Design
description: Process Design and Development: General Design Considerations; The Hierarchy of Chemical Process Design; The Nature of Process Synthesis and Analysis; Reactor networks in process flowsheets: Attainable region Separation systems in process flowsheets: multicomponent distillation for ideal and non-ideal systems, distillation column sequences, heat integration in distillation columns Heat exchange networks synthesis and utilities: Energy targets Introduction to optimization approaches to optimal design, role of simulations in process design, Design under uncertainty and failure tolerance, Engineering around variations, Introduction to process integration

Prof. Yogendra Shastri, Prof. Madhu Vinjamur, Prof. Sharad Bhartiya

His lectures were completely based on the book Systematic Methods of Chemical Process Design by Westerberg, Grossmann and Beigler. So along with the lectures this book will really come in handy.

title: Design Lab I
description: Steady-state simulation of flow sheets; Optimization and costing in flow sheets; Design and analysis of control systems; Simulations using commercial simulators (e.g. ASPEN, HYSYS), Computational Fluid Dynamics, Molecular modeling.

reviews.body:
Instructor

Prof. Kannan Moudgalya (Pre-Midsem) and Prof. Yogendra Shastri (Post-Midsem)

Course type

Core Course

Semester

Autumn (Odd)

Mode of Course

Online

Pre-Requisite

No hard pre-requisities. However, familiarity with Equilibrium Thermodynamics and CRE is a soft pre-requisite for DWSIM (pre-midsem), and CL 254: Process Fluid Mechanics is an absolutely essential soft pre-requisite for OpenFOAM (post-midsem).

Side note: We had this lab in online mode, so DWSIM and OpenFOAM were to be installed on our laptops. If this is still the case and you happen to use a Mac, you’ll want to get a Windows laptop immediately. More than half of the DWSIM part cannot be done properly on a Mac. OpenFOAM can be done well, but you’ll need to use Docker for installation.

Learning Outcome

Pre-Midsem: Besides providing good exposure to a chemical process simulator, topics on DWSIM are heavily used in CL 452: Process Design Project next sem. Might come in handy with IDP-SLPs as well.

Post-Midsem: Unless you’re going into hard-core fluids research, this course is probably the last time you will hear of OpenFOAM. Computational Fluid Dynamics (or CFD) isn’t required at all for the Design Project.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Note: This part of the course runs in two modes: (1) regular mode wherein you attend the one lab session every week (course content listed below), and (2) project mode, where you can skip all the labs and instead take up a half-semester-long project to create a new DWSIM model for some piece of equipment (to be coded in Python). Here, a final project report has to be drafted. You can freely choose which mode you want to take up, but I’d personally recommend regular mode since you will be using DWSIM extensively in CL 452.

Pre-Midsem Topics:
Week 1: Basics of Flowsheeting; Bubble and Dew Point Calculations
Week 2: Distillation Columns and Sensitivity Analysis
Week 3: Heat Exchangers, Conversion Reactors
Week 4: Multiple Reactions, Gibbs Reactor, Equilibrium Reactor
Week 5: PFR, CSTR, Heterogeneous Catalytic Reactions
Week 6: Case Study for Flowsheet Simulation

None of these topics is tough per se, but DWSIM is notorious for crashing, especially as you move on to more complex equipment.

Post-Midsem Course Content

Note: This part also runs in two modes: (1) regular mode wherein you attend the one lab session every week (course content listed below), and (2) project mode, where you can skip all labs and instead take up a half-semester-long project in which you study a fluid dynamics problem using CFD in OpenFOAM, again ending with a project report. You can freely choose which mode you want to take up — both modes are quite chill.

Post-Midsem Topics:
Week 1: Creating a 2D Geometry and Mesh using BlockMeshDict (not Gmsh)
Week 2: Creating a 3D Pipe Geometry; Simulating Flow through the Pipe
Week 3: Post-processing in ParaView; Simulation of 2D Turbulent Flow in a Channel
Week 4: Obtaining RTD Plots
Week 5: Case Study

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 5

Completeness: 5

Depth: 5

Usefulness for exams: 5

Teaching method

For both modules, there were no teaching sessions, nor slides — the ratings I’ve put down are for the FOSSEE Spoken Tutorials that the Prof provides (they’re also available for free on the Spoken Tutorials website). We had to go through them before each lab session, and there was a short SAFE quiz based on the tutorial for each week. The tutorials are quite good and cover everything required; no additional prep was necessary.

I took up regular mode for DWSIM. During the lab sessions, we were given a problem sheet which had to be completed in the 3 hours of the session. The Prof and TAs were available to solve doubts and resolve software issues. TAs were particularly helpful and had a good grasp of the concerned software. After the session was over, we were given a “Homework Sheet” to be submitted later. Overall, I had no problems with the teaching, however, DWSIM itself is a little difficult to work with and suffers from frequent crashes and *many* bugs. Be patient, and be sure to save your work regularly.

I decided to go with project mode for OpenFOAM since I was already familiar with the software. I managed to save up on a lot of time during the sem and drafted my report after the endsems. Even though it was time-consuming, the project was a great experience. While the Prof wasn’t available to help solve my doubts, the FOSSEE team was extremely helpful and got me through some major snags in the simulation.

Grading Scheme

Pre-Midsem (Regular Mode):
1. SAFE Quizzes: 5%
2. Submission during lab + HW sheet: 5%
3. Endsem: 40%

Pre-Midsem (Project Mode):
1. Report: 50%

Post-Midsem (Regular Mode):
1. SAFE Quizzes: 12.5%
2. Submission during lab + HW sheet: 12.5%
3. Endsem: 25%

Post-Midsem (Project Mode):
1. Report: 50%

Feedback on Evaluation

For both modules, the weekly problem sheets were pretty simple and didn’t take up too much time. The DWSIM endsem was a bit time-constrained, but on the whole quite simple if you had been regular with the lab sessions. The endsem for OpenFOAM was far simpler and easy to complete within the time allotted.

If you are unfamiliar with Python, the DWSIM project might get really difficult. OpenFOAM projects can be tough, but it mostly depends on the geometry’s complexity (since Gmsh isn’t allowed). But again, in both modules, the the TAs are very helpful and approachable, so if you’re stuck, please ask them your doubts.

Nature of the Course: Time Needed

Moderate

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Easy

Course Grading

Moderate

Course Grading Review

The whole class (S1+S2) was graded together. Grading was decent at 12 AAs, 15 ABs and 25 BBs in a class of 132. Refer to grading stats of CL 455 (2021-1) for more details.

Reference Texts

Only Spoken Tutorials were provided as reference material, and they were sufficient. Here’s the link: https://spoken-tutorial.org

Additional Remarks

This course requires regular efforts and good concept clarity in order to excel. Programming skills are also important since all assignments and endsems were based on the MATLAB implementation of the numerical methods.

We thank Arjun Yennemadi for sharing the course review.

We thank the Chemical DAMP team for this review

title: Optimization
description: Introduction to Process Optimization; Formulation of Various Process Optimization Problems and their Classification; Basic Concepts of Optimization-Convex and Concave Functions, Necessary and sufficient conditions for Stationary Points; Optimization of one-dimensional Functions; Unconstrained Multivariable Optimization- Direct Search Methods. Indirect First Order and Second Order Methods; Linear Programming and its Applications; Constrained Multivariable Optimization-Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for Constrained Optimum, Quadratic Programming, Generalized Reduced Gradient Method, Successive Linear and Quadratic Programming; Optimization of Staged and Discrete Processes, Dynamic Programming, Integer and Mixed Integer Programming.

reviews.body:
Instructor

Prof. Mani Bhushan

Learning Outcome

Learning Constrained and Unconstrained optimization techniques and their implementations (Both numerically and coding). 

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Problem formulation, Convexity, Hessian and properties, Unconstrained optimization-Line search, Trust region methods, AC, CC and WC conditions, Conjugate Gradient: Fletcher Reeve (FR-CG), Powell Dogleg Method

Post-Midsem Course Content

Constrained Optimization – Lagrange Multipliers, KKT Matrix, Equality Constrained Quadratic Programming (ECQP) Problem, Inequality Constrained Quadratic Programming (ICQP) Problems, Active-Set Method, 
Linear Programming (LP) Problems
Primal and Dual Problems

Slides

Completeness: 5
Depth: 5

Teaching method

Professor taught the course very well. He was genuinely interested in learning of students and clears the doubts until its fully resolved. He does not run away with course, but gives time in lecture and emphases on basic and important concepts that would be important outside the course. Some topics were left and were given for self-study(were not asked in examination) by the professor.

Moderate

Course Grading

Moderate

Better than last year but a typical grading stats of ChE course.

Reference Texts

2. T. F. Edgar, D. M. Himmelblau, “Optimization of Chemical Processes”, McGraw-Hill

Motivation behind the course:

The motivation for the course is to understand the multiphase flow systems that have become increasingly important in a wide variety of engineering systems for their optimum design and safe operations. The course will initially give the student the impression that it is a bit difficult to understand, but once the student begins to appreciate the beauty of the maths used and the concepts involved, he/she will fall in love with the subject in no time.

Course Content:

Stability of a fixed point, Bifurcations, Examples from hydrodynamics, The Jeans gravitational instability, The Rayleigh-Taylor interface instability, The Rayleigh-Plateau capillary instability, The Rayleigh-Bernard thermal instability, The Bernard-Marangoni thermocapillary instability, Criteria for linear stability, Convective and absolute instability, Instability of a mixing layer, Viscous instability of parallel flows.

title: State Estimation: Theory and Applications
description: This course will deal with theory, tools and techniques for estimation of process states (variables) in the presence of uncertainty. This uncertainty is in the form of uncertainty (noise) in measurements as well as disturbances/noise in the process. The theory covered will deal with estimation for dynamic systems for both linear and nonlinear processes, as well as for gaussian and non-gaussian noise characteristics. Issues related to constraints on process states (constrained estimation), and delayed and multirate measurements will also be dealt with. It is expected that this course will expose the student to not only the well-accepted basics of estimation, but also to recent advances in this area. Throughout the course, the main ideas will be illustrated using matlab-based examples. The following topics will be covered: (i) Linear Optimal (Kalman) Filtering: Least squares formulation, Stochastic formulation, Riccatti equation and solution techniques, Sensitivity and sub-optimal filtering, Smoothing. (ii) Nonlinear Filtering with Gaussian Noise: Extended Kalman filtering, Unscented Kalman filtering. (iii) Particle Filtering: Sequential Monte-Carlo (SMC) methods, Markov processes, SMC based particle filtering, Resampling-MOVE algorithm, Importance sampling, SMC based smoothing, Self organizing state space models, Regularization in particle filtering. (iv) Miscellaneous Topics: Deterministic linear and nonlinear observers, Combined parameter and state estimation, Constrained moving horizon estimators, Incorporation of delayed and multirate measurements.

While there are no formal pre-requisities, it is expected that the student should have some background in probability/statistics and should have done a basic course on control system design/theory, or linear system analysis or state space modeling and analysis. State Estimation – Theory and Applications


Click here to continue reading

Mani Bhushan

Autumn ‘20

Course Difficulty

Depends on previous exposure. But all preliminaries are covered from the basics, so it is manageable.

Time Commitment Required

Can vary highly from person to person. More or less like an average core course

Grading Policy and Statistics

Grading was done based on 4 assignments(15 %), midsem(15 %), endsem (45 %) and course projects (25 %).

Attendance Policy

None

Pre-requisites

Prior knowledge of probability theory and statespace representations would be advantageous and not necessary.

Evaluation Scheme

4 assignments(15 %), midsem(15 %), endsem (45 %) and course projects (25 %).

Topics Covered in the Course

The course starts with an introduction to the basics of probability theory, system modelling and weighted least squares, followed by various estimation algorithms (mainly Kalman filters).

Teaching Style

Despite the course content being new, all the topics are taught from scratch, so its not that difficult to follow.

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

Actual implementation of the algorithms provides a much better understanding. Assignments and projects were both really well designed.

Feedback on Exams

Midsems and Endsems were comparatively easier than the content covered in class.

Motivation for taking this course

I am interested in the study of control systems, of which estimation is an integral part. Kalman filters in particular have been applied to a wide range of fields. This course provides an intuitive understanding, rigorous maths as well as implementation of Kalamn filters.

Course Highlights

Better understanding of the algorithms thorugh implementation to real life physical systems

How strongly would I recommend this course?

I think this is something which is required in almost all engineering disciplines. The course structure is really good, so is the teaching. Overall I feel this is a pretty good course for those who are interested in core engineering.

When to take this course?

5th semester; 5th or 7th semester is ideal in my opinion

Going Forward

You can always chose to continue your course project with the professor even after the semester.

References Used

  1. A. Gelb (editor), “Applied Optimal Estimation”, M.I.T. Press, 1988.
  2. D. Simon, “Optimal State Estimation: Kalman, H∞ and Nonlinear Approaches”, Wiley Interscience.

  7. A. H. Jazwinski, “Stochastic Processes and Filtering Theory”, Academic Press, 1979.
  8. R. Branko, S. Arulampalam, N. Gordon, “Beyond the Kalman Filter: Particle Filters
    for Tracking Applications”, Boston: Artech House, 2004

title: Sustainable Engineering Principles
description: Part I: Overview and basics (~4 hours)  Basics of sustainability and sustainable development  Brundtland commission report, Millennium Ecosystems Assessment, IPCC report  Evolution from green engineering to sustainable engineering  Motivating examples from chemical as well as other branches of engineering Part II: Sustainability quantification (~15 hours)  Why quantification is important?  Different quantification methods: o Brief review of well established economic indicators o Footprint analysis (carbon footprint, water footprint) o Life cycle inventory and assessment o Ecological/environmental carrying capacity o Information theory based indicators for dynamic systems o Resource use intensity o Exergy, emergy etc. o Social indicators Part III: Design of sustainable systems (~15 hours)  Importance of incorporating sustainability in design  Examples of sustainable designs  Design methods for sustainability o Moving from quantification to decision making (design) o LCA o System dynamics models o Other methods  1-2 Hands-on activities using freely available tools such as the OpenLCA (http://www.openlca.org/) Part IV: Case studies: Sustainable design in chemical engineering and other allied fields (~ 6 hours)  Examples of performing sustainable design from different fields such as a energy, water, material use, solvent selection, ionic liquids  Guest lectures by domain experts (e.g. faculty from CTARA, HSS or from outside of IITB), and demonstration using educational modules (e.g. Center for Sustainable Engineering, http://www.csengin.org/csengine/)

reviews.body:
Instructor
Prof. Yogendra Shastri

Course Type

Department Elective

Semester

Autumn (Odd)

Pre-Requisite

There are no pre-requisites for this course.

Learning Outcome

The course is good for anyone enthusiastic to know/work in the sustainability sector. It gives a good backdrop of what sustainability is, its various dimensions and its importance in the world today, along with citations of some practical applications of sustainable engineering design around us.\u00a0

Pre-Midsem Course Content

The course starts with what sustainability is and a broad overview of its various dimensions. It also talks about different kind of metrics used to determine sustainability, such as water and carbon footprint and their calculation methodology, etc.

Post-Midsem Course Content

The course covers concepts of sustainable designs and green chemistry, followed by green engineering. There are also real-life case studies/examples of where these designs are used in the world around us.\u00a0

Slides
Ease of Understanding: 5
Completeness: 5
Depth: 4

Teaching method

The prof. was regular and very involved in teaching the course. He goes an extra mile to ensure that students understand the concepts taught in class and also provides additional resources to keep the students engaged.\u00a0

Grading Scheme

Weekly quizzes and assignments \u2013 minor, Project and Endsem \u2013 Major, The professor did take class participation into account, however, not sure of the exact weightage assigned to it.

Feedback on Evaluation

A quiz is conducted weekly which is based on the topic covered in the classes in that week, however, they are extremely easy to score if one is following the lectures. The project, however, does require significant effort and involvement. The professor expects the project to show results which are quantitative in nature, if possible (not just theoretical) and it should be based on the learnings from the course. The endsem paper was slightly lengthy but grading was decent.\u00a0

Nature of the Course: Time Needed

Light

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Easy

Course Grading

Moderate

Course Grading Review

The grading for the course was decent, followed the usual bell curve. The quizzes are extremely easy and there is no midsem conducted for the course, hence a major contribution to the grade is from the endsem and the course project.\u00a0

Reference Texts

The prof would give additional reading material and research papers occasionally whenever he found it relevant to the topic being covered in class.

title: Product Research & Development
description: The course will be an introduction to the methodology, skills, and "competencies" required for target/objective oriented R&D in creation of Chemical Technology embedded "Products". The "Products" under consideration could be consumer goods, foods, specialty chemicals, equipment or devices. The course will include case studies demonstrating the process of Product R&D, as well as application of rigorous Scientific and Chemical Engineering principles, to solve problems posed by real life products. Apart from class room lectures, the students will have to carry out short term assignments/projects. The course would be of benefit to those interested in pursuing careers in R&D or Product Innovation. (A few interested 2nd, 3rd,4th Year B.Tech students will be permitted to opt for the course, although it is open, primarily to 5th year Dual Degree & PG students.)

Prof. VM Naik

I was enthusiastic about Research in Chemical Engineering along with how new chemical engineering embedded products are developed to solve a real life problem in industry or society in general. The course would be of benefit to those interested in pursuing careers in R&D or Product Innovation.

There aren’t any pre requisites, but it’s good to have prior knowledge of thermodynamics.

The course will be an introduction to the methodology, skills, and “competencies” required for target/objective oriented R&D in creation of Chemical Technology embedded “Products”. The “Products” under consideration could be consumer goods, foods, specialty chemicals, equipment or devices. The course involves case studies to demonstrate the process of Chemical Product Development. It involves applications of technical knowledge such as phase diagrams, solubility etc. along with Project management skills. The case studies are mostly some real industry problems so they are quite interesting to understand the whole methodolgy of developing new product.

Attendance – 10
Participation during Class -10
Assignments – 10
Minitest 1 – 20
Minitest 2 – 20
End Sem – 30.

Course was run in an online mode in our time. Lectures were very well organised and revolved about product development case studies or technical concepts related to phase diagrams, solubility etc. They were quite interactive and at times professor used to put up a little question to get response from all students in chat box. We had 1/2 short assignments every week.They were not very difficult to solve if you understand the content of previous lectures well. The mini-tests were also not difficult to solve but were bit lengthy. We were allowed to keep one cheat sheet with us which could have all formula/information in concise form. title: Turbulent Multiphase Flow
description: Single phase turbulence Introduction to stability and transition. Vorticity dynamics. Origin and characteristics of turbulence. Spatial and temporal correlations. Time and length scales in turbulence. Momentum transport and concept of Reynolds stress. Kinetic Energy of turbulence. Spectral dynamics and energy cascading. Multiphase dynamics Properties of dispersed phase flows. Dilute versus dense phase flows. Interphase coupling between particles/droplets-fluid for mass, momentum, and energy transport. Particle-particle interactions. Phenomena of particle clustering, turbulence attenuation and augmentation, turbophoresis. Modeling and Computational methods Lagrangian-Eulerian and Eulerian-Eulerian approach to model multiphase turbulent flow. Introduction to direct numerical simulation using spectral method. Hard sphere and discrete element model (DEM) to address dispersed phase interaction.

description: Development of Chemical Process Systems; Process Integration, Philosophy of Targeting, Thermodynamic Approach, Mathematical Programming Approach; Heat Exchanger Networks and Energy Optimization; Utility and Power Systems; Total Site Analysis; Separation Systems, Distillation Column Sequencing and Integration; Mass Exchange Networks; Networks for Water and Hydrogen Management; Reaction Path Synthesis, Reactor Networks, Reactor-Separator-Recycle Systems: Grassroots and Retrofit Designs, Multiple Base Case Designs; Flexibility Considerations; Industrial Applications and Case Studies.

reviews.body:
Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Motivation behind the course: This course is very rich in content and extremely helpful for students willing to make a career in process design or process engineering. I would recommend this course for those who are interested in process design.

Professor when you took the course: Malik R.K., Sanjay Mahajani

Course Description: This course will be an advanced version of the process design course which you will learn in your last semester. This course will in fact, help you in your design project as well. Some basic understanding of CRE, McCabe-Thiele method and flowsheeting learned in 6th semester will be required and you can take this course in the 7th semester. Right from the start, the course runs in two parallel streams taught by two different professors. 

One part deals with understanding reactor system design, choice of reactor networks for obtaining maximum conversion/selectivity, heuristics for process design, analysis of flowsheets and choosing the right sequence, understanding trade-offs in selecting flowsheets, and calculating economic potential. A major part of the course will deal with designing Heat exchanger networks- this part is extremely important and spans nearly half the semester. You will also understand pinch technology and other techniques.

The second part of the course deals entirely with distillation column design. While you have learned it in the mass transfer course, here it will be on a whole different level and you will be taught to design the column sequence for any non-ideal system. 

Both parts of the course converge to the synthesis of a flowsheet, which is an extremely important skill in process design.

Pre-Requisite

It is advisable to take this course after completing CL203 and CL254 to gain a basic understanding of Fluid Mechanics. However, there was no prerequisite imposed by the professor.

Learning Outcome

Granular Mechanics is quite a new field with very little concrete research present (as the prof also mentions during the course). Prof Khakhar is actively engaged in research in this topic and is quite enthusiastic about offering projects regarding this as well. The best example of understanding Granular Mechanics is to experiment with sand (also covered in certain topics in the course).
There are no further courses where the basics of this course are applicable. However, it is a vast research domain (to which we are offered a very simplistic introduction).

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Introduction & Basics of Granular Materials (how they vary from fluidic particles), Vectors & Tensors, Quasi-static flows

Post-Midsem Course Content

The post midsem topics are slightly harder and require more attention to detail.
Topics – Rapid Flows, Dense Flows, Surface Flows, Mixing & Segregation

Slides

Teaching method

The professor was quite enthusiastic about delivering concepts. He would clarify every doubt raised in the class and ensured that students regularly followed topics covered.
He was regular in taking lectures and was quite involved in ensuring that students understood topics (via assignments and mini-quizzes).

Feedback on Evaluation

The quizzes were taken after each major topic was covered. They were easy, open-book, 15-minute quizzes which were taken to judge if we had followed the content covered.
The assignments were slightly lengthier and required revising the notes the prof had provided. However, the assignments were to be done in groups of 2/3 members and were manageable at the end. The assignments helped in applying the concepts taught in class, which was helpful for the midsem/endsem.
The project was to be completed on LAMMPS, an open-software used to simulate motion of granular particles. The prof floated a list of topics, out of which one had to be chosen. There were 2 submissions – the first one was just to define the problem and the second one was to complete the project. The project was quite easy (prof initially took a few sessions on LAMMPS and the software manual was easy to follow).

The midsem and endsem exams were open-book. A few questions had a little conceptual twist, most of them were straight-forward and easily available in the prof’s notes. Overall, the exams were fairly easy to navigate if one had revised the notes the prof had shared.

Reference Texts

The prof had initially shared a list of reference books. However, these were not required as the class notes were comprehensive.
1. An introduction to granular flow by K. Kesava Rao and Prabhu R. Nott
(Cambridge Univ. Press, 2008).
2. Sands, Powders, and Grains: An Introduction to the Physics of Granular Materials
(Partially Ordered Systems) by Jacques Duran (Springer Verlag; March 2000)
3. Pattern Formation in Granular Materials (Springer Tracts in Modern Physics,
164) by Gerald H. Ristow (Springer Verlag; December 1999)

Academic 1997)

We thank the Chemical DAMP team for this review

Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Prof. Devang Khakhar

Granular materials like sand are all around us, and the nature of their flow is important in modelling many natural and industrial processes. However, these materials are of particular interest to scientists because despite being composed of many discrete elements, they behave as one bulk material. In fact, being composed of discrete elements especially of the mm-scale imparts many unique and interesting properties to the flow. You might remember that in Newtonian fluids, shear stress is proportional to shear strain; but as you will see with granular materials, shear stress is often proportional to the square of shear strain. This is just one example of the special properties of these materials. Taking this course will provide a comprehensive introduction to the kind of research being done in this area, all the while using mathematical modelling to explain such phenomena.

None officially, but personally, it would be meaningless to take this course before completing CL 254.

Introduction – Granular materials and their occurrence. Description of typical operations.
Basics – Characterisation of granular materials. Particle-particle interactions: Friction, Collisions, Deformation, Cohesion. Angles of repose. Characteristics of flowing and static granular materials, flow regimes.
Tools – Vectors and tensors: algebra, differential operations.
Numerical methods – Hard particle simulations, Soft particle simulations. Introduction to LAMMPS, solution of example problems.
Quasi-static flows – Mohr-Coulomb theory, critical state theory. Analysis of bins and hoppers. Microscopic analysis: force chains.
Rapid flows – Bagnold theory. Granular kinetic theory. Applications.
Dense flows – Empirical models: μ-I model, applications.
Surface flows – Avalanches, chute flows, heap flows, prorating cylinder flow. Depth averaged equations. Applications.
Mixing and segregation – Self-diffusion of tracers. Mixing in tumblers. Segregation phenomena. Applications.

10% (HW Assignments x 3) + 10% (Quizzes x 2) + 10% (Project on LAMMPS) + 20% (Midsem) + 50% (Endsem)

The course had lectures of 3 hours per week. Lectures were interesting but a little slow, and by Surface Flows, it is easy to get lost in all the maths. The Prof and TA are very helpful and approachable though, so be sure to clear all your doubts. There is no need to make notes as Sir provides handwritten copies of all his notes on Moodle, although making notes is recommended to keep concentration levels high during the lectures.
Assignments were interesting, had a mix of easy, moderate and tough questions, and would normally take 7-8 hours if solved carefully. The quizzes, midsem and endsem are all straightforward if you are thorough with the way problems are solved during the lectures. All exams were open-book.
The course project was the highlight of this course. Topics were floated, and we were required to solve a problem / study a phenomenon using Discrete Element Method (DEM) simulations in LAMMPS. Getting started with LAMMPS requires a lot of time and patience, so try not to delay the simulations till the last minute. Optional help sessions for LAMMPS were also scheduled from time to time. A final report was to be drafted, detailing the methodology and results.
Overall, this was a very interesting course with a lot to offer.

title: Modelling Chemical and Biiological Patterns
description: Temporal patterns in chemical and biological systems – Introduction to temporally oscillating chemical and biological systems. Examples from chemistry such as CIDMA, Brusselator, B-Z reactions. Examples from biology such as circadian rhythms. Experimental observations and measurements. Modeling and some analytical solutions to ODEs. Spatio-temporal pattern formation and analytical solutions – Introduction to spatial pattern formation in chemical and biological systems. Reaction-diffusion systems. BZ- reactions, Turing patterns. Spatial patterns in reaction – diffusion-advection systems. Stationary and travelling waves. Modeling of spatio-temporal patterns. Some analytical solutions to PDEs. Computer simulation of spatio-temporal dynamics – Numerical solutions to PDEs. Simulating the evolution of spatio-temporal patterns in chemical and biological systems.

title: Introduction to Risk Analysis
description: Science of systems and concept of risk – introduction, systems engineering, concepts of risk assessment and management, applications to water resources; Steps of risk characterization - hazard identification, exposure assessment, vulnerability analysis, risk mapping, example of risk characterization to natural hazards, risk assessment as a distributed process: example of Monte-Carlo techniques in human health and ecological risk assessments; Uncertainty analysis – introduction, uncertainty taxonomy, sensitivity analysis, probabilistic uncertainty, fuzzy systems, interval analysis, risk-based decision making; Risk filtering and ranking – introduction, past efforts in risk filtering, methodological framework of filtering and ranking, different ranking methods, case studies; Multiobjective trade-off analysis – introduction, examples of multiple environmental objectives, surrogate worth trade-off, characterizing a proper noninferior solution; Decision-tree analysis – introduction, methodological approach, differences between single and multiple objective decision trees.

reviews.body:
How did you find out about this course, and what led you to take this course?

I found it on ASC. When I had to select an IE, I looked through the running course list in every department I was interested in, and this one fit with my timetable.

Curriculum of the course.

The curriculum of this course includes ecological and human health risk assessment, probability and statistics, uncertainty modeling, fuzzy mathematics, and decision science.

Rigor of the course.

The course is easy in the initial 3-4 weeks, then the rigor quickly picks up. Although, it is not hard to keep up with it. Just listening actively in class, and engaging with the content by asking questions, helps a lot. They keep a few tutorials and there is usually one quiz before midsem and endsem. Studying one week before those is also enough, if you stay consistent with attending classes and just reviewing the material weekly.

Pros/ cons of the course and suggestions for people wanting to take this course.

The course is very interesting. However, it is modeled heavily for research enthusiasts, especially in fields like environment and climate. A lot of PhDs take this course. You can take the course purely out of interest also, even if you have interest in decision science and not much in risk assessment.

Relevance & Usefulness of the course.

This course is highly relevant for me, because my major is Environmental Engineering. I can directly use the learnings from this course for my research projects.

title: Atmosphere and Climate Change
description: The atmosphere – scales of motion, large-scale motion, general circulation, troposphere-stratosphere transport, atmospheric composition, global cycles and lifetimes. Atmospheric structure and thermodynamics – pressure, density, composition, temperature structure, water in the atmosphere. Atmospheric transport – Equations of atmospheric fluid mechanics, energy equation, turbulence, mixing length models, boundary layer processes, variation of wind speed with height. Atmospheric photochemistry and chemical kinetics – tropospheric gas-phase, aerosol and cloud chemistry. Aerosol dynamics in the troposphere – transport properties, thermodynamics and ucleation, dry and wet deposition. Cloud physics – water equilibrium in the atmosphere, cloud and fog formation, cloud condensation nuclei, cloud processing of aerosols, ice clouds, rain, cloud climatology. Atmospheric radiation transfer – solar and terrestrial radiation, energy balance for earth and atmosphere, light scattering and absorption, Rayleigh, Geometric and Mie theory, extinction and aerosol mixing state, radiative models for clear and cloudy atmospheres. Atmospheric chemical transport and general circulation models.

title: Introduction to Climate Change
description: 1.The physical science of climate change Climate System; Causes of Climate Change, Climate data and trends; Analyses of climate data; Global atmospheric composition: Greenhouse gases and aerosols; Extreme weather events, sea level rise; Climate projections and their uncertainties. 2.Climate impacts, vulnerability and risks Assessing climate impacts on key sectors and systems (heat stress, water resources, coastal zones, agricultural systems); Concepts of vulnerability and risk; Assessing vulnerability and risk; Concepts of coping, adaptation and risk management, adaptive capacity, indicators and metrics; Adaptation planning and management including mainstreaming and climate resilient development. 3.Climate mitigation and policy Economics of climate change, Least cost carbon strategies; Frameworks for multi-criteria mitigation assessment; Multilateral and national responses; International climate negotiations and geopolitics of response; Policies and measures, including CDM, emissions trading; National policies for climate change (NAPCC, national missions).

reviews.body:
Instructor

Prof. Chandra Venkataraman, Prof. Pradip Kalbar, Prof. Trupti Mishra

Semester

Autumn ‘21

Course Difficulty

Content wise heavy, a wide range of topics covered in the course of 4 months, might get overwhelming. You will be expected to finish reading material (avg. 10 pages document) before each class

Time Commitment Required

out of class hours 5 hours per week(according to me)

Grading Policy and Statistics

Problem sets / tutorials / comprehensive problems (PS1-PS3, P4-P5, P6-P7) – 20% (grading on
completion and computational / research effort)
Midsem – 25%
Endsem – 35%
Presentation / quiz / term paper / debate / class participation – 20% (10% each of any two)

AA 1
AB 8
BB 4
BC 4
CC 1
CD 1
FF 1
FR 2

Attendance Policy

Class participation marks, so in a way compulsory

Pre-requisites

Second and third module certain policy aspects, if you have some background that could help

Topics Covered in the Course

introduction to a fundamental understanding and problem centered analysis of climate
change science and responses, with an emphasis on regional scale impacts, assessment of technological options to address mitigation and abatement strategies

Module 1 provides an overview of the physical science of climate change
Module 2 will address climate impacts, vulnerability and risks
Module 3 focuses on climate mitigation and policy

Teaching Style

Lectures slides used for teaching in class (slides not exhaustive), further a 20 pages (approx) document- reading material to be covered before each class. In each class you are supposed to come prepared with the answer of a question given to you before itself and speak for about 2 minutes, that carried about 10% weightage

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

They are designed to build your understanding properly, only if you give sufficient amount of time on the tutorials/assignments as well as attend classes regularly

Feedback on Exams

Quizzes- objective type
Midsem and endsem fairly difficult

Motivation for taking this course

I am into meteorology, climate change, sustainability and have worked on the same in the past. I took this course with the belief that it will cover the science aspect of climate change and relate it to predictability, but it is just one of the three modules.

How strongly would I recommend this course?

If you can invest a lot of time and energy for a particular semester based on your overall load, then it can be a great learning opportunity. YOU WILL NEED TO PUT A LOT OF EFFORTS.

When to take this course?

Fourth year, autumn semester

References Used

Reading material (topic specific) will be provided by the respective instructors
