reviews.body:
Course Description :

The course content includes Proofs and structures, Counting and combinatorics, Elements of graph theory and Introduction to abstract algebra and number theory. The course had an associated tutorial with it.

Being a core CSE department course, CS207 was not open for non-CSE students under Prof. Akshay.

Course Pattern and Logistics :

4 Quizzes + Midsem + Endsem

Pop up Quizzes in class and assignments (5%)

Comments on the Course and Instructor :

Prof. Akshay was very enthusiastic and interested in student’s learning and answered questions in detail. The professor kept the class interactive as well. He was present in the tutorials and  supervised the TAs as well as helped students. This made it one of the best tutorial sessions. Attending the tutorial and lectures regularly was very useful.  Also there were multiple optional help sessions arranged.

Attendance was not mandatory. Even then turn up was very high. But very low attendance was taken seriously.

The course as well as the instructor was very good.

Who will find it interested ?

Anyone who is interested in Mathematics will find it interesting. This gives you an introduction to various mathematics tools required in Computer Science.

Review 2
——-

Instructor : Prof S Akshay ( https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~akshayss/ )

Logistics :

Comments on course :

Comments on instructor :

 

References :

Review 3
——-

Course description – The course is one of the most important building blocks of Computer Science. It teaches you how to think mathematically in terms of detailed proofs. The concepts taught in CS207 are central to the understanding of Computer Science and problem solving. Discrete structures is slightly different from the usual mathematics that students are used to. It involves rigorous proof techniques, theorems and results which are applicable to a wide variety of situations. The main topics include:

 

Course pattern and logistics – There are 4 quizzes, a midsem and an endsem. The weightages depend on the instructor. Generally, quizzes are 7.5% each (25% overall), midsem is 35% and endsem is 40%. In the past 2 years, it has been seen that quizzes are slightly harder than the midsem and the endsem

 

Comments on the instructor – Professor Akshay S was the instructor for the course in Fall 2014 and 2015. He is an excellent instructor who has a brilliant understanding of the subject. The best part about him is that he is enthusiastic about the course and he actually wants the students to learn. He personally attends the tutorials and tries his best to ensure that each student is understanding the topic. Definitely, one of the best instructors our department has.

 

Who will find it interesting – Anyone who has interest in Mathematics and Computer Science will find the course interesting. The course offered by Prof. Akshay isn’t open for non-CSE students. The non-CSE students can join the minor course offered separately. Note that this course is a bit heavy and demands your time. So, don’t take this course if you are looking for a light course in which you can doze off during the lecture.

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Name – Shreya Pathak

Course Name – Discrete Structures

Course Code – CS 207

Academic Year – 2019-2020

Semester – Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Manoj Prabhakaran 

Motivation: Core Course/ Covers basics of a lot of concepts for further courses

Prerequisite: None 

Course-content: Logic, Formal Proofs, Number Theory, Cryptography, Set Theory, Combinatorics, Graph Theory, Recursion, Complexity Theory, Automata Theory. Only the introduction and basics of the topic are contained. The course was mathematical and logical. The content seemed vast, but, the topics were covered very well.

Lectures: Twice per week, of 1.5 hours each. 

Labs: (Teams info) Not Applicable

Project: Not Applicable

Assignments: Not Applicable

Exams: Apart from the SAFE quizzes (5%) conducted in each class, there were 5 quizzes of which best 4 were considered(30%), midsem(25%) and endsem(40%). 

Difficulty: The course covers a lot of topics so it seemed vast. Some topics were also somewhat complicated. However, the professor clears the concepts very well. The tutorial sheets provided also contained application-based questions which required original thinking on behalf of the student. The exams were very logical and covered all the topics taught in class. They were more focused on the application of the concepts than just rote learning with several hints provided in the papers. The end-semester exam was, however, somewhat long. 

Grading: The grading was somewhat strict with AA given to only 10% of the class (cut-off ~ 90%). However, it is easy to get good marks in the course if one follows the course content regularly.  

References: Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, Kenneth H. Rosen

Advanced courses: It has applications in several of the theoretical CS courses in further semesters

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Review By – Sahil Shah

Course Name – Discrete Structures

Course Code – CS 207

Academic Year – 2017-2018

Semester – Odd

Course Instructor – Prof. Ajit Diwan

Motivation: (it is a core course!)

Prerequisite: None (not sure really! But again, does not matter for core courses)

Course-content: Starts with propositions, logic and proof techniques and their applications. Next comes sets, relations, cardinality and counting problems including combinatorics as well. The final 30% of the course is on graph theory.

Lectures: Every week there are 3 hours of lectures assigned as per the timetable, of which 2 were for teaching and 1 was to get doubts in the tutorials solved.

Assignments: No assignments (but several tutorial sheets)

Exams: There were 3 quizzes of which the best 2 were considered. This accounted for 20% of the final grade. The midsem accounts for another 30% and the endsem has 50% weightage

Difficulty: The course is difficult. There is no denying that. Most of you would have heard about the course and the instructor at some point in your stay at IITB, but the course is not as bad as it is made out to be. The tutorials are really difficult and you will rarely be able to completely solve a tutorial, but that is what the instructor aims for. The aim is to develop mathematical and logical reasoning skills and if you show that you have tried the problem, he will help you with it. The examinations are tough as well, but there is usually at least one fairly straightforward question on every exam. In the midsem and endsem examinations, solving all the problems is extremely difficult and not even necessary to secure a good grade. Last year, the endsem was relatively easier than the other examinations, and accounts for half of the final grade. Keeping up with the course material and discussing tutorials with friends would be helpful in this case. It is imperative to not give up early on if you score poorly in the first quiz or two.

Attendance: The instructor does not care about attendance but attending classes is in your benefit as it will be very difficult to understand the concepts a day (or even a week) before the exam.

Grading: Quite relaxed as compared to the difficulty of the course. The cutoffs go pretty low (the cutoff for AA was in the low 60s and that for BB was 38 as far as I remember) though there are relatively few AAs and ABs handed out. He usually tries not to fail anyone (but this does not mean you can slack off completely!)

References: Kenneth Rosen but Prof Diwan will not follow it for more than 40% of the course. There is no book that he adheres to as such.

Advanced courses: Graph Theory and any maths-intensive course

 

 

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Name – Mohammad Ali Rehan

Course Name – Discrete Structures

Course Code – CS 207

Academic Year – 2019-2020

Semester – Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Manoj Prabhakaran

Motivation:  It exposes students to fundamental mathematical concepts, and arguing techniques essential in computer science. This course basically covers all the mathematical background that may be required in a variety of subfields in computer science. The content covered here will form a base for students to take up later theoretical courses such as those on complexity or graph theory. The general methods and theorems discussed here also prove highly useful when students do competitive programming or general coding, as this course covers ideas to bound running time of algorithms, argue about the correctness of results, infer properties of given structures, etc.

Prerequisite:  NA

Course-content: Basics of Boolean Logic, Combinatorics, Countability, Complexity theory, graph theory, recursion, automata theory, cryptography, number theory. Topics covered include Hall’s theorem, RSA, Sterling numbers, Bell number, Set bijections, Master Theorem, Euler Totient Function, graph colourings and set covers, intro to NP-completeness, etc.

Lectures: 2 per week, 1.5 hours each. Regular tutorials were also held. The lectures were interesting, and sir explained each concept really well. Doubts were extensively cleared. There was a SAFE quiz in each class. The slides were informative and very nicely structured. In case a student missed a lecture, he/she could cover it by thoroughly going through the slides. Professor loved to make the class interactive and engage students in thoughtful discussions. The class was well-paced, and sir regularly took feedback about the pace to adjust it accordingly. Revision classes were also held before the exams.

Labs: (Teams info) NA

Project: NA

Assignments: NA

Exams: Best 4 out of 5 quizzes, one midsem, one end sem. The end sem was quite a lengthy and difficult paper. It brought together apparently independent concepts studied in different areas of the course and required the students to apply them together to solve the problem at hand. The questions were lengthy; however, each question had a motivation. Every question proved some famous result or property that was very relevant to the course but couldn’t be covered in the class and hence added to the knowledge of the student. A good enough grasp on all concepts to apply them in new situations was necessary. Juxtaposed to this, the midsem and quizzes were lighter, meant to make the students more comfortable with what they learned in class. 

Difficulty: The course was moderately difficult. One simply couldn’t leave it to be done a day before the exam and hope to get good marks(in most cases). Solving the book and problem sets proved useful. Some problem sets were quite difficult and required the students to learn new stuff and theorems. SIr always made sure that a lot of difficult topics weren’t done together, but rather alternated with lighter material. Having said all this, if one kept apace with the class and solved questions regularly, would surely get good marks.

Grading: Getting an AA was difficult (requiring at least 90%); it was handed out to only around 10 students. The rest of the grades were distributed normally as like any other course.

References: Discrete Mathematics and Applications- Rosen

Advanced courses: It has applications in most theoretical CS courses.

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

title: Data Structures and Algorithms
description: Introduction to data structures, abstract data types, analysis of algorithms. Creation and manipulation of data structures: arrays, lists, stacks, queues, trees, heaps, hash tables, balanced trees, tries, graphs. Algorithms for sorting and searching, order statistics, depth-first and breadth-first search, shortest paths and minimum spanning tree.

reviews.body:
Review By – Sumit Chaturvedi

Course Name – Data Structure and Algorithms

Course Code – CS 213

Academic Year – 2017-18

Semester – Autumn Semester

Course Instructor – R.K. Shyamsundar

Motivation: This is an important course to bring you up to date with what many call to be the bread and butter of computer science. Broadly speaking, there are two elements to problem solving – Efficient encoding of a problem statement (Data Structures) and the steps which translate this encoding into the solution (Algorithms).   

Prerequisite: CS 101

Course-content: The professor gave a comprehensive list of all the topics that would be covered throughout the semester. The list included the following:

Note that the above list is not comprehensive in any way.

Points 2, 3 and 4 above explore different ways to provide efficient insert, search and delete operations on some data.

Point 5 deals with text compression algorithms. You have probably seen Huffman Codes in the department introductory course.

Point 6 actually encompasses a lot of algorithms and their applications. For example, Depth First Search is used for topological sorting of a directed acyclic graph, Dijkstra’s algorithm and Bellman Ford algorithm deal with finding the shortest paths in the graph. The last two deal with finding a minimum spanning tree in a graph.

Lectures: To be honest, the lectures are very boring. The professor comes into the class, starts reading the lecture slides and makes weak attempts at eliciting a response from the class. The flow of the lectures is abrupt and incoherent. As a result, by the end of semester, class attendance dropped to less than 25%.

Assignments: The professor hands out some problems which he finds interesting (but frankly no one else does) and tries to discuss them in class. The good part, however, is that there is an accompanying lab course. The problems and tests conducted in the lab are challenging and fun. Make the most of them.  

Exams: There were 3 quizzes, 1 mid sem and 1 end sem exam last year. The first quiz was easy and so was midsem. After midsem, the quizzes became a bit challenging. That being said, they were crucial in grasping the course material.

Difficulty: I found the CS 293 lab to be quite challenging. Practice is essential for this foundational course. Luckily there are a lot of resources when it comes to that. Mentioned in the references section below.

Grading: I don’t want to comment on this because all the grading statistics are freely available on ASC. From there you can do all the number crunching to ensure that with 99.1922% probability you get an AA.

References:

Advanced courses: In the following spring semester, you’ll have the Design and Analysis of Algorithms course. That can be considered as an advanced version of this course. Apart from that, this semester Abhiram Ranade is offering a course on Algorithms and Complexity although from what I gather, it appears to be a graduate level course. Anyway always remember, textbooks and self study are good substitutes for conventional courses.

 

 

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Course Name: CS213
Review By: Gagan Jain
Name of the Prof: Ambarish Kunwar
Year the course was taken in: 2
Attendance Criteria: 0
Difficulty of evaluation(How difficult the test and assignment were and how difficult was the grading): 3
Grading Scheme: 10-20% Assignments, nearly 20% quizzes, rest major exams
Time spent on course(hours per week): 3
Pre-requisites(if any): CS101
Overview(Expect to learn and future prospects): The course is one of the fundamental ones which introduces you to the various important data structures and algorithms for time and space efficient programming.
Course Rating: 4

Instructor

Prof. Milind Sohani

Course Type

Minor Course

Semester

Spring (Even)

Pre-Requisite

There are no hard pre-requisites for this course. Soft prerequisites: At least a bit of coding knowledge, although not expected from the course perspective, is definitely required for this course. Preferable language is C++.

Learning Outcome

CS213-Data Structures and algorithms is the most important course for all those aspiring to pursue a career on the tech side. The knowledge gained during this course is what you are tested on during coding/ technical interviews. The use of DSA is so vast that it serves as a base for all your career options on the IT/software/tech side.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Introduction to DSA, Stack, Queue, Linked List, Trees, Binary trees, Time Complexity, Algorithms & PseudoCodes, Dictionary, Graphs
The Toughest part of the course is applying what you have learnt. Because examination questions are not at all straightforward

Post-Midsem Course Content

Sorting Algorithms, Dijkstra Algorithms, DFS, BFS, More Data structures & their applications, hashing, searching, solving problems using DSA

Slides

Ease of Understanding: 2

Completeness: 3

Depth: 4

Usefulness for exams: 3

Teaching method

Our course was conducted in the online/hybrid mode. The professor shared a google doc with Youtube links for different chapters. The whole course was done on Youtube with NPTEL lectures by a professor from IIT Delhi in 2005. A doubt lecture was conducted once/twice a week, with the professor clearing the doubts in the chapter discussed that year.

Grading Scheme

Quiz(1&2)- 30%
Midsem 30%
Endsem 40%

Feedback on Evaluation

So basically, there are only 4 evaluations evenly distributed throughout the semester. All the examinations are quite tough. It requires a good understanding of the subject to be able to answer them. Pre-Midsem Content is comparatively easy than post-midsem. All exams are open notes, so do maintain notes.

Nature of The Course

Time Needed: Heavy

Level of Difficulty of Understanding: Difficult

Course Grading

Harsh

Course Grading Review

Overall with respect to absolute grading, people score really less in terms of total marks. People with prior coding knowledge tend to perform better. Only Last day study would really put you on the line of passing/failing.(like me, passed by 2 marks. DD xd). You have to be regular, at least with respect to this course. Professor does give FRs to students.

Reference Texts

These are the things I learned the hard way. Do attend the lectures. Try to solve the tutorials, although it’s very tough. Most exams are open book so try to write algorithms & maintain personal notes of important data structures. Online resources can be accessed from anywhere on the net. There are tonnes of resources available.

Comments

By personal experience I would say, Please don’t think it is a last day study easy pass exam. I was very very lucky enough to pass this course somehow. Even minimum time commitment per week would help you easily clear and score well in this course. The course is quite useful for the intern/ placement season. Because this what companies want.

Course Name: CS 213
Review By: Gagan Jain
Name of the Prof: Prof. Subpratim Biswas
Year the course was taken in: 2
Attendance Criteria: 0
Difficulty of evaluation(How difficult the test and assignment were and how difficult was the grading): 3
Grading Scheme: 10-20% Assignments, nearly 20% quizzes, rest major exams
Time spent on course(hours per week): 3
Pre-requisites(if any): CS 101
Overview(Expect to learn and future prospects): The course is one of the fundamental ones which introduces you to the various important data structures and algorithms for time and space efficient programming.
Course Rating: 4

Course Name

CS 213: Data Structures and Algorithms (Minor)

Year for which the review is written 

2017 – 2018 Spring

Professor who took the course

Prof. Abhiram Ranade

Motivation behind the course

The motivation for this course is learning to apply algorithms suitably to access, store and later update the data which requires quite a lot of visualisation and thinking of the various data structures thereby improving our logical thinking as well.

The course also aims to familiarise the student with fundamental data structures like arrays, binary search trees, heap trees, vectors, maps, 2-3 Trees (extension of binary trees), Tries, hash tables, undirected and directed graphs, doubly linked lists and stacks.

Various Algorithms that were introduced are merging and sorting arrays, heapsort and quicksort as advanced sorting techniques, Dijkstra’s and Bellman Ford’s shortest path algorithms, fast multipole method and insertion, deletion and accessing advanced data structures. Also, a brief application of Line Scan technique was discussed.

The above-mentioned topics are important as a building block for studying further organisational structures as it helps in understanding them more deeply. Also, the algorithms discussed are applicable in various other courses such as computer networks and the basic structure of an algorithm can always be built upon in more advanced courses such as machine learning.

Course Content

Pre-requisite

CS101- Computer Programming and Utilization

Feedback on lectures

The professor demanded 80% attendance from the students. The lectures were interesting and the professor would present a problem at the beginning of a class and would develop ideas to tackle it. The professor would not give any breaks and so the student is expected to maintain his concentration for the full class. Attending classes with full concentration helped quite a lot in understanding deep ideas which the professor explained very nicely and with ease.

Feedback on tutorials, assignments and exams

There were 3 C++ programming assignments throughout the course. Problem sets were given for practice occasionally. Solving the assignments cleared any doubts quite effectively. Problems in the exam required a thorough understanding of the concepts as well as speed. The exams were not too scoring for the students with average hovering around 30 to 40 percent.

Evaluation Scheme was as follows:

Assignments: 4%

Quiz: Best 2 out of 3 : 46 %(23 % for each quiz)

End semester exam: 50%

Grading

 

Moderately Tough as can be seen from the following:

AA    5

AB    11

BB    20

BC    22

CC    26

CD    27

DD    28

FR    33

II    3

W    7

 

Study Material and References

The Professor uploaded the slides taught in class on Moodle. He also referred to the following:

An Introduction to Programming through C++ (by the professor himself) was followed for the first half of the semester including the problems in the quizzes.

For the later half of the semester mainly Algorithms By S. Dasgupta, C. H. Papadimitriou, and U. V. Vazirani was followed.

Advanced courses that can be taken after this course

Reviewed By: Himanshu Pradeep Aswani (himanshuaswani123@gmail.com)

Instructor

Sharat Chandran

Semester

Spring ‘20

Course Difficulty

Moderate - difficult if your coding basics are not strong and will require more time commitment. If you have a background of CP should be quite easy for you.

Time Commitment Required

Assignments were quite heavy. Will require good amount of time to understand everything clearly. Lectures also will need good time to absorb.

Grading Policy and Statistics

Normal
12 AAs
22 ABS
35 BBs
8 BCs
21 CCs
8 CDs
5 DDs
out of 111

Attendance Policy

None

Pre-requisites

Knowing DSA beforehand, competitive coding experience will help a lot

Evaluation Scheme

Was not announced clearly in the beginning and was kept under the wraps till the end.
Initially best 5 out of 7-10 tasks were announced but only 6 were given till the end and only 5 were graded. Each had 15% weightage.
Midsem and endsem also had 15% weightage
10% were participation and miscellaneous.
Vivas were announced but later cancelled. Scheme can change anytime with this prof.

Topics Covered in the Course

Priority Q
Recursion 
Quicksort, Analysis
Analysis, and the Method of Summation
Generating Functions, ADT, Dynamic stack 
Dynamic stack implementation (OOP)
Stacks and Linked Allocation
Iterators, Containers, Position
Trees: Basics, Representation, Traversal
Euler Tour, Dictionary (BST), Hashing
Tree
BFS, DFS
Topological sort
Single Source Shortest Paths
Minimum Spanning Trees
Order statistics
Balanced trees
Trie

Teaching Style

Pre recorded lectures of about 2 hours were provided every week. Quality of audio in videos is not very good. Teaching is okayish. Prof is very particular about his rules and his instructions are very diplomatic and confusing.

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

Assignments were quite heavy taking up entire weekends to complete. Will help a lot to get a grip on the subject and also frustrating at times. Plagiarism is strictly checked for and heavily penalized as well.

Feedback on Exams

Exams were online timed contests. Execution was very bad. You will have to write code for each ques and also explain your logic in a separate document in a very short time. Instructions were very long and complicated, which reduced time to solve the question. This is specific to this prof and online sem. Offline sem might have Fill in the blanks or write your logic based questions.

Motivation for taking this course

It is a pre-requisite for most CS courses and also a compulsory course for completing your CS minor.

Course Highlights

Will give you a good idea of most data structures and algorithms

Course Importance

Very useful. It introduces you to the necessary basics for anything ranging from competitive coding, company tests, and any CS course which has DSA as a prerequisite

How strongly would I recommend this course?

I would very highly recommend you to take this course if you are interested in pursuing CS in the future.

When to take this course?

I had taken this in my 4th sem since it was not offered in the 3rd sem. I would suggest you to take it as early as possible so that you will have more freedom to choose courses as ALCs/IEs from the CS department as most of them have DSA as a prereq.

Name – Sumanyu Ghoshal

Course Name – Data Structures and Algorithms

Course Code – CS 213

Academic Year – 2019

Semester – Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Ajit Diwan

Motivation: 

Data Structures and Algorithms is one of the most important courses that a CS undergrad takes up.

One of the most influential books in this domain is:

Data Structures + Algorithms = Programs by Dr. Niklaus Wirth.

 The title of this book itself suggests how important the course is to find efficient solutions for developing programs to solve the problems at hand. Ergo, any student who wishes to make a career in the field of Computer Science must take this course seriously. The course would aid in developing the rigor to think about a problem and come up with elegant and efficient solutions, thus significantly improving an individual’s problem-solving skills.   

Prerequisite:  N/A

Course-content:

The course revolves around the various ways data can be represented(data structures) and some algorithms that can be used with a representation.

Lectures:

Each week had three lectures, each being 1 hour long. Two of these lectures were used to teach the course content. One lecture would be utilized as a tutorial session where the professor would discuss the questions in tests and assignments where we at large had faced problems in and hand out questions that we could discuss among ourselves, the professor, and the Teaching Assistants. 

The lectures were extremely engaging, and they would help in understanding the concepts. They were fast-paced, and thus at times, it could be hard to catch up with content being taught. The tutorial session would further contribute to consolidating the concepts, and at times a new concept would be introduced via the new questions that the professor would hand out.

There were no attendance criteria for the course, but the lectures were extremely important towards the understanding of concepts, and hence it is highly encouraged to attend them.

Labs: There were weekly labs usually associated with the content taught during that time. Please refer to the CS 293 course review.

Project: N/A

Assignments:

There were six ungraded assignments handed out to us. The assignments’ problems were generally tough and could take up from hours to days to go through the questions. Since the professor had kept the office hours as anytime from 9 am-5 pm on weekdays, with the only requirement being to drop a prior intimation mail if possible, a willing individual could clarify any doubts in the assignment.

Exams:

The following was the Grading structure:

The exams were generally on the tougher side, with the end-semester exam being the toughest in our year. The questions asked in these exams were always original. The professor would grade the answers leniently, with some credit being provided as long as the answer provided by the student would have a hint or a direction towards the correct solution.

Difficulty:

This is the toughest course a sophomore would go through, and as per previous reviews, it probably is the toughest course in the UG curriculum. It is easy to get intimidated by the problems discussed in class, the assignments, and the exams. 

Grading: 

Anyone getting above 50% would almost certainly get an AA or AB in the course. Since it is extremely tough to score ‘well’ in the exams, the grading statistics would seem to be harsher than other courses that took place in the third semester.

References: 

Introduction to Algorithms – Thomas H. Cormen, Charles E. Leiserson, Ronald L. Rivest, and Clifford Stein

Advanced courses: CS 218

Name – Samad Koita

Course Name – Data Structures and Algorithms

Course Code – CS 213

Academic Year – 2019-20

Semester – Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Ajit Diwan

Motivation: One of the most important core courses in the department. In addition to improving your problem-solving ability, it helps in developing patience and confidence to solve new and intimidating problems.
Knowledge of data structures and algorithms is also required for coding tests/interviews for internships.

Prerequisite: CS 101

Course-content: 

Lectures: 3 hours of lecture per week. Though engaging, the lectures are quite fast-paced. Many of the lectures are just spent thinking about and later discussing some particular problem to introduce some particular concept. Attendance is not compulsory, but paying attention in class will help in both labs and exams.

Assignments: No graded assignments, but around 6 tutorial sheets, which were later discussed during the lectures.

Exams: The grading scheme was as follows:

Exams are original, and it is unlikely you would have seen the problems anywhere before. Questions are often related to concepts covered in class and tutorial sheets. Even if you are unable to solve the problem completely, partial marks are awarded for thinking in the right direction. Few questions in the exam are generally impossible to do in the given timeframe, so it’s important not to get stuck anywhere and lose your confidence.

Difficulty: The most difficult course of second year. Majority of the students are unable to solve most questions in the tutorials and exams. The professor understands this, and gives credit for your approach to the problem, even if you are unable to solve it completely. It is common for people to feel intimidated and give up after a poor performance early on. However, if you put in consistent efforts, discuss the problems with friends, and take help whenever needed, you should end up with a decent grade.

Labs: See review of CS 293 (19-20)

Grading: Grade cutoffs are generally low. Scoring above 50% would almost certainly get you an AA or AB. However, relatively few good grades are awarded, due to the difficulty of the course. Very few FRs were awarded.

References: From the point of view of the course, it is helpful to look at past problem sets & question papers by the professor. However, to learn Data Structures and Algorithms in general, you can refer to the following:

reviews.body:
Name – Pratyush Agarwal

Course Name – Data Interpretation and Analysis

Course Code – CS215

Academic Year – 2019-20

Semester – Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Ajit Rajwade(Before MidSem), Prof. Suyash Awate(After MidSem)

Motivation: This course provides the basis for Machine Learning with many important concepts like singular value decomposition, principal component analysis, distributions which forms the backbone for further Machine Learning and Image processing courses.

Prerequisite:  Basics of probability, statistics(JEE stuff) and linear algebra(MA106)

Course-content: First part of the course taught by Rajwade Sir starts with basic probability and statistics, random variables , expectation values, some data interpretation techniques and other terms related to it. It then introduces some theorems and inequalities (Markov’s  inequality, Chebyshev’s inequality , central limit theorem) and the further part of the course till the midsems is based on analysing different distributions like gaussian, poisson, uniform etc and some basic parameter estimation techniques like MLE and its bias, variance and MSE.

The second part of the course by Awate Sir starts with Multivariate Gaussian, transformation of Random variables and a brief introduction of PCA(Principal Component Analysis) and Eigen Decomposition. It then picks up on MLE from before and goes further into parameter estimation focusing mostly on Bayesian Estimation, some related terms like Priors, Posteriors, Risk, Loss, Conjugate Priors and  ends with some related theorems like KL Divergence, Fisher Information.

Lectures: All the lectures are organised quite well. For the first part of the course , Rajwade Sir uses quite explanatory slides and the order and teaching way is very systematic and hence easy to follow. He encourages students to get their doubts clear and is very happy to help on moodle, mails or in office hours.

For the second part, Awate Sir doesn’t use slides and teaching is mostly like classroom teaching with him explaining the concepts on board, also the concepts are quite new and the teaching is at a good pace, so it is easy to lose track of the course if classes are missed or there is lack of attention. Though he provides notes which cover the material taught in class but that can’t make up for the classroom teaching. The concepts are taught in detail in class and he puts much effort on clearing all the doubts of the students, hence paying attention in his part is very important since his exams (quiz + endsem) are also quite conceptual and you’ll do good only if you have a thorough understanding.

Labs: (Teams info) There is no lab component

Project: There is no project

Assignments: There are about 4-5 assignments, 2-3 before midsems and 2-3 after midsems. The assignments follow the lectures and hence are a good way to get the concepts clear as well as to get to know the practical applications of the course content. The assignments are generally short with theory part as well as some coding part and they are of a good level. The assignments can be done alone or in groups of 2.

Exams: Apart from the assignments, there are 2 quizzes and midsems and endsems. The quiz1 happens 10-15 days before the midsems and quiz2 also around 10 days before the endsems. They serve as a wakeup call for the midsems and endsems respectively. The concepts of the first part are required for the later part of the course hence the quiz and endsem can be considered to be cumulative. 

The required formulas are provided in the exams and no mugging up is required, the exams are to test the understanding of the content.

Difficulty: The first part of the course is easier than the other one. The exams for the first part are also relatively easier. Attending the lectures regularly and going over through the slides in detail makes it easier to understand the concepts and perform well in quiz and midsem.

The second part of the course is not tough to understand if paid attention in class, but the exams are a bit challenging requiring a firm grasp on the concepts. Reading just the notes won’t be enough. Following classroom teaching, getting all doubts cleared and practising sample questions helps a lot.

Grading: Mid-sem exam: 25%. Final exam (cumulative): 25% . Programming and written assignments (about five): 35% – all to be done individually or in groups of 2 students. Two pre-announced quizzes: 15% .

Grading is fair with more than 20% people getting AA or AB and approximately 75% getting BC or above.

References: Introduction to Probability and Statistics for Engineers and Scientists

 Advanced courses: Digital Image Processing (CS 663). Medical Image Computing (CS736), Advanced Image Processing (CS754)

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Name –  Vidit Goel

Course Name – Data Analysis and Interpretation

Course Code – CS215

Academic Year – 2021-22

Semester –  Autumn 

Course Instructor – Suyash Awate

Motivation: This course is a firsthand introduction to data analytics and statistics. This course starts with a discussion on random variables, probability and goes on to give description of various distributions and why they are used in statistics. Further this course provides the basis for Machine Learning with many important concepts like singular value decomposition, principal component analysis, distributions which forms the backbone for further Machine Learning and Image processing courses.

Prerequisite:  Basics of probability, statistics(JEE stuff) and linear algebra(MA106)

Course-content: The course starts with basic probability and statistics, random variables , expectation values, some data interpretation techniques and other terms related to it. It then introduces some theorems and inequalities (Markov’s  inequality, Chebyshev’s inequality , central limit theorem). The further part of the course till the midsems is based on analyzing different distributions like gaussian, poisson, uniform etc and some basic parameter estimation techniques like MLE and its bias, variance and MSE. Post midsem we start with Multivariate Gaussian, transformation of Random variables and a brief introduction of PCA(Principal Component Analysis), SVD(Singular Value Decomposition) and Eigen Decomposition. It then picks up on MLE from before and goes further into parameter estimation focusing mostly on Bayesian Estimation, some related terms like Priors, Posteriors, Risk, Loss, Conjugate Priors and  ends with some related theorems like KL Divergence, Fisher Information.

Lectures: The lectures were conducted in a flipped-classroom mode where we were provided with recorded lectures(taught by Suyash sir) and the slides, and online discussions were held during lecture hours that primarily involved doubt-solving. The recorded lectures were self contained along with the slides to understand the course-content, but attending online doubt-solving sessions by sir helped understand the trivialities of the topics being discussed and hence used to be helpful. 

Labs: (Teams info) No labs for this course

Project: No Project

Assignments: 3 interesting assignments both programming based and theory based were given to us, 1 pre-midsem and 2 post-midsem. These are really extensions of what is taught in the class and help us understand the practical application of all the concepts taught to us. These assignments were particularly helpful throughout the course to visualize things better. The assignments are of good level and can be done individually or in a group of 2.

Exams: Apart from the assignments, there are 2 quizzes and midsems and endsems. The quiz1 happens 10-15 days before the midsems and quiz2 also around 10 days before the endsems. The concepts of the first part are required for the later part of the course hence the quiz and endsem can be considered to be cumulative. In general the level of exams is towards a bit more challenging side but with a proper foundation of course content all the questions can be solved easily. The questions asked in the exams are really interesting as they generally are application based and not just theory-based.

Difficulty:  4/5

Grading: 

References: Introduction to Probability and Statistics for Engineers and Scientists

Advanced courses: Digital Image Processing (CS 663). Medical Image Computing (CS736), Advanced Image Processing (CS754)

Name – Sushant Tarun

Course Name – Data Interpretation and Analysis

Course Code – CS215

Academic Year – 2017-18

Semester – Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Ajit Rajwade(Before MidSem), Prof. Suyash Awate(After MidSem)

Motivation: ML(Machine Learning) being one of the hottest topic in the CS field, this course provides the basic amenities needed to study it. It provides a outline on which all the further ML related courses are built upon.

Prerequisite: Basics of probability, statistics(JEE stuff) and linear algebra(MA106)

Course-content: First part of the course (by Rajwade Sir) starts with the basics of probability and statistics, further delving into random variables, its estimation, related terms and theorems and later studying some properties of common distributions like Uniform, Gaussian, Poisson, Bernoulli, Beta, Gamma, Exponential, Binomial with basics of Maximum Likelihood Estimation(MLE) and elements of an estimator like bias, MSE(Mean Squared Error).

Second part of the course (by Awate Sir) picks up on MLE from before and goes further into parameter estimation focusing mostly on Bayesian Estimation, some related terms like Priors, Posteriors, Risk, Loss, Conjugate Priors and  some related theorems like KL Divergence, Fisher Information, and further study transformation of Random variables, Multivariate Gaussian and ultimately get a brief introduction of PCA(Principal Component Analysis) and Eigen Decomposition.

Lectures: All the lectures are organised quite properly and in a smooth fashion with both professors encouraging doubts and explaining them as neatly as possible.

Some tutorials are organised in order to help in practicing questions. The tutorials are supposedly optional but it’s quite beneficial to attend them.

Assignments: There are 4 assignments (2 before MidSem and 2 after) totalling to 35% weightage. The assignments consist of either written questions or programming in MATLAB, both of which heavily depend upon the understanding of the lectures delivered by the profs.

Exams: There are two quizzes, MidSem and EndSem. The exams test mostly on your understanding and grasp on the concept of the course and very little on your memory. In the EndSem professor may allow an A4-sized cheat sheet.

15% Quizzes, 35% Assignments, 25% MidSem, 25% EndSem – probable weightage

Difficulty: The first part of the course is a bit easier than the other one. The exams for the first part are also relatively easier. Attending the lectures regularly makes it easier to understand and no need to mug up just before the exam.

The second part of the course is not tough to understand but the exams are a bit tough requiring a firm grasp on the concept. Practising questions definitely helps.

Grading: Grading is fair with more than 20% people getting AA or AB and approximately 75% getting BC or above.

References: Introduction to Probability and Statistics for Engineers and Scientists

Advanced courses: Any ML related course. CS725(Foundation of Machine Learning) is a one of the basic course of ML.

 

 

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Name – Arjun Kashettiwar

Course Name – Data Interpretation and Analysis

Course Code – CS-215

Academic Year – 2019-20

Semester – Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Ajit Rajwade (1st Half) and Prof. Suyash Awate (2nd Half)

Motivation: The course provides the base for all subsequent Machine Learning related courses. 

Prerequisite:  Linear Algebra (MA106)

Course-content:

The first half of the course introduces descriptive statistics, discrete and continuous probability distributions, random variables with expectation and variance, properties of important families of  distributions like Gaussian, Bernoulli, Uniform, Poisson, Exponential, Binomial etc., Parameter estimation, Regression, Probability density estimation.

The second part of the course starts with Transformation of Random Variables, then introduces Multivariate Gaussian Distribution and its properties, SVD, Eigen Decomposition and PCA. The course ends with Bayesian Estimation, with related terms like Priors, Posteriors, Risk, Loss, Conjugate Priors and some information theory topics like KL Divergence and Fisher Information.

Lectures: We had 2 1.5 hr lectures a week. Both professors are very clear with all the concepts and always welcome doubts from the students. There were also tutorial sessions (optional attendance), attending which helps in understanding the content taught better.

Project: There was no course project, either compulsory or optional.

Assignments: There were 5 assignments (3 before midsem and 2 after) totalling to 35% weightage. The assignments consist of theory questions and 1 or 2 programming (MATLAB) questions. The assignments are moderate in difficulty and are based on the content covered in the lectures. Teams of 2 were allowed for doing these.

Exams: There are 2 quizzes, 5 assignments along with the midsem and the endsem.

Weightage: 15% Quizzes, 35% Assignments, 25% Midsem, 25% Endsem 

Difficulty:

1st Half: This part is relatively easier, and paying attention to the lectures is sufficient to get a good understanding of the topics covered. The  quiz of the 1st half and the midsem are also relatively easy to score high in.

2nd Half: The exams were harder, with the questions being more open ended. Solving tutorials and practice questions given by the professor is very helpful for the exams. 

Grading: Grading is lenient, with around 35-40/135 students getting >=9 grade in the course. The full statistics can be seen on ASC.

References: 

1st Half: https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~ajitvr/CS215_Fall2019/ 

2nd Half: ttps://docs.google.com/document/d/1Ssf6hMO6i_RgYgUqCsZUABvZEa94X98frJ7JUykzqHY/edit?usp=sharing

title: Design and Analysis of Algorithms
description: Models of computation, algorithm analysis, time and space complexity, average and worst case analysis, lower bounds. reviews.body:
Course Name

CS 218: Design and Analysis of Algorithms (Minor)

Year for which the review is written 

2018 – 2019 Autumn

Professor who took the course

Prof. Sundar Vishwanathan

Motivation behind the course

As discussed in my review of CS 213: Data Structures and Algorithms, this course is a step up for those interested in learning how people came up with the algorithms in the first place. The motivation for this course is learning to come up with algorithms for any problem you may encounter and being able to optimize the running time by analysing the algorithm you have come up with.

Also, in the later-half of the course we see the data structures we have studied in CS 213 come up as natural necessities to the algorithms we finally design which is quite beautiful.

The below-mentioned topics are important as building blocks for studying further algorithm spaces as it helps in understanding them better. Also, the algorithms discussed are applicable in various other domains. We thus eventually realise the need to appreciate Algorithm design as a field in its own right.

Course Content

Pre-requisite

CS 213 – Data Structures and Algorithms

Feedback on lectures

The professor didn’t demand attendance but emphasized problem solving. The lectures were interesting and the professor would present a problem at the beginning of a class and would expect us to completely solve the problem so as to appreciate the principles. Classes could be a bit boring at times since you are usually required to think about the problem independently. Attending classes with enthusiasm and concentration helped quite a lot in understanding difficult concepts which the professor explained as if it were quite natural to approach the problem in the way he approached it.

Feedback on tutorials, assignments and exams

There were 2 algorithm design assignments throughout the course. Solving the assignments took a lot of time as they were quite difficult compared to examples discussed in the class. Problems in the exam required a thorough understanding of the concepts. The quizzes involved one problem which had been discussed in the class and two new problems. They were of moderate difficulty. The end semester exam involved 4 new problems and were towards the difficult side.

Evaluation Scheme was as follows:

Homework Assignments: 10%

Quizzes: Best 2 out of 3: 40 % (20 % for each quiz)

End semester exam: 50%

The Professor uploaded lectures on the website he had hosted which were more than enough if lectures were regularly attended.

A reference book: Algorithm Design by Kleinberg and Tardos is easily available online.

Reviewed By: Himanshu Pradeep Aswani (himanshuaswani123@gmail.com)

Link to the actual review

Instructor

Abhiram Ranade

Semester

Autumn ‘20

Course Difficulty

This course is moderately difficult, and requires consistent effort and motivation. This should be expected from a course doing a deep dive into algorithms.

Time Commitment Required

6-8 hours per week

Grading Policy and Statistics

AA 12
AB 16
BB 11
BC 14
CC 12
CD 13
DD 2
Total 80

Pre-requisites

CS101 level knowledge is a must. A little bit of Data Structures and Algorithms knowledge would be beneficial, but is not a prerequisite, as the course doesn’t assume much knowledge in this regard.

Evaluation Scheme

20 marks : Best 4 of 5 assignments, 5 per selected assignment
5 marks : Quiz
25 marks : Midsem
50 marks : Endsem

Topics Covered in the Course

Models of computation, algorithm analysis, time and space complexity, average and worst case analysis, lower bounds. Algorithm design techniques: divide and conquer, greedy, dynamic programming, amortization, randomization. Problem classes P, NP, PSPACE; reducibility, NP-hard and NP-complete problems. Approximation algorithms for some NP-hard problems.

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

Assignments are a bit time-consuming, but doable and easy to score in. It’s generally a list of exercises from the reference textbook, so though solutions are easy to find on the web, it is highly recommended to try on your own and give your best first, to help develop an algorithmic acumen.

Feedback on Exams

Quizzes were simple, and good conceptual understanding helps score easily in the quizzes.

Midsem and Endsem exams were moderately difficult, and a deep understanding of topics, and practice with various kind of questions is beneficial to come up with efficient algorithms on the spot.

Motivation for taking this course

You should take this course if you want to explore how to come up with efficient approaches to tackle seemingly complex problems, how some of the best algorithmic techniques work, famous algorithms used in production, and a decent depth of exposure into the various kinds of algorithms. The biggest takeaway from this course is the development of an algorithmic mindset.

How strongly would I recommend this course?

If the course motivation mentioned above resonates with you, this course is highly recommended. The professor is really good at teaching, and he also holds regular discussions, where he is extremely helpful and patient.
Though, keep in mind that courses like this have a lot of awesome alternative resources online to cover the same content if you only care about learning the topics, and not about also formally doing the course.

When to take this course?

5 semester.
This course should ideally be taken after CS213 - Data Structures and Algorithms.

References Used

Algorithms - Dasgupta, C. H. Papadimitriou, and U. V. Vazirani (http://algorithmics.lsi.upc.edu/docs/Dasgupta-Papadimitriou-Vazirani.pdf)

title: Computer Networks
description: Internet architecture and the layering abstraction. Application layer: network application architectures and examples. Socket programming. Transport layer: transport protocol design, analysis of TCP. Network layer: addressing, routing, forwarding, interdomain routing. Router design and scheduling. QoS and resource allocation. Traffic engineering, network address translation and other practical topics. Link layer: channel access, switching, VLANs, MPLS. PHY layer basics: framing, encoding, modulation.

Varsha Apte

Pre-Requisite

CS 101 for the coding assignment and some knowledge of Data Structures and Algorithms

Learning Outcome

Computer Networking typically involves a layered architecture where each layer is an abstraction of services to the layer above. The course content is fairly theoretical, explaining the current state of this layering and the protocols used.

Pre-Midsem Course Content

The history and architecture of the Internet include all 5 layers – application, transport, IP, link & physical, popular applications, congestion control protocols, routing and switching, and wireless networking, plus some recent advances in the field of computer networking.

–

Most of the class received B’s and C’s grades, so the grading was moderate.

Course Description:

This course is about the various layers of the internet architecture and the protocols that deal with them. It covers the following

Physical layer – physical transmission of data, encoding, various modulation techniques

Data link layer – deals with delivery of frames between 2 local devices. Includes media access control protocols (MAC), switching, error checking

Network layer – addressing, routing from one device to another via intermediate routers, IP etc

title: Digital Logic Design
description: Introduction : The nature of logic, Boolean Algebra and switching functions, Number Systems, binary, hexadecimal and other systems. Switching functions: Representation and properties of switching functions and their logic realizations using GATES and Switches. simplification of switching functions.Introduction to VHDL: Introduction to CAD for logic design, concepts of behavioral and structural description, Introduction to CAD tools, VHDL language concepts and statements. CAD of logic circuits through VHDL and FPGAs, CPLDs. Digital Systems Building blocks: Combinational logic elements, Decoders/Encoder, Multiplexors,De-multiplexors, Sequential logic elements, Flip-Flops, Registers, Shift Registers and Counters. Examples of applications. Optimal Design : Minterms and maxterms, canonical forms, cubes and subcubes, implicants and prime implicants, Minimization using K-map, Quine-McCluskey algorithm for finding prime implicants, selection of Prime implicants for a minimal expression using PI charts. Hazards in combinational circuits. Finite State machines(FSMs): Synchronous, asynchronous and pulse mode models,mealy and Moore machines. State diagrams, state tables, state assignment and synthesis using D,JK and T flip flops. State minimization for completely and incompletely specified machines. Primitive flow table, state minimization and reduced flow table. state assignment and Races in asynchronous circuits, Race free state assignments Error detection and correction: Errors in fixed length messages and their detection using parity checks. Correction through hamming codes. Errors in variable length messages and polynomial codes (cyclic codes) error detection properties of polynomials and burst error detection.Implementation using linear machines.

Instructor: Prof. Krishna S.

Session: 2019-2020

Prerequisites: N/A

Course content and structure:

This course was entirely a theory course with no programming assignments etc. (but an abundance of very interesting problems). The main topics covered were:

1. Introduction to Propositional Logic
2. Rules for Natural Deduction and Proof Engines
3. Soundness and Completeness of Propositional Logic
4. Normal Forms, Horn Algorithm and Resolution
5. Introduction to First Order Logic
6. First Order Logic for Languages
7. Deterministic Finite State Automata (DFA)
8. Nondeterministic Finite State Automata (NFA)
9. Monadic Second Order (MSO) Logic
10. MSO on Words
11. Transition Systems and Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)
12. Infinite Words and Buchi Automata (DBA and NBA)
13. LTL Model Checking
14. Generalized NBA and LTL Model Checking Complexity

Feedback on lectures: There was no mandatory attendance policy and it wasn’t even recorded. However, a lot of examples and explanations were on the board, so I imagine those who skipped classes would spend more time covering up for them in self-study. The professor’s teaching was easy to understand, and the material covered was quite interesting and intellectually stimulating. The lectures were, for the most part, evenly paced (though a little slow at the beginning). The professor encouraged doubts and, in my opinion, always understood and cleared them up quite effectively.

Feedback on tutorials and exams: I found the tutorial problems very intriguing and enjoyed solving them. We were given the problems beforehand and encouraged to solve them before the session, where they were discussed in detail. Doubts regarding the problems or otherwise were addressed on Piazza and the solutions to the unsolved problems were also posted here. The examinations were neither too easy nor too hard. A number of problems (especially in the end-semester exam) were quite original and not just derived from the tutorials. However, solving the tutorials did help a lot. The strictness of the evaluation was largely dependent on the TA allotted to the question.

Difficulty (on a scale of 1 being very easy to 5 being very hard): 3

Textbooks & References: The lecture slides and class notes were more than sufficient. However, the professor also recommended some textbooks for additional reading on some of the topics, pdfs of which were provided by her on Piazza.

Grading Statistics: 
AP 2
AA 8
AB 17
BB 36
BC 30
CC 15
CD 11
DD 7
FR 6

Additional comments & what you learnt from the course: Overall, I quite enjoyed the course and especially liked the formalization of the logic which we use nearly everywhere. If the absence of coding isn’t a deal-breaker for you and you enjoy solving logical problems, I would recommend that you try this course out.

Name: Missula Meghana

Course Name: Software Systems Lab

Course Code: CS251

Academic Year: 2019-20

Semester: Autumn

Course Instructor: Amitabha Sanyal

Motivation: You get to learn at least 8 new programming languages, libraries and modules including two major programming languages, Java and Python. 

Prerequisite:  None

Course-content: Bash, Awk, Sed, Python, SciPy, NumPy, Matplotlib, Pandas, Make, CMake, git, Java, Android, MySQL, HTML, CSS, Bootstrap, Javascript, Latex, Beamer, Doxygen, Tikz

Lectures: Weekly once lecture, 2 days before the lab to give an overview of what will be covered in that week.

Labs: There are 2 types of labs, Inlab and Outlab. Inlabs are to be done individually and the team size for Outlabs is 3. Inlab is supposed to be completed within the lab and it is graded. However, the difficulty of Inlabs is not much and can be completed by learning through different websites. Outlabs are also based on that week’s topic but are slightly more challenging than the Inlabs. Outlabs are also graded and you typically get a weekend deadline for finishing it. 

Project: (To be done along with the same team as of your Outlab) One project is to be submitted at the end of the course which has a significant weightage (In our case it was 15%) to your grade. You have to choose from a list of projects which have some basic features as well as some additional features to be implemented. The project mostly uses the tools/languages covered during the course. Additionally, you may need to learn new things for the project. There are mid-evaluations and final evaluations which include a working demo, presentation and viva.

Assignments: Outlabs are the only home-assignments you get in the course.

Exams: There are 4 quizzes of 3 hours duration each during which you have internet access to some specific tutorial websites, documentation, references shared by the instructor and StackOverflow.

Difficulty: Inlabs are quite easy with basic functions to be implemented. Outlabs and project being slightly on the difficult side, you have to put in efforts to finish them by searching across various websites and discussing with your teammates. Quizzes are lengthy and are difficult to attempt within the quiz duration. Here, you need to be fast in debugging and knowing some useful functions from the documentation can help you save the time of writing long chunks of code. Overall, the difficulty is hard and requires effort for a decently good grade especially since you learn a new topic every week.

Grading: In our case, the weightage for the various evaluation heads was as follows: Quizzes- 35%, Project-15%, Outlabs-40%, Inlabs-10%. The grading was lenient with a majority of the class getting 8+. 

References: Online tutorials like w3schools, tutorialspoint, StackOverflow, geeksforgeeks, StackExchange and documentations are very helpful throughout the course.

Advanced courses: None

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Reviewer’s Name: Adwait Godbole

Course Name: Software Systems Lab

Course Code: CS 251

Academic Year: 2017-18

Semester: Autumn Semester

Course Instructor: Prof. Kavi Arya

Motivation: This course is a fast paced tour of the current programming landscape. By the end of the course you would be familiar (if you work hard enough, proficient) with a range of tools and languages used by software developers today.

Prerequisite: None

Course-content: Vim, HTML/CSS, Bash Scripting, Makefiles, Java, Python, version control using GitHub, code documentation using Doxygen, Latex, GnuPlot (a plotting utility), JavaScript, PHP.

Lectures: One lab per week. One tutorial per topic was taken by Prof/TAs. An overview of the upcoming lab topic was giving during the tutorial including resources/reading material. No lectures were taken.

Assignments: The most important part of the course. Assignments were to be done in groups of 3. Each week had one Inlab assignment (to be completed on the day of lab) and one Outlab to be completed over the weekend following the lab.

Exams: Two quizzes were conducted along with a mid semester exam and a course project towards the end of the semester.

Project: Significant focus was given on the course project. Along with the working code a detailed exposition covering motivation for the project, method of approach, time plan and final goals was required. The project concluded with a viva and a presentation.

Difficulty: More than conceptual difficulty, completion of assignments and project work is time consuming and requires you to be resourceful. 5/5 for hard work.

Grading: All labs are graded but with weightage dependant on difficulty of lab. Not too hard to get 9 or 10 if consistent efforts are put in. Depending on performance in Viva/Written exams grades may vary between group members.

References/Resources: https://ryanstutorials.net, https://www.w3schools.com/, man pages (Linux Documentation), Stack Overflow for specific queries, Python Documentation,  https://try.github.io/, https://www.tutorialspoint.com/, Java APIs

Advanced courses: None. There are courses that deal with the general ideas of Programming Languages, Compilers and other systems aspects in greater detail.

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Reviewer’s Name : Riya Baviskar

Course Name : Software Systems Lab

Course Code : CS 251

Academic Year : 2018-19

Semester : Autumn

Course Instructor : Soumen Chakrabarti

Motivation: This course gives you a brief introduction to the programming languages and tools which you will need and find useful when you work on any real-world project.

Prerequisite:  None

Course-content: Bash scripting, Python, Pynetworking, Build tools (Make, Cmake), Git, HTML/CSS, Javascript, PHP, Latex, Beamer, Java, Android, Relational Databases, Tikz, Matplotlib, Spreadsheets

Lectures: There were no lectures. But there was a tutorial session in each week which gave an overview of the topics to be covered in the week. 

Labs:   Every Lab had two components: Inlab and Outlab. Each of them was supposed to be done in groups of 3 (You can form your own groups in the beginning which are to be continued till the end).

Inlab was to be done in the Software Lab itself in a duration of 3-4 hours. It was prepared in such a way that a newbie can get started by following that.

Outlab was a take home assignment based on the topics we had worked in the Inlab and usually you get the weekend to complete the Outlab.

Project: It was also to be done in groups and formed a major component of the course. We had to choose among the given set of problem statements, work on it and add extra features. The project involved working on the tools learned in the course and also exploring new things. There was a mid-evaluation in which we were supposed to present what is done so far and the next plan. Then finally the project concluded with presentation, demo and viva.

Exams: There were 4 programming quizzes of duration 3-4 hrs during our time and almost all of them had internet access (access was given to stackoverflow and almost all tutorial websites.)

Difficulty: As you have to learn new things every week and complete an assignment based on it, the course is one of the most difficult amongst the courses seen so far by a 2nd year undergraduate. Also it has an initial steep learning curve because you are not familiar with searching and reading documentations on your own. You have to put in a good amount of efforts to learn in this course.

Grading: Inlabs, Outlabs, Quizzes, Project are all graded. Grading was chill with almost 60% of the class getting  8 or above. Depending on performance in Viva/programming quizzes grades may vary between group members.

References: Online tutorials for most of the topics are available. Documentation as well is pretty precise and it’s always good to refer documentation for any query. Sites like stackoverflow help a lot for specific queries as others might have faced the same problem before.

Advanced courses: None. This is the basic course which gives a glimpse of all the programming languages you might require to use in your future courses.

title: Database and Information Systems
description: Overview of data management systems. Relational model and query languages (relational algebra and calculus, SQL). Database design using the ER Model, ER Diagrams, UML Class Diagrams. Relational database design and normalization. Integrity and Security. Design and development of Web based information systems. Overview of storage structures and indexing, query processing and optimization, and transaction processing. Introduction to Big Data management concepts such as: distributed and scalable data storage, including distributed file systems, key value stores, column stores and graph databases, replication and consistency, and concurrent data processing using the Map Reduce paradigm. Introduction to decision support and data analysis, data warehousing and data mining, and Information Retrieval.

reviews.body:
Name – Aditya Vavre

Course Name – Operating Systems

Course Code – CS347

Academic Year – 2019-2020

Semester – Autumn (5th Semester)

Course Instructor – Mythili Vutukuru

Motivation: A very interesting and practical course that deals with the functioning of operating systems since we usually deal with different operating systems whenever we use our computers. 

Prerequisite:  No prerequisite is required for this course.

Course-content: Overview of operating systems broken down into 4 parts: 

1. Processes 2. Memory 3. Concurrency 4. I/O and FileSystems. Also includes the functioning of the xv6 operating system in detail. 

Lectures: Attending the lectures is highly recommended because all the concepts are clearly explained in the class on the white board, and even though there are class notes and lectures available online, they do not have a very detailed explanation. 

Labs: (Teams info) All labs are individual. The details of the lab course can be found in the review for CS 333.

Project: There is no project related to this course, neither compulsory or optional.

Assignments: The course doesn’t have any assignments.

Exams: In our course offering, there were 2 quizzes contributing 15% each towards the final garde, a mid-semester exam contributing 30% towards the final grade and an end-semester exam contributing 40% towards the final grade. The end-semester exam included all the topics covered in the course with more weightage given to topics covered after mid-semester exam. 

Difficulty: The exams and quizzes were not very difficult if one has understood all the concepts well. In my opinion, attending all the lectures to understand all the concepts, practicing all the questions on the course web page, and going through the reference book for this course is more than enough to secure a good grade in this course. All the exams had a subjective/numerical section and a short answer/True or False section. The short answer questions were very specific and required a single clear answer, otherwise no marks were awarded. Mid-semester and end-semester exams were more subjective and had design questions as well. If this course is interesting to you and if you want to explore more in this area, you can take up CS 695 (Topics in Virtualization and Cloud Computing) which is a more advanced course in the area of Operating Systems. 

Grading: In our offering of the course, the grading was a little lenient. AA: 24, AB: 23, BB: 22. Usually scoring above 90% is guaranteed an AA. 

References: 

Advanced courses: CS 695: Topics in Virtualization and Cloud Computing.

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Name – Syamantak Kumar

Course Name – Operating Systems

Course Code – CS 347

Academic Year – 2018-19

Semester – Fall

Course Instructor – Purushottam Kulkarni

Motivation: Part of the core course curriculum and also pretty interesting since we are all commonly dealing with different operating systems without knowing what’s under the hood.

Prerequisite:  There is no hard prerequisite for the course.

Course-content: Overview of operating systems: batch processing, multi programming, time-sharing and real time systems. Concurrent processes: communication and synchronization. Process management, deadlocks. Main memory management: paging, segmentation, sharing of programs and data. Device management : file system, security.

Lectures:  During our offering, we had 3 one hour lecture slots on Monday, Tuesday and Thursday. Attending classes is highly suggested as professor uses board work and his way of teaching is very unique and following the classes makes exams very easy.

Labs:  All labs are individual. The details are covered in a separate review.

Project:  There is no project related to this course, neither compulsory or optional.

Assignments:  The course doesn’t have any assignments. Though there are lab assignments but as lab is a separate course hence no assignment for this course.

Exams:  During our offering, there were 4 quizzes contributing for 30% of total marks, mid semester contributes to 30% and end semester to 40%. The quizzes were almost for disjoint topics that were covered after the last quiz.

Difficulty: The quizzes and exams are very conceptual and based heavily on content/questions covered in class. I personally enjoyed the professor’s method of teaching, since he would first note down the topics to be covered in class and then open them up for discussion, writing down notes on the way. He would also share the compiled notes afterwards. The quizzes generally had 1-2 mark questions which were based on understanding of class material. True/False questions with reasons were the professor’s favourite questions. Mid semester and End semester exams had more subjective questions where you were required to either design or compute something. They were also based quite a lot on discussions done on class and attending classes would help a lot in understanding.

Grading: AA – 10, AB – 18, BB – 24  in a class of 133.  Grading is a little harsh however those attending the lectures get a good grade.

References:

Advanced courses:

Name – Devansh Shah

Course Name – Operating Systems

Course Code – CS347

Academic Year – 2017

Semester –  Autumn

Course Instructor –  Prof. Purushottam Kulkarni

Motivation:  Operating system is the software that interfaces between hardware and the desktop applications we use. It is interesting to learn about how such a complicated system is built using simple abstract ideas.

Prerequisite: None

Course-content:

The first half of the course talks about the services required from a operating system and how it abstracts out the user applications from the kernel via dual mode of execution. The course moves on to how an OS needs to efficiently manage its resources available – processor time and memory. The concepts of context switching for process scheduling and memory management via virtual memory were discussed in detail.

Post-midsem the course covered locking and synchronization in a shared resource environment. This becomes particularly important in multi-processor system where the same memory is used by all processors. The last part involved file systems and recovery mechanism in case of sudden shutdown.

Lectures: The professor used his notes to teach in class. During the first 15-20 minutes of every lecture he briefly revised the topic that was going on. The course content was exhaustive but weekly quizzes helped us stay in tune.

Assignments: The operating systems theory course is accompanied with a lab component. The labs involved working on the xv6 OS which is a much restricted OS but with all the core functionalities. We had to implement basic modules in xv6 in line with what was covered in the theory class.

There were 4 lab-quizzes which had a majority weightage in the lab grading. The in-labs were more for practise and getting accustomed to the xv6 OS.

Exams:

In class quizzes every alternate week – total of 30 marks .

Midsem – 30 marks

Endsem – 40 marks

Difficulty: The purpose of the weekly quizzes was to ensure students stay on par with what is covered. The quizzes were straight forward if one was regular in class. Midsem and Endsem papers were lengthy and involved knowing the theory well.

Grading: The grading was a bit on the tougher side – 8 students got AA and an equal number got AB.

References: Operating System Concepts by Silberschatz

Advanced courses:  Puru sir takes CS695 (Topics in virtualization and cloud computing) in the Spring semester.

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Session – 2018-19

Instructor – Mythili Vutukuru

Course Content –

1. Processes
2. Memory
3. Concurrency
4. I/O and File Systems

Prerequisite –
No hard prerequisites but extensive coding in C/C++ is required, so brush up your CS101

Feedback on the lectures –
The pace of the course is fairly quick and the instructor enforced a strict 80% attendance policy using the SAFE app. The instructor’s lectures are very interesting and the instructor manages to convey the topics in a very structured manner. While lecture notes as well as links to video lectures are provided, being engaged in the lectures is important. The lecture topics build on top of one-another and so.it is important to clear any doubts in the lectures themselves. In the interest of time, the instructor did not cover some minute details and students are expected to thoroughly read the book. Luckily, the book (OSTEP) is a very enjoyable and amusing read and I highly recommend it.

Feedback on Assignments/Tutorials/Exams –

There were 2 quizzes, a midsem and an endsem. The exams were on the tougher side, with questions on the minute details covered in the book, mixed in for good measure.
Also, there were 3 programming assignments. These are very useful and really help in understanding the material. The deadline for each is about 3 weeks after the announcement in class, though they are available on the course webpage before that. Teaching assistants do organize help sessions which is useful to sort out the various bugs (usually arise due to the use of various OS distributions). The instructor is really strict about plagiarism and copy-detection software is used on all submissions.

Difficulty (on a scale of 1-5 with 5 being very tough) –
4

Textbooks/References –
Operating Systems: Three Easy Pieces (OSTEP), Little Book Of Semaphores

Softwares used –
C/C++ coding, a bit of bash

Comments –

Courses on System Security, Multicore/Distributed systems would be better appreciated after this course.
Pro Tip: The instructor is really focused on ensuring the class understood the concepts. Poor performance by the class on a particular topic, usually results in it being tested again in the future.
P.S. The topics in the end (concurrency) are quite tricky and non-trivial. Pay special attention in class and be sure to clear any doubts.

Reviewed by Sarthak Consul

title: Game Theory and Algorithmic Mechanism Design
description: A tentative list of topics are as follows. • Non-cooperative game theory – foundation for discussing mechanism design ◦ Quantitative models of strategic interaction: rationality, intelligence, common knowledge ◦ Complete information simultaneous move games – normal form representation ▪ Ideas of equilibria: domination of strategies, Nash equilibrium ▪ Existence results for mixed and pure Nash equilibrium ▪ Complexity of computating Nash equilibrium ▪ Correlated equilibrium. ◦ Complete information sequential move games – extensive form representation ▪ Perfect and imperfect information extensive form games ▪ Equilibria concepts – subgame perfect equilibrium, perfect Bayesian equilibrium, analogies with pure and mixed Nash equilibrium ◦ Incomplete information games ▪ Bayesian games ▪ Equilibria concepts tied to the belief system ▪ Nash and Bayesian equilibria in incomplete information games • Introduction to mechanism design ◦ Incomplete information to player types ◦ Social welfare function, Arrow’s impossibility result ◦ Social choice function, Gibbard-Satterthwaite result ◦ Domain restriction ◦ Single-peaked preferences ◦ Task allocation domain ◦ Quasi-linear preferences • Some real world applications of mechanism design
