Click here to continue reading

Name – Rupesh

Course Name – Advanced Machine Learning

Course Code – CS 726

Academic Year – 2018-19

Semester – Spring

Course Instructor – Prof. Sunita Sarawagi 

Motivation: 

This is the most advanced level course in ML in the CSE department and completing all the basics of ML, this became the next course to do. Sunita ma’am is one of the top researchers as well in this domain and this class is also a good platform for discussions.

Prerequisite:  

An introductory machine learning course is needed. Also, prof tried to add an informal cutoff of a 7+ grade in the course because if you had performed badly in the previous course, this one is just more advanced and tough.

Course-content:

There are 2 parts of the course. Non-DL parts include 2 major Graphical models, Bayesian Network and Markov Random field. On these Graphical models, Representation, training, inference, and sampling all 4 were taught in detail. In the DL part, a major focus was on RNN as sequence models, AutoEncoders and GANs as Generative models, and Word-Embeddings as knowledge representation models. All the course content was latest and hence might even be updated in the next offerings. Also, some additional topics like the Gaussian Point process were added, and hence, there is the flexibility of the content if something interesting comes up.

Lectures:

Lectures are more like a discussion and prof explains the high-level view in the case of Deep Learning parts and assumes the basics to be clear. For Graphical models, everything was explained and clarified in detail like a normal lecture and didn’t have much to discuss.

Labs: (Teams info)

A team of 4 for a course project.

Project:

Something new is a big requirement for the course project. This can be building on an existing paper with its new use case or some extension. The time is ample and evaluation is divided into the mid evaluation and final evaluation. 

Assignments:

There wasn’t any coding assignment. There was 3-5 homework that had around a week to work on and was handwritten theory assignments on graphical models. The assignments were moderate with 1-2 hard questions left to ponder but mostly ensured that everyone was in sync with the course.

Exams:

Quizzes, Mid Sem, and End Sem. The early quiz was easy but post mid sem quiz was really tough. Similarly, mid sem was on a bit moderate side while the end term was filled with challenging questions. Apart from some questions that were mainly working out some formula taught in class, there were questions that were more situation based which asked us which algorithms and techniques should you pick and justify. 

Difficulty: 

The course overall was very vast and needed extra time to keep up with the pace of the class. Many students ended up either not attending the class, or just being silent and inactive in any discussion. Though the course being modular and new topics coming in, makes it easy to jump into any discussion but the extra effort is needed than a usual course load.

Grading: 

Though the paper was difficult in the latter half of the course, the course overall is split into lots of evaluations like exams, homework, and project. This made it moderate for an interested student to just be consistent and perform average in each evaluation to get good overall. Stats wise, 30% obtained a grade above 9 and around 40% got 7-8. That added to the fact that only the most interested and good performing students take this course, this is surely not the course to look for good grades.

References: 

Do visit the course website once for a detailed overview and reference.

https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~sunita/cs726/

Advanced courses:

EE-763: Science of Information, Statistics, and Learning

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Name – Siddhant Garg

Course Name – Advanced Machine Learning (AML)

Course Code – CS 726

Academic Year – 2017-18

Semester – Spring

Course Instructor – Prof. Sunita Sarawagi

Motivation: This course is perfectly suited for students interested in Machine Learning and having already undertaken the introductory course. This course covers a vast domain of topics including Deep Learning, several of which are recent and used in cutting edge ML research. This one of those rare CS courses where you will be asked to read research papers as a part of the theory and is an excellent option if you want to take up a BTP/RnD further in this domain.

Prerequisite:  Foundations of Machine Learning (CS725) or equivalent course from any other department is the required prerequisite and it will be quite difficult to take up this course without having prior knowledge of ML concepts.

Course-content: The course is split into 2 advanced topics of Machine Learning: Probabilistic Graphical Models, which is taught before midsem, and Deep Learning, which is covered post midsem. Graphical Models part of the course covers Bayesian Nets, Undirected graphical Models, Variable Elimination, Junction Trees, Approximate Inference, learning model parameters, etc. This part of the course is quite well structured and is in sync with the reference book(Probabilistic Graphical Models: Principles and Techniques, by Daphne Koller and Nir Friedman). The Deep Learning portion of the course starts by covering Sequence Prediction Models(Encoder-Decoder model) and Generative Models(VAEs and GANs). Several recent research papers and ideas of Deep Learning which were presented in top ML conferences of the recent years (SPENs, max-margin training, etc) are also discussed and thus this part of the course is very up-to-date with latest developments in Deep Learning.

Lectures: The professor is enthusiastic in teaching the course and clarifies the doubts of the students. Since the content of the course is so vast, most of the times the theoretical proofs/ideas were skipped in class and were asked to be self-read. This would lead to some concepts not being completely understood.

Assignments and Project: The first half of the course involves 3-4 hand written theory assignments(weightage ~ 12-15%) based on concepts of Graphical Models covered in class. The assignments are easy-to-moderate in difficulty and are well synced with the course content to ensure that the student is regular with the classes. The course project(weightage ~ 10%) offers students an option to work on any interesting topic pertaining to graphical models or deep learning and gives an opportunity to work on new ideas.

Exams: 2 quizzes(one of which was basic introductory), Midsem and Endsem are conducted which covers the major chunk of the course weightage. The Midsem and Endsem exams were difficult with applications of several concepts from theory and some research papers and also involved some open-ended problems.

Difficulty: I would rate this course as one of the slightly difficult advanced courses offered by the CS Department because of its vast theory content, reading research papers and application based exams.

Grading: Grading for the course is difficult with only a small percentage of people scoring top grades out of a very big batch who took the course.

References:

1) Probabilistic Graphical Models: Principles and Techniques, by Daphne Koller and Nir Friedman     

2) Deep Learning by Ian Goodfellow, Yoshua Bengio and Aaron Courville

 

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

title: Formal Models for Concurrent and Asynchronous Systems
description: 1.Petri nets – Modeling and analyzing asynchronous systems using Petri nets. Decision problems related to reachability, coverability, etc. for different variants of Petri nets. Other verification related issues. Tools, implementations and case-studies. 2. Asynchronous automata and Zielonka`s theorem. - partial orders, traces, diamond closure, optimal constructions. 3. Message passing automata and message sequence charts – Models for scenario based modeling of concurrent and message passing systems. 4. Lossy channel systems and generalization to the theory of well-structured transition systems. Properties of well-quasi orders, well-structured transition systems and decidability issues. Relation to Petri nets and other examples.

Click here to continue reading

Name – Kumar Saurav

Course Name – Formal Methods for Concurrent and Asynchronous Systems

Course Code – CS 735

Academic Year – 2019-20

Semester – 7

Course Instructor – Prof. Akshay S.

Motivation: I was interested in following up on the automata theory course and seeing what different models existed apart from the ones we were taught in automata. Apart from this, I was also interested in taking a theory course in CSE and in seeing how teaching in a small class happens (we were only 6-7 people in the course)

The course outline was quite explicit as well: https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~akshayss/courses/cs735/cs735-2019.pdf 

Prerequisite:  CS 310 (Automata Theory)

Course-content: Petri nets, Elementary net systems, P/T nets, Reachability graph, Dynamics of nets (concurrency, conflict, causality, contact, confusion), Confusion free subclass of nets (S-systems, T-systems, simple and extended free-choice nets), behavioral theory of ENS, firing sequences, traces, distributed automata models, message passing automata, lossy channel machines

Lectures: Standard 6 credit course structure. We had 1.5 hour classes for 2 days a week and there were no extra classes apart from a few which happened because the professor was out of town. The class strength was very small (6-7) and the professor welcomed any questions from the students. The intent was always to make the class as lively and engaging as possible. Communications outside classes were through Piazza and it was quite active.

Labs: (Teams info): None

Project: Paper presentation at the end of the course. A team of 2 was created and each team had to present their understanding of a research paper to the class and the professor. The professor helped in picking the research paper, but the students were always welcome to choose their topics independently if they wanted to, provided the material was relevant to the course. The professor also discussed with each team what all they were going to present, so that no team was being under or over-ambitious with their presentations.

Assignments: Maximum 5-6 questions to solve in each assignment. Though the assignments weren’t regular and were more for encouraging us to study outside of classroom hours rather than to grade us. We had 3 assignments in total and all were based on the stuff taught in the class. There were no strict deadlines to submit the assignments and we often had 3-4 classes to turn it in.

Exams: 2 quizzes, 1 midsem + 1 endsem (35%), 1 paper presentation (25%)

Difficulty: It’s not such a difficult course if you understand Automata theory (you don’t necessarily have to score a high grade in Automata to show that you understand it). As long as you keep pace in the class and clear doubts, it’s manageable enough. The course contents might be considered advanced, but the professor manages the pace according to the class, so there’s not a whole lot covered in a single lecture. Instead each topic is slowly and carefully done.

Grading: CS 735

References: Mainly professor’s own notes. These were additional materials he referenced from:

The course website is : https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~akshayss/courses/cs735/cs735-2019.html

Advanced courses: This itself is an advanced course. Related advanced courses include CS 615, CS 617

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

title: Medical Image Computing
description: The topics covered will include the following: 1. Introduction to imaging modalities, mathematical imaging models, noise and artefact models, sampling, signal modelling and fitting X ray, computed tomography (CT), positron 302225 emission tomography (PET), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (including diffusion MRI, functional MRI), ultrasound, microscopy 2. Visualization Methods: sectioning, multimodal images, 302225 overlays, rendering surfaces and volumes, using glyphs Application domains: 3D imaging, PET-CT 302225 imaging, diffusion tensor imaging 3. Image reconstruction Methods: image models, sampling, problem 302225 formulations, algorithms Application domains: MRI, CT 302225 4. Image denoising Methods: Bayesian estimation, nonlinear 302225 smoothing Application domains: MRI, CT, others 302225 5. Image segmentation Methods: clustering, Bayesian estimation, 302225 graph partitioning, classification Application domains: brain, heart, knee, 302225 thorax, abdomen; MRI, CT, ultrasound; cancer imaging 6. Anatomical shape analysis Methods: descriptors, learning shape models, 302225 hypothesis testing Application domains: brain, others 302225 7. Image registration Methods: similarity, transformation 302225 Applications: anatomical atlas, co- 302225 registration, motion correction 8. Content based medical-image retrieval Methods: image descriptors, image similarity 302225 Applications 302225The implementation-based experiments will rely on C/C++or Matlab environments. As part of the implementation-based experiments the students will be introduced tosoftware tools for medical image processing; examplesinclude popular open-source cross-platform softwarepackages for medical image analysis like the InsightToolkit (www.itk.org), Visualization Toolkit (www.vtk.org),etc., or other tools built using these packages. The coursewill use simulated and clinical medical image datasetsavailable freely through the Internet from universities orresearch institutions worldwide.

reviews.body:
Name: Tarush Goyal

Course Name: Medical Image Computing

Course Code: CS 736

Academic Year: 2019-20

Semester: Spring

Course Instructor: Prof. Suyash Awate

Motivation: Machine learning related ideas and strategies, more emphasis on ideas than results, no restrictions on course project

Prerequisite: CS 663 / good grade (>=8) in CS 215 (recommended)

Course-content: Following topics were covered in detail (maybe lesser than usual due to covid-19):

Lectures: 3 hours / week (generally 2 lectures). Awate Sir’s lectures can be summarised as “ heavy discussion on ideas / motivation, awkward jokes and a quick glance through use cases “. A lot of content discussed in class is not there in slides. So it is important to attend lectures though he does not care about attendance. He regularly asks for doubts in class and welcomes alternate ideas and “why” queries. However, the lectures are quite fast in general. It is important to keep up or you can be lost.

Labs: None

Project: Course Project is announced before the mid-sem and has to be completed around end-sem. There are no restrictions on the topic, coding language, softwares or data used. Previous year project topics are provided for reference. Suyash sir can provide some hints or suggestions but major portion of the project has to be done by the students themselves. Marking depends a lot on your presentation of the project.

Assignments: 4 assignments are given (3 our time). Generally assignments focus on one topic (see Course Content) and are very good for understanding. They mostly involve tuning parameters manually or locating points by hand which make them time confusing. About two weeks are given per assignment and the deadlines are not very strict. 

Exams: There are two exams : Mid-sem and End-sem. Just midsem was conducted in 2020 due to covid-19. The exams are pretty different from those in most other courses. Many questions are based on modifying a known scenario and demanding an improvisation over existing methods (highly conceptual rather than factual). A lot of questions also revolve around finding the correct approximation. However the marking is pretty lenient.

Difficulty: Assignments are on the tougher side because they involve a lot of simultaneous learning. Project can be easily completed with a timely start. Exams and sample papers might seem frightening but presenting a correct idea with explanation is much more important than the final result.

Grading: In our case, the weightages were pretty different from usual. Generally the project is 15%, assignments 35% and exams 50%. Otherwise the grading was moderately stringent with ‘AB’ being the mode grade.

References: The classes, slides and assignments are pretty comprehensive. Still, Suyash sir provides references for books before beginning each topic.

Advanced courses: None

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Name – Sheshansh Agrawal

Course Name – Medical Image Computing

Course Code – CS736

Academic Year – 2017-18

Semester – 2

Course Instructor – Prof. Suyash Awate

Motivation: CS663 interested me into the classical methods used in image processing. MIC is an extension of CS663 into a particular domain of medical images.

Prerequisite:  CS 663 / Instructor Consent

Course-content: MIC offers a bit more than just image processing as it needs a bit of domain information and covers some of the challenges in data collection, solutions and their impacts on the final 2D/3D image. The course content on course webpage is exhaustive https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~suyash/teaching/medical_image_computing.html. Though the content covered may be a subset of this.

Lectures: Lectures were interesting and faster paced as compared to CS663 in which Suyash Sir goes slow (maybe because of the smaller class strength in MIC).

Assignments: There were 4 assignments in our offering. Almost all the concepts, algorithms taught are covered in assignments as well which help a lot in understanding them. They are time consuming and moderately difficult.

Exams: Though the exams were never memory based, we were allowed to carry a limited number of written formulas in the exams. Also, most of the questions are based on a case studies, designing/ discussing the solution rather than finding the exact numerical answer. So,

Difficulty: Medium

Grading: lenient

Advanced courses: None

Motivation –

The course deals with basic algorithms used in medical image processing. This is a really good course for those interested in hard core mathematics and optimization algorithms in action.

Course Content –

The topics covered in this course are as follows –

Feedback on Lectures :

The instructor taught with the help of slides, occasionally using the board to derive or explain an algorithm. Paying attention in class is highly advised since it makes preparation for exams easier. The instructor readily solved the doubts and was jovial while teaching. Classes were held in evening slots, 2 days a week for 1.5 hours each.

Feedback on Assignments and Exams :

There were a total of 5 assignments given based on the implementation of the material taught in class. Assignments demanded considerable amount of time but they also helped in understanding of the material taught in class which made exams seem easy.   

There was an end semester exam, a mid semester exam and a quiz. The exams were closed book (cheat sheets allowed as per the instructor’s will), based on the material covered in the class. They were moderate in difficulty level, and mostly based on the material discussed in class. Easy to score if basic concepts of each topic is understood, not much rote learning required.

Study References –

The content uploaded on the moodle was more than enough.

Weightage and Grading:

Assignments – 35%

Exams – 50% (individual weightages not known)

Course Project – 15%

Grading difficult or chill is personal opinion. I found it moderate. Check for yourself

Parth Kothari

parthkothari811@gmail.com

Name – Ansh Khurana

Course Name – Medical Image Computing

Course Code – CS 736

Academic Year – 2018-19

Semester – 4

Course Instructor – Prof. Suyash P. Awate

Motivation: I thoroughly enjoyed CS 215 and wished to continue taking courses on image processing and computer vision. Prof. Suyash allowed enthusiastic second-year students to take the course and I went for it for the experience. 

Prerequisite:  CS 663 / Instructor Consent

Course-content: The course focuses on image processing algorithms applied to medical image datasets and which are useful for medical modalities. Most of the algorithms discussed are generic and can be applied outside the medical domain as well. Further, some techniques to obtain medical image data will also be discussed. Specifics about the course content can be found on https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~suyash/teaching/medical_image_computing.html. The course does involve some mathematical derivations and concepts which require a good understanding of Bayesian probability. 

Lectures: 2 x 1.5 hours per week. The lectures are important for understanding the course. The lectures are usually fast-paced but the professor resolves doubts raised by the students very well. Prof. Suyash takes attendance during the lectures. 

Labs: (Teams info): Teams of 2-3 for assignment and project.

Project: Course Project, roughly 10 per cent weightage.

Assignments: 3-4 assignments based on concepts taught in lectures. The assignments help in improving the understanding of the course and sometimes similar questions are asked in the examinations. The assignments are moderate in difficulty.  

Exams: 2 Quizzes, Midsem and Endsem examinations. The quizzes are directly based on the lectures and are there for keeping in touch with the course contents. Midsem and Endsem questions are based on the understanding of the concepts and would be more analytical / application based.

Difficulty: Moderate. 

Grading: Lenient. 

References: NA

Advanced courses: NA

title: Advanced Network Security and Cryptography
description: Math background for cryptography, Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC), optimization techniques for ECC. Side channel attacks on AES and DSA. Attacks on factorization and the discrete logarithm. SSL vs. IPSec. WLAN and cellphone security protocols. FMS attack on WEP. Web security – advanced attacks and defences. Botnets – design and detection. Cloud security issues – data auditing, homomorphic encryption, etc. Electronic payment and currencies (ex. Bitcoins).

reviews.body:
Name: Suraj

Course Name: Advanced Network Security and Cryptography

Course Code: CS741

Academic Year: 2018-19

Semester: Spring

Course Instructor: Prof. Bernard Menezes

Motivation: Interest to explore the field of Cryptography & Security, especially how cryptographic attacks work.

Prerequisite: Discrete Structures(CS207), Computer Architecture(CS305),  Cryptography and Network Security-I(CS649) or equivalent course

These are not hard-prerequisites(Many of the students including me completed only CS207 and the professor allowed us).

Course-content: Elliptic Curve Cryptography(ECC), El-Gamal encryption and signatures, ECC Optimization(NAF, WNAF & Projective coordinates), EC over Binary Fields, Side-channel attacks(AES & DSA), Spectre attacks, Intel SGX, Linear Cryptanalysis, WEP and RC4 attacks, Pollard-rho attack, Homomorphic Encryption, Learning With Errors(LWE).

Lectures: 2 lectures per week(1.5 hrs each). The lectures are interesting but may require revision(or extra reading) to catch up. The instructor is enthusiastic, interested in students’ learning, and asks for inputs in the lectures.

Labs(Teams info): No labs. Only programming assignment(s) and a project. Team of 4 students(choose your partner yourself).

Project: Major course project with a demo, viva, report document, and presentation. Most of the focus on the presentation and the demo. The project is generally an implementation of a research paper presented at one of the top Security and Cryptography conferences. The attendance during the project vivas(for all the projects) is compulsory.

Assignments: 1 small programming assignment. Team of 4 students.

Exams: 3 exams(2 quizzes and 1 end-sem, No mid-sem). The quizzes are easy and directly based on the concepts covered in the class and the assignment(s). The end-sem has new and interesting questions, and thoroughly tests the fundamental concepts. The end-sem also involves questions related to the projects presented by the students.

Difficulty: The quizzes and end-sem test the concepts learned in the lectures with interesting applications. Understanding the concepts covered in the lectures is very crucial for this course. The project depends on the students, but is of moderate load and should be discussed with the professor before the final decision. The professor also discussed some of his research papers during the course. Overall the course is on the higher side of moderate difficulty and workload.

Grading: The correction of the exams is fair and not very strict. The approximate weightage is as follows:

References: Network Security and Cryptography – B. Menezes

Advanced courses: N/A

title: Foundations of Network Security and Cryptography
description: Vulnerabilities, risks, attacks, defences, forensics. Examples and applications of Public Key Cryptography and Secret Key Cryptography. Crypto hash, digital signatures, digital certificates and PKI. Basic Authentication and Key Agreement protocols, SSL/TLS and Kerberos. Vulnerabilities and attacks on TCP, DNS, ARP, etc. and DDoS attacks. Network/host intelligence gathering and reconnaissance methods. Web security – SQL injection, XSS, etc. Software security and buffer overflow. Malware types and case studies. Access Control, firewalls and host/network intrusion detection.

reviews.body:
Name – Kartik Singhal

Course Name – Foundations of Network Security and Cryptography

Course Code – CS 742

Academic Year – 2017-18

Semester – Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Kameswari Chebrolu

Motivation:

The importance of network security is increasing, due to the growing threat of hackers trying to infect as many computers possible. This is a course which deals with understanding the exploits and how to defend/avoid them along with some standard protocols which give you enough knowledge to deep further in the area of network security.

Prerequisite:  

This is more practical course rather than the theoretical heavy CS 406. So you don’t need that much mathematical background for this course.

Networks course (CS224) is a hard prerequisite.

Course-content:

First half of the course, primarily deals with cryptography diving into ciphers, crypto-analysis, one time pads, symmetric & asymmetric key encryption, block modes, hashes, MAC and digital signature.

Second half of the course deals with network security. It covered different types of attacks with their prevention, firewall, malware, os & systems security, network protocols and web security.

Lectures: The professor is very clear with all the concepts and always welcome doubts from the students. She tries to clear them in the best possible way.

Assignments: There were 2 labs given as home assignment which had a written test related to the method & observation of the assignment. Each one had 10% weightage. The assignment were interesting and verifies your theoretical understanding.

Exams: There were total of 6 quizzes on SAFE app along with midsem and endsem. Handwritten notes were allowed in midsem and endsem so you don’t need to mug up stuff. Though the exams were based on practicality and application of the concepts taught.

10% Safe Quizzes

20% Assignments

30% Midsem

40% Endsem

Difficulty: The course overall was easy compared to other electives that I have done. If you pay attention in the class, you don’t have to pay much time out of the class.

Grading: Grading is not very tough. Around 20% people got AA’s or AB’s

References: Network Security and Cryptography by Bernard Menezes

Advanced courses: CS 741 – Advanced Network Security and Cryptography which is usually taken by Prof. Bernard in the spring semester

 

 

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Name – Kushagra Juneja

Course Name – Foundations of network security and cryptography

Course Code – CS 742

Academic Year – 2019-20

Semester –  Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Kameswari Chebrolu


Click here to continue reading

Name – Kushagra Juneja

Course Name – Foundations of network security and cryptography

Course Code – CS 742

Academic Year – 2019-20

Semester –  Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Kameswari Chebrolu

Motivation: With the increasing concern against various security loopholes and numerous cyber crimes, it is important for any professional developer and CS student to learn about these threats, security loopholes and various protocols already in place to keep these in check.

Prerequisite:  CS 224 (Computer Networks) 

Course-content: First half of the course deals with cryptography. This includes ciphers, symmetric and asymmetric key encryption and decryption, digital signature, hashes and MAC. The course is not very theoretical so not much mathematical background is required to understand the concepts.

After the midsem, the course covers network security. This part requires knowledge about the contents of CS 224 course. However, the professor also gives a recap of the required course contents in the videos. This part includes various attacks and protocols designed to prevent those attacks at different levels of the protocol stack. This also includes the concepts of firewall and web security including SQL Injection, XSS etc. However, don’t expect to become a security expert or a hacker by following this course, it just provides the basics.

Lectures: The course follows a Flipped classroom model. This means that the instructor used to upload videos explaining the concepts on BodhiTree and we were expected to go through them before coming to the lecture. In the first 30 mins of the lecture, the instructor would provide a quick recap of the concepts discussed in the videos and also clear any doubts that the students might have. The instructor is quite knowledgeable as well as forthcoming in clearing doubts. This would be followed by a quick 10 mins SAFE quiz. After that it was time for a hands-on session (lab) where we were given time in the class to solve some practical problems related to the theory covered in the videos. This was quite fun and interesting. Since we were expected to give time in watching the videos as well, the instructor took care not to exceed the expected workload limit considering a 6 credit course by only calling us for classes once a week for 1.5 hours. 

Labs: (Teams info) The in-class labs were not graded, so we were given freedom to do them alone or in teams of two. There were two lab exams of weightage 10% each which were individual. They were quite relevant to the concepts covered in class and in in-class labs. They were quite simple if you followed the course contents well.

Project: No project

Assignments: No assignments

Exams: All the exams(except SAFE quizzes) are open notes. The instructor focuses on understanding the concepts and not memorizing them, so handwritten notes are allowed in all exams, though printouts of slides are not allowed. The questions are also designed in a way that they aren’t picked up directly from the slides and require some understanding of the concepts to solve. Most of the questions in the exams were straightforward once you followed the contents of the course but some were designed to be tricky. 

The weightage of the exams were :- 10% to in-class SAFE quizzes, 10% each to two lab exams, 30% midsem and 40% endsem.

Difficulty: The course overall was easier than other electives I have done. Watching the videos on a regular basis and paying attention in the class is enough to get a decent grade.

Grading: This year, the grading was a bit tough, roughly 10% students got AA or AB.

References: No particular book was referred or followed throughout the course, but for the interested reader, you can refer to “Network Security and Cryptography by Bernard Menezes”

Advanced courses: CS 741: Advanced Network Security and Cryptography by Prof. Bernard Menezes

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

title: Foundations of Intelligent and Learning Agents
description: (1) Agency, intelligence, and learning(2) Exploration and multi-armed bandits(3) Markov Decision Problems and planning(4) Reinforcement learning(5) Search(6) Multi-agent systems and multi-agent learning(7) Case studies

reviews.body:
Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Name – Siddhant Garg

Course Name – Foundations of Intelligent and Learning Agents (FILA)

Course Code – CS 747

Academic Year – 2017-18

Semester – Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Shivaram Kalyanakrishnan


Click here to continue reading

One of the best courses in CSE according to me. This course is quite chill in terms of workload yet allowing you to learn and explore a lot more so that interested folks need not feel bored. SIr posts a lot of reading material and research papers which arent exactly part of the course yet good reading stuff for the interested. The subject is really interesting especially for the AI/ML intersted guys. This course does have programming component which wont be trivial but manageable to do even for new learners if spent adequate time. Grading is really chill which is one of the good part of the course yet making sure you learn the important concepts since one builds up on the other. Its adequate to spend 2-2.5 hrs per week if you are new to the subject excluding programming assignments. Look into the web-page for more information https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~shivaram/teaching/old/cs747-a2020/index.html.

Instructor

Shivaram Kalyanakrishnan

Semester

Autumn ‘20

Course Difficulty

The course is not very difficult, and the lectures are very good and enough to score well in the course. The recommended textbook and readings are also relevant, and give a deep understanding of the topics.

Time Commitment Required

5-7 hours per week

Grading Policy and Statistics

AA 103
AB 33
AU 1
BB 25
BC 24
CC 13
CD 6
DD 2
FR 6
Total 213

Pre-requisites

The course is open to all Ph.D. students, all masters students, and undergraduate/dual-degree students in their third (or higher) year of study.
Understanding of basic probability theory is expected. Can also be picked up towards the beginning of the course.
Basic familiarity with coding in Python is required, as the assignments require you to code in Python.

Evaluation Scheme

Weekly Quizzes of 2 marks each (capped to 20 marks)
4 coding assignments (10 marks each)
Endsem (40 marks)

Topics Covered in the Course

(1) Agency, intelligence, and learning (2) Exploration and multi-armed bandits (3) Markov Decision Problems and planning (4) Reinforcement learning (5) Search (6) Multi-agent systems and multi-agent learning (7) Case studies

Teaching Style

The lectures are very clear, and well paced, with the right amount of concept depth explored in lectures. The supplementary reading material is nicely curated, and extremely relevant in exploring the topics further to get a good understanding. The professor also holds regular discussion / doubt sessions. The weekly quizzes and assignment are very nicely designed, and greatly help the learning process.

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

Programming Assignments are very intellectually stimulating, and give a deep understanding of the theory. They are time consuming though, required as much as 5-10 hours of effort, but they are definitely worth it.

Feedback on Exams

The weekly quizzes are conceptual, and based on only the lecture content of the current week. They are easy to score in, and help keep up with the course content.
The Endsem was a take-home exam for us, with very stimulating questions, and was on an overall level moderately difficult. Though attempting this paper was a lot of fun.

Course Importance

This course is important if you want to explore the field of reinforcement learning, as it is the perfect introduction to the field. RL is a very up and coming field of AI, with a lot of research going on, and applications in robotics, trading, banking, health care, manufacturing, agriculture, etc.

How strongly would I recommend this course?

If you are even moderately intrigued / amazed by the field of RL (a must for taking the course), you SHOULD do this course.

When to take this course?

I took this course in my 5th semester. This would be the ideal semester for taking this course, as you can’t take this course before this, and taking it as early as you can allows to enough time to explore the field further before you graduate.

Going Forward

After finishing this course, you can take up the project-based advanced CS748 course by the same professor in the following semester, explore RL in fields like Robotics, Finance, Health Care, Manufacturing, etc., explore research under a professor in the form of a BTP or RnD, take up self projects in RL, or apply for university interns.

References Used

Reinforcement Learning - An Introduction (Richard S. Sutton and Andrew G. Barto) (https://web.stanford.edu/class/psych209/Readings/SuttonBartoIPRLBook2ndEd.pdf)

All content from the Autumn ‘20 iteration from CS747 (https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~shivaram/teaching/old/cs747-a2020/index.html)

Other Remarks

Take the ethical code of conduct very very seriously in this course, as the professor is intolerant of any breaches, whatever may be the cause.

CS 747 Review By: Shubham Lohiya

We thank the Mechanical DAMP team for this review

Name – Siddhant Garg

Course Name – Foundations of Intelligent and Learning Agents (FILA)

Course Code – CS 747

Academic Year – 2017-18

Semester – Autumn

Course Instructor – Prof. Shivaram Kalyanakrishnan

Motivation: This is one of the few advanced AI/ML courses that the CS Department offers and it covers the domain of Reinforcement Learning(RL). RL is a framework which has a long list of applications both practical as well as in research. RL features in several of the latest ML research projects with new fields like Deep-RL on the rise. This course can also be a good preparation if you plan to take up a BTP/RnD in this field.  

Prerequisite:  Though the course does not have any hard prerequisites, the instructor only allows final year B.Tech. students to register for the course. It will be an added advantage if you have undertaken the CS 344: Artificial Intelligence course since you will be able to follow the course contents more easily. Its okay if you haven’t done a Machine Learning course before doing FILA since the concepts taught here are somewhat orthogonal to that.

Course-content: The course provides an excellent overview of several concepts of Reinforcement Learning(RL) starting with Multi-armed Bandits, MDP Planning, Monte Carlo policy evaluation, Temporal Difference(TD) learning, Q-Learning, Sarsa, Tile coding, Policy gradients, Multi-agent learning, Inverse RL, Reward shaping, etc. The course contents are directly matched with those provided in the reference book (Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction by Sutton & Barto) and this makes it easier to follow and understand the course.

Lectures: The professor is quite enthusiastic in teaching this subject. Most of the teaching is on the board with slides only being provided for some select topics. The teaching pace of the lectures is a bit slow in my opinion, with the professor repeating the concepts many times over, which may make the lectures boring at times if you have already understood the concepts. The professor ably answered all the doubts and always entertained new ideas/queries on the topics being taught.

Assignments: There were 4 programming assignments(10% each) and one project(15%) which takes a big chunk of the course weightage away from theory exams. The programming assignments, which were to be done in Python or C++ as per your preference, involved implementing the algorithms taught in class. The assignments were quite easy and did not require a lot of time. The professor insisted on the course project having some new idea/proof/concept/technique as compared to projects of other courses where you end up working on an already explored idea, and doing this can be interesting if you like the domain of RL.

Exams: There were only 2 exams which were conducted: Midsem and Endsem and both were quite well designed and appropriately linked with the course content, having most of the questions based on application of concepts. The overall difficulty of the exams was easy-to-moderate and one can easily score well if you have read the theory from the book.

Difficulty: Overall difficulty of the course is easy-to-moderate as by reading the book and attending the lectures, one can easily follow the content being covered and score well in the assignments and exams.

Grading: Grading for the course is moderate but it is tougher to score top grades since the professor expects very high scores for these.

References: Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction by Richard S. Sutton and Andrew G. Barto

Advanced courses: This is the only advanced Reinforcement Learning course that the CS Department offers and is a good option to opt for if your are interested in AI/ML and want to explore Reinforcement Learning.

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

title: Advances in Intelligent and Learning Agents
description: Advanced topics and research projects building on topics covered in ``Foundations of Intelligent and Learning Agents``. Specific topics covered could include, for example, the following. (1) Contextual bandits (2) Partially Observable Markov Decision Problems (3) Function approximation for reinforcement learning (4) Sample complexity of reinforcement learning (5) Monte Carlo tree search (6) Evolutionary algorithms

reviews.body:
Instructor

Shivaram Kalyanakrishnan

Semester

Spring ‘20

Course Difficulty

The course is primarily project based, which is chosen by your team (of a size of up to 3) after discussing with the professor, so difficulty is variable. Other than the semester long project, the course lectures cover additional advanced RL topics, with weekly quizzes which are not very difficult.

Time Commitment Required

5-7 hours per week

Grading Policy and Statistics

AA 10
AB 14
AU 1
BB 5
Total 30

  • Students auditing the course must score 50 or more marks in the course to be awarded an “AU” grade
  • An outstanding research project will bypass the regular evaluation criteria and automatically result in an “AA” grade for the concerned team

Pre-requisites

CS747 - Foundations of Intelligent and Learning Agents is a hard prerequisite for this course.

Evaluation Scheme

There are 10-12 weekly quizzes, each worth 4–6 marks, and together totaling at least 50 marks. The marks contributed by the quizzes to the grade will be the maximum of the total marks earned in the quizzes and 40.

The research project carries 60 marks, divided as: 5 marks for an introductory presentation, 5 marks for a proposal, 10 marks for a mid-stage presentation, 10 marks for the final presentation, and 30 marks for the final report.

Topics Covered in the Course

Advanced topics and research projects building on topics covered in “Foundations of Intelligent and Learning Agents”. Specific topics covered generally include: (1) Contextual bandits (2) Partially Observable Markov Decision Problems (3) Function approximation for reinforcement learning (4) Sample complexity of reinforcement learning (5) Monte Carlo tree search (6) Evolutionary algorithms

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

Project: After the problem statement is decided, the team is expected to work regularly throughout the semester, and not just a few days before the mid-term and end-term submission, where the latter case leads to low quality projects with lower learning output. There is no significant push from the Professor’s side, so the team doing the project has to be motivated enough to maintain regularity. The professor does hold weekly meets where you can join to discuss your project progress or ask doubts.

Motivation for taking this course

This course is a wonderful opportunity to explore advanced topics in Reinforcement Learning, while doing a semester-long guided research project.

How strongly would I recommend this course?

If you enjoyed CS747 a lot, and would like to dive deeper into RL, you can consider this course. If you also want to do a research-level semester long project with guidance, this course is highly recommended. But, if you get an RnD or SLP in a topic that interests you, I would recommend you choose that over this.

When to take this course?

I took this course in my 6th semester. The ideal semester for taking this course is the one after the semester you took CS747 in (for which Sem 5 is recommended)

References Used

Reinforcement Learning - An Introduction (Richard S. Sutton and Andrew G. Barto) (https://web.stanford.edu/class/psych209/Readings/SuttonBartoIPRLBook2ndEd.pdf)

All content and references from the Spring ‘20 iteration of CS748 (https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~shivaram/teaching/cs748-s2021/index.html)

title: Digital Geometry Processing
description: 3D shape representations: meshes, implicit surfaces, volumetric representations Basic geometric analysis: dimensions, geodesics, curvature, moments etc Advanced geometric analysis: discrete differential geometry, medial axis transform, spectral decomposition Geometric features (local and global): distance histograms, spherical harmonics, light field descriptors, heat kernel signatures Shape search and classification: nearest-neighbor lookup, statistical classifiers Shape segmentation: unsupervised (single and multi-shape), supervised Shape correspondences: sparse and dense Shape parametrizations (local and global): minimizing various metric distortions Surface reconstruction Statistical models of shape structure: templates, grammars, part-based graphical models Shape manipulation: editing and deformations Research topics and trends: review of current state of the art and area trends – models of semantics, function, aesthetics, manufacturability, interaction etc. Reading and discussion of significant selected papers. Applications to design, acquisition, vision and robotics. Small programming assignments for selected subset of above topics, plus final project (ideally a small research project, but can also be a development-based one).

title: Linear Optimization
description: Vector Spaces: bases, echelon forms, rank and determinants. Gauss elimination and its complexity, Inner products, Gram- Schmidt orthogonalization. Linear transformations. Optimization: Modeling and formulation of optimization problems. Linear costs and convex domains. Mean-square (distance) minimizations. Linear programming and the Simplex algorithm. Duality and the primal dual method. Examples from combinatorial optimization. Shortest paths, network flows and matchings. Approximation and randomized algorithms. Matrix Games.

title: Principles of Concurrent and Parallel Programming
description: 1. Concurrency Fundamentals: Critical Section, locks, mutual exclusion, mutex primitives, deadlock freedom, starvation freedom, progress requirements, atomicity, concurrent objects, nondeterminism, monitors, sequential consistency, linearizability, lock free concurrency, communicating sequential processes, rendezvous, java concurrency, data races, thread programming, memory consistency, software transaction Memory 2. Fundamentals of Parallelism (What is the purpose of Parallelism): power and potential of parallelism, paradigm shift, scalable performance and portability, parallel computer models, candidate type architectures (CTA), measuring performance and performance loss, scalable algorithmic techniques, parallelism with multiple instruction streams 3. Realizing Parallelism: Creation and coordination of parallelism , spawn concurrency (async, barriers, finish), abstract performance metrics (work, critical paths), Amdahl`s Law, weak vs. strong scaling, data races and determinism, data race avoidance (immutability, futures, accumulators, dataflow), deadlock avoidance, abstract vs. real performance (granularity, scalability), parallel algorithms, run-time scheduling, work distribution 4. Locality & Distribution (Effective use of Features of Programming Languages): memory hierarchies, locality, cache affinity, data movement, message-passing (MPI), communication overheads (bandwidth, latency), MapReduce, accelerators, GPGPUs, CUDA, OpenCL. Partially global address space programming languages like X10, Fortress, Chapel 5. Distributed Consensus Algorithms and Blockchains: Distributed consensus algorithms like PAXOs, Byzantine fault tolerance, etc., Bitcoin architectures as well other prominent variants. Transaction processing

reviews.body:
Instructor:

Prof R K Shyamasundar

Motivation to take this course:

After my second year summer intern, which gave me flavour of what the practical challenges of working with distributed systems could be like, & having completed the online Operating Systems course offered on Udacity, I decided to actually try and broaden by knowledge in software development by developing an intuition about working with distributed systems: the core ideas, developments over the years & some practical aspects. Hence, I took up this course on “Principles of Concurrent and Parallel Programming”.

Sections:

Just a single section. Very small section (~15 people) with ~40% UGs, mostly from the CS & EE Departments.

Semester:

Autumn ‘19

Course Difficulty:

This course is typically an advanced level course. Although this course initially touches upon the basics of threads & processes along with basic synchronization constructs, it is definitely a good idea to have done an OS course prior to taking this one. Grasping some advanced concepts could be tricky at times & a decent amount of time needs to be spent on reading relevant research papers (provided by the professor) & browsing through multiple reference books & online resources to develop a firm understanding of the content. Exams were typically of moderate-difficult level, but the project was pretty doable. Overall, I would rate the course difficulty as 9/10.

Time commitment required:

Lectures account for 3 hours per week for this course. Simply studying from class notes wasn’t of much help because the slides were very minimalistic. Around 3-4 hours per week should be devoted to review the week’s content & explore related stuff from the references & online sources. Reading the research papers provided by the instructor along with the lecture slides (or trying to figure them out from the snippets present in the slides, hunting them down & reading them) could take up another 2-3 hours weekly (but not necessary every week). So overall, 4-5 hours of weekly self study was necessary to achieve a good grade (8+) in this course.

Attendance Policy:

Attending classes was mandatory, with the general Institute Attendance Policy.

Grading Policy and Statistics:

The grading was reasonably fair, with 2 AA’s, 2 AB’s, 6 BB’s and the remaining to also follow similarly. No FRs were awarded.

Prerequisites:

No hard prerequisites are needed for this course, but having a decent knowledge of Operating Systems, processes, threads & synchronization constructs could ease the ramp-up process. Knowledge of Python/Java/X10/Golang would be beneficial for completing the Course Project.

Evaluation Scheme:

20% Quizzes, 30% Project, 25% Midsem, 30% Endsem

Course Contents (in brief):

The course is meant to familiarize students with fundamentals of concurrency, including concepts like treads, critical sections, different synchronization constructs & the way to analyse algorithms for deadlocks, fairness & starvation. The course kicked off with the introduction to Guarded Commands & Nondeterminism, gradually moving towards classical synchronization problems & related concurrency & parallelism fundamentals. Students were introduced to X10 Parallel Programming Language & were expected to convert pseudocode in different notations to X10 code & vice versa by leveraging the various constructs that were mentioned throughout the course. Representations of concurrency problems using CSP & Ada were also introduced. Post midsem, students were exposed to a multitude of applications of distributed systems including RPCs, Time Synchronization, Big Data handling using MapReduce & parallelism in GP GPUs. The last leg of the course (post quiz 2) focussed on ACID properties of distributed databases & different types of consistency that could be achieved.

Mechanism of Instruction and Teaching Style

The instructor used a mix of slides & whiteboard teaching during the course. Although most of the content explained on the whiteboard were present in the slides, it was still important to take down notes in class because advanced variants of some examples illustrated in class would appear in the exams. Hence, developing an intuition was crucial to extend logical arguments & make suitable assumptions while tackling a problem. The teaching style was reasonable, but definitely not the best that one would expect. Concepts were often left unclear in the students’ minds and we had to do our own research to make sense of some content. Nevertheless, the instructor did make an effort to address doubts in class & was also pretty approachable out of class.

Feedback on Tutorials/Assignments/Projects etc

There were no tutorials or assignments for this course. The course project accounted for 30% of the total score & was around designing & developing a distributed system to cater to some use cases or implement some distributed algorithms explained in a research paper. Students were provided with a list of topics to choose from (while they could also suggest their own ideas & get them verified from the instructor). Language wasn’t a boundary, but Java or X10 was preferred. A 10-min presentation + demo was expected at the end of the course based on the project including some critical analysis of the system.

How strongly would you recommend someone for taking this course?

This is a pretty advanced course, with some reasonable background knowledge implicitly expected. Also, given the vast amount of content covered throughout the course, it could be challenging. Hence, I would recommend this course only to those students who are enthusiastic about facing challenges, want to broaden their thinking (lots of edge & corner cases need to be tackled in a distributed environment), have a decent degree of familiarity with coding & are ready to put in a decent amount of extra effort to connect the dots mentioned in class & grasp the nuances of building large scale distributed applications.

When did you take this course? What will be the ideal semester to take this course? Any other course which can be done before this? I took up this course in my 5th semester (Autumn ‘19). Being a pretty advanced course, I would suggest picking it up only in the 3rd, 4th or 5th year (preferably 4th or 5th year). Doing some course on Operating Systems (CS 347 M or any online variant) thoroughly would set a great foundation to take up this course later on.

Feedback on Exams:

The exams were completely application-oriented & tested the understanding of the student largely. Some questions demanded careful analysis of some variants of the algorithms mentioned in class to spot the places where things could go wrong, while others involved writing code (or pseudocode) to solve some concurrency problems. One isn’t expected to get the syntax all correct if (s)he manages to get the solution idea correct. The difficulty of all the exams could be categorized as moderate-difficult and evaluation was pretty strict.

References used:

No fixed reference suggested by the instructor. References used by me:

  1. The Little Book of Semaphores (by Allen B. Downey)
  2. An Introduction to Programming with Threads (by Andrew Birrell)
  3. The Art of Multiprocessor Programming (by Maurice Herlihy and Nir Shavit)

title: Automatic Speech Recognition
description: • Introduction to the statistical approach for automatic speech recognition (ASR) • Weighted Finite State Transducers and their Application to ASR • Acoustic Signal Processing for ASR • Acoustic models: Hidden Markov Models, Gaussian Mixture Models, Baum-Welch Maximum Likelihood Estimation • Discriminative Training of Acoustic Models: Maximum Mutual Information, Minimum Word/Phone Error Criteria • Acoustic models continued: Neural network models (Deep feed-forward neural networks, convolutional neural networks and recurrent neural networks) • Pronunciation models: Pronunciation dictionaries, grapheme-to-phoneme models, feature-based models • N-gram language models: estimation, smoothing • ASR decoding problem: search algorithms, Viterbi estimation, finite-state transducer optimizations

CS 753 Review By: Samyak Shah

We thank the Mechanical DAMP team for this review

Name – Vaibhav Raj

Course Name – Automatic Speech Recognition

Course Code – CS753

Academic Year – 2021-22

Semester – Spring

Course Instructor – Prof. Preethi Jyothi

Motivation: I was interested in exploring Machine Learning courses in the institute and I
was eligible for CS753. ASR was a new topic for me and Preethi Ma’am specializes in the
field which makes it a very good choice. The website from a previous offering
(https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~pjyothi/cs753/index.html) was also pretty detailed and expansive
in coverage.

Prerequisite: An introductory course in ML – one or more of CS 725, CS 726, CS 747, CS
419(M), EE 769, IE 663, GNR 652, GNR 638, DS 303.

Course-content: The course starts off with classical approaches towards ASR like Hidden
Markov Models and Weighted Finite State Transducers. Language Models are discussed in
detail with various smoothing techniques. Gradually, parts of the framework are replaced
with Neural Networks and the course moves on to Recurrent Neural Networks, Attention.
More techniques like CTC Loss, Beam Search, Lattices and wav2vec are introduced. Prof
also discusses feature construction for audio and how audio is produced.
One might feel that covering traditional ML topics like HMMs, WFSTs won’t help, now that
Transformers are all the hype but as you go through the course, you realize baselines set
by the former models are very hard to beat even with NNs and they are good to know
because new models often derive inspiration from them.

Lectures: Part of the semester was online, so Prof used to upload recorded lectures and
organised doubt-clearing/problem-solving sessions in the course slot. Second half of the
course (post-midsem) was primarily seminar (offline+online) – 3 teams presented their
allotted papers every lecture (more on this below).

Project: The usual project was replaced by a seminar with 5 components. 5 different
papers were allotted out of a list of 50.

Assignments: Two in number – both introduced new libraries/frameworks. 1st was on
OpenFST and Kaldi – toolboxes for traditional ASR techniques. 2nd was on Coqui
(https://github.com/coqui-ai) for Transfer Learning to Indian languages. They were hard to
approach because you will practically have 0 experience with any of these and the learning
curve is steep but once you complete them, you will be pretty confident with going deeper
into ASR.

Exams: 2 – Midsem and Endsem – problems were descriptive (asking about some
technique/model discussed in class) or about solving/creating probabilistic frameworks for
different ideas (like false start). The questions were interesting and some were on the
moderate-hard side.

Weightage: Assignment 1 – 10%, Assignment 2 – 10%, Midsem – 15%, Endsem – 30%, Seminar –
30% (Talk, Review – 8 each, others ~ 4/5 each), Participation (weekly short quizzes on Moodle) –
5%

Difficulty: Hard (4/5)
Time-consuming course because of so many components but makes you well-posed for
research in ASR.

Grading: Moderately-Hard
Total – 105 | AA – 8, AB – 11, BB – 25, BC – 29, CC – 21, CD – 9, DD – 1, AU – 1

References: Website from 2019 offering –
https://www.cse.iitb.ac.in/~pjyothi/cs753/index.html is a good start.

Advanced courses: Not followed by any other courses, RnD/BTP under Prof Preethi
Jyothi is a good possibility.

title: Advanced Image Processing
description: (1) Image transforms and statistics of natural images ● survey of statistical properties of image transform coefficients ● implications of these statistics for important image processing applications such as denoising, compression, source separation, deblurring and image forensics ● non-local self-similarity in images (2) Dictionary learning and sparse representations in image processing ● Overview of Principal Components Analysis (PCA), Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) and Independent Components Analysis (ICA); PCA, SVD and ICA in the context of image processing ● Sparse PCA ● Concept of overcomplete dictionaries ● Greedy pursuit algorithms: matching pursuit (MP), orthogonal matching pursuit (OMP) and basis pursuit (BP) ● Popular dictionary learning techniques: Method of Optimal Directions (MOD), Unions of Orthonormal Bases, K-SVD, Non-negative sparse coding – along with applications in image compression, denoising, inpainting and deblurring ● Sparsity-seeking algorithms: iterative shrinkage and thresholding (ISTA) (3) Compressed Sensing (CS) ● Concept and need for CS ● Theoretical treatment: concept of coherence, null-space property and restricted isometry property, proof of a key theorem in CS ● Algorithms for CS (covered in part 2) and some key properties of these algorithms ● Applications of CS: Rice Single Pixel Camera and its variants, Video compressed sensing, Color and Hyperspectral CS, Applications in Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), Implications for Computed Tomography ● CS under Forward Model Perturbations: a few key results and their proofs as well as applications ● Designing Forward Models for CS ● Low-rank matrix estimation and Robust Principal Components Analysis: concept and application scenarios in image processing, statement of some key theorems, and proof of one important theorem

Name: Atulya Kumar Verma

Course Name: Advanced Image Processing

Course Code: CS754

Academic Year: 2019

Semester: Spring Semester

Course Instructor: Prof. Ajit Rajwade


Click here to continue reading

CS 754 : Advanced Image Processing

Semester : Spring 2017

Professor : Ajit Rajwade

Prerequisites : The professor strongly recommends one of the following courses as a prerequisite : CC 663, EE 610 or CS 725.

Course Content :

This course was the taught time first this Semester and the professor might change the topics slightly depending on the reviews. The detailed course contents are available on course website. (The link is at the end of review). Broadly, the topics covered are:

Statistics of Natural Images

Denoising in different conditions

Compressive Sensing

Dictionary Learning

Tomographic Reconstruction

The focus is slightly more towards image processing with a perspective of signals as compared to learning based approaches.

Feedback on lectures :

Excellent. Easy to follow. Professor teaches the topics very well and ensures that you understand each topic along with its nuances very well in the class. The slides are comprehensive but it is necessary to pay attention in the class. Often the discussions in the class are helpful.

Exams and assignments :

The professor intends this to be more project and assignment based course. Hence both the midsem and endsem are of 10 marks and are very straightforward, the questions mostly drawn from slides and class discussions. A major chunk of the weightage (55%) goes to assignments. There were 5 assignments and it takes considerable amount to solve them. They were a mix of coding part and theoretical questions. The questions in the assignment are well chosen and provide a good insight into the covered content. The project is for 25% and highly stressed by the professor. This course is more inclined towards the research side and hence the project is very important. The professor strongly recommends and motivates a well-thought out project and promotes and rewards ingenious ideas and implementations.

Difficulty :

The course as such is easy to follow and professor makes it comfortable for you. The main stress is on projects and assignments for which there is ample time allotted. Anyone who is enthusiastic to take up a higher level course in Image processing but on a broad scale without going into the specifics of any substream, this is a recommended course. Also it isn’t very intensive as such.

References :

The slides are comprehensive and contain everything that is required. More details and specifics of certain parts of the course can be found out here.

Sudeep Salgia

4th Year BTech

Link to the actual review

Name: Atulya Kumar Verma

Course Name: Advanced Image Processing

Course Code: CS754

Academic Year: 2019

Semester: Spring Semester

Course Instructor: Prof. Ajit Rajwade

Motivation: I had done CS215: Data Analysis and Interpretation in the third sem as a core course and had enjoyed it. I found out later about Prof Ajit’s offering of CS754 with only CS215 as a pre-req and was eager to explore more. It was my first course on Image Processing

Prerequisites: The course did have CS663: Fundamentals of Digital Image Processing, which is a more introductory course on Image Processing, as a prereq. But having done only CS215 was sufficient as well because the Prof explains the basics thoroughly whenever required.

Course Content: The course begins with the theoretical compressed sensing, its algorithms, applications, examples etc. It then covers tomography, dictionary learning, low rank matrix recovery and also covers some machine learning topics. Although the later part of the course was not covered due to the COVID situation, the content covered was very thorough and well organised.

Lectures: We had two 1.5 hr lectures every week, on Tuesday and Friday in the 5:30 to 7:00 pm slot. 

Project: Project was a huge part of the course. It was to be done individually or in teams of two. Originally we had till a few days after the endsem to submit it. The topics were preselected by the Prof., but we could choose to work on a completely new topic as well. There were both maths heavy and implementation heavy projects. We worked on a video denoising project, which incorporated low rank matrix completion algorithms. All the implementation was to be done in MATLAB. The project was graded through a presentation and viva. 

Assignments: Assignments were the other large part of the course. There were regular assignments, through the entire semester. They were also done individually or in teams of two. They typically contained a mixture of theoretical/proof related problems and some implementation problems. Assignments were actually very helpful as they cleared up all the doubts in the current topics and also brought us up to speed.

Exams: There were midsem and endsem, but they were not very heavy. They were meant to only test some basic concepts and were pretty easy to score good marks in just by following the lectures.

Difficulty: ⅗. Without any background in Image Processing, the start of the course seemed a little overwhelming, but due to Ajit Sir’s enthusiastic teaching efforts, it eventually smoothens out. Overall, it was a moderately difficult course.

Grading: Mid sem and end sem were 10% each, the assignments altogether formed 50% of the course, and the project was 25%. 5% was kept for class participation. 

References: The course doesn’t follow a book, and the lecture slides are enough. Every now and then you’ll need to read research articles for assignments and project.

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review

Instructor: Prof. Ajit Rajwade

Session: 2019-2020

Prerequisites: CS 663 or EE 610 or CS 725

Course content and structure:

1) Image compression through DCT.
2) Compressed sensing, theory (convergence bounds) and algorithms like OMP, MP, LASSO, ISTA.
3) Rice single pixel camera, Increasing frame rate without much loss of quality
4) Tomography
5) Dictionary learning through SVD, PCA, NMF, NNSC
6) Overcomplete dictionaries. KSVD
7) Blind Compressed sensing
8) Classification problems

Feedback on lectures:

Lectures are well paced. Instructor uses both board and slides to explain the concepts. He highly encourages doubts and 5% marks were kept for class participation to encourage students to ask more doubts. The instructor also kept office hours before the class where he used to clear doubts of students.

Feedback on tutorials and exams:

The instructor used to keep tutorials (mostly included doubt solving) on weekends to solve common doubts and some questions.
There were 6-7 assignments decided (but due to COVID only 3 were graded). They were put every 2 weeks and were interesting as well as highly demanding. Assignments had 50% weightage.
Midsems and endsems were 10% each.
Course project had 25% weightage and was the major factor in deciding the grades. The project included reviewing a related paper.
Finally, 5% was kept for class participation.
The instructor also expects compulsory attendance.

Difficulty (on a scale of 1 being very easy to 5 being very hard): 3

Textbooks & References: Lecture slides were sufficient.

Grading Statistics: Refer asc. Grading was on the lenient side with majority of the class getting AA and AB.

Additional comments & what you learnt from the course: The course majorly deals with Compressed sensing and sparse signal processing which is a highly valued field in signal processing. Compressed sensing techniques are widely used in tomography, MRI and video and image compression. Sparse signal processing provides a good alternative to ML based techniques majorly when dataset is scarce.

Review by – Ameya Anjarlekar (ameyanjarlekar@gmail.com)

Link to the actual review

Course Name : Advanced Image Processing (CS 754)

Instructor : Prof. Ajit Rajwade

Course run in Spring 2018

Prerequisite : A basic probability and statistics course (equivalent to CS 215 or EE 223) and preferably DIP (CS 663/ EE610), or CS 725 (Machine Learning) is strongly recommended by the professor

Motivation : Image processing is a very active area of research right now, as well as finds applications in various interdisciplinary avenues. The images that we see and acquire in our day-to-day life have a number of very interesting properties. This course talks about many such properties and their applications to various algorithms in detail. It covers a wide spectrum of sub fields in image processing such as image noise removal, blur removal, image separation, compression and even image-based forensics. and introduces to many standard techniques used frequently in statistics and machine learning. If you are someone who enjoys mathematical proofs as well as coding, you will enjoy this course.

Course Content : This year was the second offering of the course and the professor might change the topics slightly depending on the reviews. The detailed course contents are available on course website. (The link is at the end of review). Broadly, the topics covered this year were the following (in the same order):

The focus is slightly more towards image processing with a perspective on signals as compared to learning based approaches.

Feedback on lectures : The lectures are excellent and very easy to follow. The Professor teaches the topics very well and ensures that you understand each topic along with its nuances in the class. He encourages doubts and discussions in the class which are very helpful. The slides are comprehensive but it is necessary to pay attention in the class. This year the lectures were recorded for CDEEP too.

Exams and assignments : This course unlike most other courses is focussed towards assignments and the project. The midsem and endsem both are of 10% weightage, and are fairly straightforward. Being attentive in the class discussions, and going through the slides properly is sufficient to do well in the midsem and endsem. A major chunk of the weightage (55%) goes to assignments. There were 5 assignments in teams of 2, and it takes considerable amount of time and effort to solve them. Reading new research papers and working out different (but related) theory and proofs was the theme of most assignments. They were a mix of coding and theoretical questions. The questions in the assignment are well chosen and provide a good insight into the covered content.  The project is of 25% credit and is highly stressed by the professor. This is a research centric coure and hence the project is a very important part of it. The professor strongly recommends and motivates a well-thought out project and promotes and rewards ingenious ideas and implementations. If you are working with the professor for an RnD project that semester, he also allows you combine your RnD work with the course project.

Difficulty : The professor ensures that the course in enjoyed by everyone, and that everyone in the class is on the same page always. The course is mainly about the assignments and the project, which take some dedicated amount of time. The assignments are well spaced out, so it is not very intensive as such. This course is highly recommended for anyone who is enthusiastic to take up a higher level course in Image processing but on a broad scale without going into the specifics of any substream.

References : The slides are comprehensive and self sufficient. The instructor also posts some additional reading material and references on moodle. For more details about specific modules of the course, refer this link.

Link to the actual review

Name – Ritesh Goenka

Course Name – Topics in Computational Complexity

Course Code – CS 760

Academic Year – 2019-2020

Semester – Spring

Course Instructor – Sundar Vishwanathan and Mrinal Kumar


Click here to continue reading

Name – Ritesh Goenka

Course Name – Topics in Computational Complexity

Course Code – CS 760

Academic Year – 2019-2020

Semester – Spring

Course Instructor – Sundar Vishwanathan and Mrinal Kumar

Motivation: This delivery of the course mainly focused on the application of algebraic ideas to problems in combinatorics, algorithm design, discrete geometry and discrete mathematics. The course title is somewhat misleading and a more appropriate title would be ‘Algebraic methods in discrete mathematics’. It is important to note that the course content may change depending on the instructors. The theme of the topics covered in this course is unique and exciting, and the techniques used are relatively quite new — many techniques taught in the course were developed in the late 20th and the 21st century.

Prerequisite: A basic knowledge of Linear Algebra was assumed (equivalent to the level of MA 106). Some knowledge of abstract algebra (mainly fields) would be useful but it’s not a hard prerequisite.

Course-content: The first half of the course focused on the application of linear algebra to problems in combinatorics. Some of the topics covered were – Oddtown problem, the Ray Chaudhari-Wilson theorem and its variants, Polynomial method, Combinatorial Nullstellensatz, and the Cauchy-Davenport theorem. Before the semester was cut short, some of the topics covered in the second half were –  bounds on the size of Kakeya and Nikodyn sets, size of 3-AP free sets, and matrix rigidity.

Lectures: The lectures were well-paced and the instructor gave sufficient time to solve tutorial problems in the class. The tutorial problems were discussed in the class as well. In each lecture, some students were asked to scribe notes and upload on moodle.

Labs: (Teams info) This is a theory course.

Project: There was no course project.

Assignments: In the first half of the course, the instructor handed out a couple of homework assignments, each of which consisted of 1-3 problems. The solutions were to be written on sheets and submitted in the class. In addition, the instructor challenged the students with some tough problems, submitting the solutions to any one of which would guarantee a good grade.

Exams: There was a mid-semester exam. Due to the semester being cut short, the instructor was not able to conduct an end-semester exam and possibly one or two quizzes.

Difficulty: The mid-semester exam was based completely on the topics taught in the class. The classes were easy to follow and the instructor was determined to clarify any doubts in the class itself. Consequently, for anyone who attended the classes and made an effort to understand the covered material, the course was easy and fun.

Grading: The grading was extremely lenient and the instructors were only interested in students’ learning.

References: [1] Algebraic methods in in combinatorics – A course by Boris Bukh. [2] Polynomial method in combinatorics – A course by Larry Guth.

Advanced courses: The course is only one of its kind offered in the department.

We thank the Computer Science DAMP team for this review
