EN 653

Course Name 

Energy Policy Analysis


Click here to continue reading

Instructor:

Prof Rangan Banerjee

Motivation to take this course:

Exploration of the public policy space with a focus on technology. It will equip you with the tools to analyse and design a policy intervention. You will be able to assess the government policies towards dealing with climate-change/global warming etc.

Sections:

Only one section, not a core course

Semester:

Spring ‘20

Course Difficulty:

Moderate

Time commitment required:

Moderate

Attendance Policy:

Attendance in classes, presentations, computer tutorials is compulsory. To encourage 100% attendance, an incentive of + 5 marks in the in semester will be given for 90%+ attendance, a penalty of -3 marks for attendance between 70-80 % and a penalty of -5 marks for attendance less than 70%.

Grading Policy and Statistics:

Relative Grading AA-4; AB-11; BB-13; BC-7

Prerequisites:

No pre-reqs (Some overlap in initial course content with EN606, but a small handout is given with relevant content)

Evaluation Scheme:

Assignment-10; Course project-30; Midsem Test-10; Weekly Quizzes- 10; Endsem Exam- 40

Course Contents (in brief):

The course will provide the tools and techniques necessary for analyzing energy policies. Students will be expected to carry out a project to analyse an existing policy in a specific context or design a policy intervention for a specific goal.

  • Overview of energy policy instruments – pricing, regulation, Incentives, subsidies
  • Framework for policy analysis, stakeholders, criteria – energy access, security, sustainability, development
  • Case studies of a few energy policies – successes and failures, Free riders and rebound effect
  • Reference Energy system, End Use Analysis
  • Modelling Energy Demand, Elasticities
  • Substitution- S-shaped Logistic curves, Examples of accelerated diffusion- factors affecting diffusion
  • Economy wide impacts- Input-Output models, Optimisation models, TIMES-MARKAL
  • Scenario generation – approach and examples
  • Energy policy analysis project

Mechanism of Instruction and Teaching Style

Rangan sir is a very good instructor. He teaches by quoting a lot of real-world and current examples. A great mix of theory, problem-solving and analysis. Sir gives a lot of focus to understanding the numbers that result from calculations - significance, comparison etc. He is patient and clears all doubts well.

Feedback on Tutorials/Assignments/Projects etc

Assignment and Project are both group submissions. Project will require significant time commitment over the semester with a couple of interactions with the Prof, TA(s) to discuss progress. Mid-term report has to be submitted as well. Overall, the different modes of evaluations offer diverse learning outcomes and hence the course is well-designed in that perspective.

How strongly would you recommend someone for taking this course?

If you’re remotely interested in the policy space with application to tech, definitely would recommend this course.

When did you take this course? What will be the ideal semester to take this course? Any other course which can be done before this?

I took the course in my 8th semester. Can be done in any semester.

Feedback on Exams:

Quizzes are very-short 5-10 min long. They are directly based on what is taught in class, easy-level questions and do not require any prep as such. Mid-Sem paper was a little difficult and lengthy. However, every question usually has 4-5 subparts, the first few parts in each are usually easy to answer. In other words, sometimes the solution is just broken down into sub-parts of the question- this makes it easier to solve as the method is indirectly laid out. End-Sem was not conducted (Due to the semester termination) but can be expected to be similar to the mid-sem

Importance of course:

Useful for research in Transportation/Energy policy.

References used:

Professor shares relevant articles from time to time. There is some overlap with the EN606 (Energy Resources, Economics and Environment) course offered in the autumn semester- NPTEL videos are available (Rangan Sir only) https://nptel.ac.in/courses/109/101/109101171/

Other interesting links:

The course will help you make better sense of the discussions below and encourage you to get involved

  • Renewables in Tasmania
  • Climate Change
  • NDC goals of different countries under Paris Agreement

title: Micro and Nanoscale Energy Transport
description: ontinuum laws and limitations ; Energy carriers at micro/nanoscale and their attributes; Engineering applications, Size effects 2. Energy states Schrodinger equation; Energy states of quantum wells, harmonic oscillators, rigid rotors, hydrogen atoms, electrons and phonons in crystals; Applicability in nanoscale devices such as quantum dot lasers, superlattices and photonic crystals 3. Energy storage Density of states; Ensembles; molecular partition functions; specific heat of gases; Fermi-Dirac and Bose Einstein distributions; Specific heat of phonons and electrons 4. Energy transport Energy transport by waves: plane electron waves and reflection, electromagnetic waves and reflection at single interface, interference and tunneling, acoustic waves; Energy transport by particles: Wave to particle transition, Boltzmann equation, scattering mechanisms and relaxation time approximation, Derivation of continuum laws including Fourier law, Newton’s shear stress law, Ohm’s law from the Boltzmann equation Transport by liquid: Derivation of Navier Stokes equation for gas molecules, Single phase liquid flow and heat transport in microtubes and channels; Two-phase heat transfer in microchannels; 5. Energy conversion: Nonequilibrium between different heat carriers, nonequilibrium between electrons and phonons in laser-materials interaction, Hot and cold electron effects in semiconductor devices and thermoelectric devices 6. Simulation and measurement techniques in micro and nanoscale transport processes Introduction to Monte Carlo technique and molecular dynamics ; Scanning thermal microscopy; Optical techniques for local measurements and short-time heat transfer effects

title: Introduction to Innovation & Entrepreneurship
description: The following is a list of topics of which a majority will be covered for all students (in any given year) Week # Topics Innovation 1 Importance of innovation, design thinking and creative problem-solving 2 Design principles; System design, embodiment, and components 3 Design brief; Concept generation; Idea sketching 4 Proof-of-concept fabrication; Rapid prototyping 5 Prototype testing; Early customer feedback; Design improvement 6 Design for manufacturability; design for environment and other factors 7 Intellectual property rights; Technology licensing models. Entrepreneurship 1 What it takes to be an entrepreneur; Market opportunity identification 2 Customer discovery; Problem definition; Customer value proposition 3 New product development process; Product lifecycle management 4 Basics of finance and business planning for technology ventures 5 Marketing and go-to-market planning of technology products 6 Leadership, communication, team building and conflict management 7 Business model canvas; Pitch deck for presentation to potential partners

timetable.professor: Anuradha Narasimhan, Ramesh Mangaleswaran, Sankalp Pratap

reviews.body:
Who was the course instructor?

Anu Narasimhan and P Kumaresan

What was taught in this course?

The entrepreneurship part of the course gives a good idea about the basics of business and entrepreneurship, focusing on what goes into making and sustaining one along with the market jargon. We also had a lecture on the basics of accounting wherein we learnt how to make a balance sheet. The innovation part teaches you about design and how it is relevant to entrepreneurship (mainly about product design)

What were the evaluation components of the course?

1 quiz on entrepreneurship, 2 quizzes on innovation, 1 group video presentation on an existing startup and endsem. Attendance also carried marks

How has your experience been during the course duration?

It was quite an interesting course with engaging classroom discussions in ENT lectures. Innovation lectures didn't have a very set content so it was not always engaging but overall the course was light and interesting.

Any suggestions for people doing this HASMED in the future and words of advice?

Pay attention during lectures, be very sincere about attendance, grading was generous so it will be a good chance to boost your CPI and most importantly, take it easy;)

Who was the course instructor?

Prof. Anu, Prof Ramesh, Prof Kums

What was taught in this course?

Majorly about entrepreneurship and growth of a start-up, which was taken up by Prof Anu. While Prof Ramesh took up the financial aspect of a business. Prof Kums taught innovation and design in the creation of a product.

What were the evaluation components of the course?

There were 2 quizzes(one for entrepreneurship and one for design) and 1 endsem(1 papers of ent and innovation respectively)

How has your experience been during the course duration?

The course was generally fun and interactive throughout. The innovation part of the course did feel a little empty. We often felt as if we were going through random texts and not actually learning to implement our creativity.

Any suggestions for people doing this HASMED in the future and words of advice?

It’s a really fun course where you’ll learn many new terms and aspects of start-up culture. A word of advice would be to not only attend classes but pay keen attention to what the professor is saying.
Also, study a little for the quizzes, but don’t necessarily take stress. If you’ve paid attention in class, it would be easy to score well.

title: Introduction to Entrepreneurship
description: *To acquaint the students with the evolution and impact of entrepreneurship on business and society. * To introduce the students to the importance and application techniques of innovation and creativity to the generation of new ideas. *To help the students understand the process of transformation of ideas into business opportunities. * To help aspiring student entrepreneurs in establishing the feasibility of a new venture.

reviews.body:
Instructor

Anand T Kusre(Course Instructor), Milind D Atrey, Rajen Jaswa

Semester

Autumn 2019-20

Course Difficulty

Easy

Time Commitment Required

1.5-2 hours/week (apart from classes)

Grading Policy and Statistics

Absolute grading normalised according to highest score
Statistics are normal and not-so-lenient

Attendance Policy

80% attendance was compulsory and counts towards grades. DX was also given to students with very low attendance.

Pre-requisites

No prerequisites

Evaluation Scheme

3-4 easy case study based assignments
1 final group presentation
Midsem with basic definitions and focused on BMC
Endsem on application based based questions and case-studies

Topics Covered in the Course

Real life case studies
An Entrepreneur’s mindset
Understanding a Business Model Canvas and how to make one in detail
Basics of Intellectual Property
Basics of Business Communications: Pitching and Presentation
Guest lectures by established Entrepreneurs

Teaching Style

Interactive classes, case study based approach, pace may be slow but every aspect is covered and discussed

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

Assignments are easy and stimulate creative thinking
Group project/presentation gives exposure to basics of entrepreneurship practically

Feedback on Exams

Exams are moderately easy. Case study, definitions and BMC cover most of them.

Motivation for taking this course

Passion for entrepreneurship

Course Importance

A good course to get to know the prerequisites before embarking on your startup journey

How strongly would I recommend this course?

If you have a thing for starting a new venture of yours soon or in near future, this course would be a good initial guide

When to take this course?

I am passionate about entrepreneurship and want to complete my minor in the same. 3rd semester is ideal because the course does not require a lot of time commitment and is a good time to explore this field as the course is introductory.

References Used

Slides and classroom discussions are enough

title: Developing the Proof-of-Concept
description: The course is designed to enable students in converting their ideas with commercial potential into a tangible product/service.*Understanding the new product development process, *Learning to integrate the needs of the end-consumer into the process,*Acquiring the skill to apply the standard concepts and tools of the process, *Using the new product development process to conceiveown new product or service, *Developing the new product in a typical laboratory environmentand generating an effective go-tomarket strategy

reviews.body:
Instructor: Prof. P. Kumaresan

Session: 2019-2020

Prerequisites: N/A

Course content and structure:

The course is designed to enable students in converting their ideas with commercial potential into a tangible product/service. In addition to helping them understand the new product development process, it helps the students in learning to integrate the needs of the end-consumer into the new product development process. In this course the students are expected to use the new product development process to conceive their own new product or service. At this stage they are not expected to build a startup around this product or service.

Feedback on lectures: In the first part of the course lectures of 3 hrs were conducted weekly. Prof explained the concepts in a very interesting manner. Usual institute policy of attendance was enforced.

Feedback on tutorials and exams: Being a complete project based course it didn’t have any exams,not even midsem and endsem . Weekly labs were conducted in the second half of the course in the IDC lab. Few small assignments were given in between. The final project comprised of a footwear design model . The final evaluation was done almost entirely on the final project.

Difficulty (on a scale of 1 being very easy to 5 being very hard): 1

Textbooks & References: No specific textbook required being a practical training course

Grading Statistics:

Chill :
AP-1
AA-31
AB-19
BB-17
BC-5
CC-2
W-8

Additional comments & what you learnt from the course: The overall flow of the course is pretty chill. Follow the prof’s instruction and take the final project very very seriously.

Review by – Anurag Kumar (aforanurag99@gmail.com)

Link to the actual review

Instructor

Kums P Kumaresan

Semester

Autumn ‘20

Course Difficulty

Super easy.

Time Commitment Required

Very less load. Hardly 1 hour per week was required. Attending the 1.5 hour lecture every week and interact was enough to get a good grade .

Grading Policy and Statistics

  1. Those who didn’t submit any assignments and attended zero classes got FF.
  2. Those who had attendance below 50% and submitted less number of assignments got BC or BB , depending on the quality of work.
  3. Those who had above 50% attendance and submitted all assignments got AB, AA. Quality of work, involvement in class was considered.
  4. Those who had above 90% attendance, decent quality designs got AA.
  5. CC would be the least grade. He hoped nobody falls in that category.
    Grading Stats:
    AA 51
    AB 35
    BB 21
    BC 7
    FF 5
    Total 119

Attendance Policy

No attendance but brownie points for attending the class. Anyways there was only 1 lecture of 1.5 hours every week.

Pre-requisites

No pre-requisites. No prior knowledge required.

Evaluation Scheme

No midsem and endsem. Evaluation was just based on the assignments and the final presentation.

Topics Covered in the Course

The course is designed to enable students in converting their ideas with commercial potential into a tangible product/service.
Understanding the new product development process, Learning to integrate the needs of the end-consumer into the process.
Acquiring the skill to apply the standard concepts and tools of the process.
Developing the new product in a typical laboratory environment and generating an effective go-to market strategy.

Teaching Style

Professor showed real life examples and he himself did some practical work for the students. Live lectures were taken on MS Teams and info was conveyed by the professor on WhatsApp and MS Teams. He was very interactive on WhatsApp as well and really enjoyed talking to students.

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

Only assignments were there and a final presentation containing all the assignments was submitted. Some assignments were very interesting, some were not that great.

Feedback on Exams

None

Motivation for taking this course

Non theory course. Completely practical work course, so someone who does not want to do theory can definitely take this course. And one of the coolest professors of the institute, who gives his best so that the students can enjoy his course. Apart from this very chill grading stats.

How strongly would I recommend this course?

If you really want to do something different from studying and want a good grade, then with some effort you can definitely achieve it.

When to take this course?

I took this course in the 5th semester. You can take this course in any semester as it won’t affect any other course that you have taken in that semester.

Milind D Atrey, R R Hirwani

Spring 2020-21

Easy

1.5-2 hours/week (apart from classes)

Grading Policy and Statistics

Absolute grading based on the highest score

Compulsory attendance (10% weightage), relaxed in online sem

No prerequisites

Patent Search Assignment 20%
Case study group assignment 20%
MCQ quiz 20%
Endsem exam 40%

Introduction to IP, Patent Fundamentals, Copyrights, Designs, Trade Marks, Service Dress, Branding, Trade Secrets and other forms of IP, Industry specific IP issues, IP Management, IP Issues in Agreements

Moderate exams. Not too easy. Practical application based and case study questions along with definition based questions.

Motivation for taking this course

Passion for entrepreneurship and learning about its legal aspects

How strongly would I recommend this course?

For those interested in research or entrepreneurship: 8/10. Prof Atrey is Dean R&D and takes this course which is again a big plus

When to take this course?

6th semester. Ideally it can be taken in the 4th semester as well. I opted for it then but since it is a 2nd half semester course, it had to be cancelled then due to the pandemic

Instructor

Prof Rajen Jaswa

Semester

Spring ‘20

Course Difficulty

The course wasn’t difficult. It mainly was thinking about an idea, discussing about it and presenting it.

Time Commitment Required

Although the classes were optional, some events and talks were super fun to attend. Do attend if an external speaker is invited.

Grading Policy and Statistics

Not as chill as other ent courses. So if you’re looking for a free grade, please find another ent course.

Attendance Policy

Optional

Pre-requisites

None. If you have a group of 3-4 with you, it becomes easier than to make a random group.

Evaluation Scheme

Presenting your start-up project’s progress. Lite only.

Topics Covered in the Course

TVC is like a crash course of the whole ent minor. It’s the capstone course of ent. Also, there’s trip to a rural setting involved here, sponsored by DSSE itself. Spring ‘20, they took us to a village in Ahmedabad to interact with the community and understand their problems. This was to enable us to come up with ideas solving tech related issues, specifically for villages.

ENT 208 Review By: Shardul Burde

title: Technology Venture Creation
description: Course Content (List of the topics/sub-topics to be covered in the lectures/practicals/assignments): The course will focus on converting technology ideas into business opportunities through the following important topics Opportunity Assessment –Assessing technology ideas in terms of market opportunity Market Validation - Market Research & Evaluation Customer Discovery – Identifying the customer need and the right target customers Product Development Plan Product Validation Go-to-market planning Business Model Analysis Early and later stage financing of startups Management of Intellectual Property The course envisages student teams going through all the stages of a new venture creation with critical inputs from mentors leading to business proposals which would be assessed by experts.

reviews.body:
Instructor: Prof. Raj Jaswa, Anand Kusre

Session: 2019-2020

Prerequisites: N/A

Course content and structure:

Technology venture creation is one of the capstone courses offered by DSSE, IITB and does full justice in terms of “practical” learning you experience if you are enthusiastic about entrepreneurship.
This course typically runs in spring semester and focuses on creating a complete business plan, including team, product, customers, supply chain, market research, financial sustainability etc.
As engaging the course was, a word of caution needs to be spoken and that is it can’t be taken lightly. You must be willing to give time and energy as many people might be under the false notion that since it isn’t a typical theoretical academia course, it would be really easy to swim through it – nothing can be further from the truth. Infact this will force you to step out of your comfort zone in ways you wouldn’t have done before.
Just a fun fact, there was also a field visit of entire batch to a remote village in Gujarat organised by DSSE team for us to inteact with the people there and possibly come up with a viable business idea which solves their problem(s). (social entrepreneurship)

Feedback on lectures:

The environment of lectures were not having the typical environment of professor bombarding information and student taking notes, rather the instructors typically focussed on do’s/dont’s, startup case studies etc. which were intended to be interactive.
One of the awesome things was that there were guest instructors delivering 1-2 lectures with their invaluable experience and insights since most of them were themselves business tycoons and not some person possessing bookish knowledge with little to no hands-on.
You could ask any questions/doubts that you are facing while working on your idea and gain real nuggest of wisdom from them. However you need to be expressive and remove the skin of shyness that surrounds most students as it will ultimately hamper your learning.

Feedback on tutorials and exams:

Coming straight to the point, there were no exams (neither midsem nor endsem) /tutorials. As it should be, this course was extremely practical where push was given to taking action and not mug up entrepreneurship or business jargon eventually regurgitating in exam.
Grading was done based on Gate -1 and Gate -2 team presentation followed with a detailed team report (single report for 1 team) pertaining to your business idea which you had been pursuing during the semester.
The Gate -1, Gate -2 were essentially in-depth pitching sessions before professors (course instructors and from other departments as well) and other batch mates where the ideas and progress were presented followed by Q&A.
The report can be referred to as a case study for a team which basically expected us to summarize our journey and findings, how the idea evolved over time, what we did to validate our assumptions, what MVP did we build and the plan of way ahead.

Difficulty (on a scale of 1 being very easy to 5 being very hard): 4

Textbooks & References:

No “textbooks” were recommended, infact there aren’t “academic books” on entrepreneurship. Lecture slides however, were shared on moodle which mainly covered case studies that were discussed in lecture itself.

Grading Statistics: Viewing the course while solely focusing on grades, one can say it was rather difficult to secure good grade. There were around 46 students enrolled in the course (208 as minors + 602 as electives) out of which ~18 students were awarded with AA, AB, & BB grades. It would definitely be suggested to not opt for this course with the casual mindset to increase his/her CPI by tagging it or in any similar way.

Additional comments & what you learnt from the course:

Be prepared to speak in front of people. You’ll notice drastic improvement in your confidence and public speaking skills as a byproduct of this course. The Q&A which generally follows any presentation sessions also prepares you for future interviews where you need to give short and precise answers without missing any important detail. The overall learning you’ll have would be intangible in that you cant showcase it on your resume but that doesn’t decrease its value as it will be something that will remain with you for the rest of your lifetime and you dont even need to revise it. ;p
Also if you are really passionate about starting a startup, the lectures will be energising instead of energy sucking and you’ll be excited and look forward to the next lecture thanks to the fact that its not so cognitively taxing like typical science lectures.
At the same time people end up withdrawing/dropping the course due to its load because they weren’t mentally prepared to take such a huge responsibilty of starting something before enrolling in this course.

title: Introduction to Entrepreneurship
description: Course Description: Now more than ever is a great time to create value and to create wealth in emerging economies as an entrepreneur. This activity-oriented foundation course provides the necessary context and content for learners interested in innovation and entrepreneurship. Learners will gain an understanding of the Entrepreneurial process – the entrepreneurial mindset, opportunity identification and innovation, customer discovery and Go-To-Market, entrepreneurial finance and technology venture creation. This course will use a combination of concept teaching and real-life storytelling to bring alive the entrepreneurial journey for learners. Learners can apply the principles and practices taught through a hands-on course project. There have been ventures created by students who have taken this course, and some of them will be covered as case studies. Course instruction is by a faculty team who bring in experience and expertise in entrepreneurship, academia, industry. Learning Objectives: (1) Getting into the entrepreneurial mindset (2) Gaining an understanding of the innovation and entrepreneurship process (3) Recognizing opportunities for new business (4) Getting hands-on during the Customer Discovery Process (5) Understanding Technology evolution and product and business life-cycles (6) Gaining insights into startup financing (7) Understanding of business functions essential for success of technology enterprises. Course Pre-requisite: None. B.Tech 2nd - 4th Year & M.Tech Students, PhD and Post-Doctoral Researchers can enroll. Course Evaluation: Mid Sem & End Sem (50%), Groups of 4-6 Project Business Model Canvas( 30%), Attendance. Class Participation , Assignments (20%)

reviews.body:
Instructor

Prof. Anuradha Narasimhan, Prof. Rajen Jaswa, Prof. Ravi B.

Course Type

Institute Elective

Semester

Autumn (Odd)

Pre-Requisite

There are no pre-requisite for this course.

Learning Outcome

This course gives an insight into entrepreneurship and emphasizes the role of technology & technological innovation. The course allows students to develop a full-fledged startup and present it to a panel of investors. We get to learn how to create a Business model canvas, minimum viable product, and financial modeling. The topics covered in this course might help in the other courses offered by the DSSE under the ENT department. 

Pre-Midsem Course Content

The following modules were covered in this course:

1. Role of innovation and entrepreneurship
2. What it takes to be an entrepreneur
3. Business fundamentals
4. Leadership and team building
5. Business model canvas
6. Opportunity identification and evaluation
7. Basics of new venture creation
8. Customer discovery and customer value proposition
9. Marketing and go-to-market

All the modules are very interesting and are covered in an interactive way. Even though every module is unique and important, you need to be more attentive during business fundamentals and business model canvas modules.

Post-Midsem Course Content

The following modules were covered post- midsem in this course: 

1. New product development – crossing the chasm
2. New product development – Developing proof of concept
3. Technology evolution and business life cycles
4. Basics of IP for entrepreneurs
5. Entrepreneurial Finance
6. Business communication

The above mentioned modules were covered using various case studies and with the aid of guest speakers. The topics which were difficult than others are: basics of IP and Entrepreneurial Finance.

Slides
Ease of Understanding: 5
Completeness: 5
Depth: 5

Teaching method

The profs were very passionate for the lectures. They ensured that all the lectures are activity-oriented and every minute of the lecture was utilised. The profs were adamant that everyone attends all the lectures and thus made every lecture interactive enough for the students. Many lectures were taken by guest speakers and talked about relevant topics. Most of these speakers were entrepreneurs who described there own startup journey in detail, which served as case studies for this course. All the students were asked to generate a startup with a team of 4-8 people and apply all the things which were taught in the lectures as part of a course project.

Grading Scheme

25% Endsem
25% Midsem
30% group project
20% Assignments, Attendance, Class participation 

Feedback on Evaluation

The course has 5 assignment, mostly associated with working on the startup idea/ group project. The midsem and Endsem exams were open book and relatively easy. You can find all the answers in the slides given by the profs. Since the exams were easy the mean scores for the exams were pretty high. 

Nature of the Course: Time Needed

Heavy

Nature of The Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding

Moderate

Course Grading

Lenient

Course Grading Review

The grading was very good. More than 65% of students received AB and AA grade in this course. 

Reference Texts

NA

Comments

This is highly activity based course which requires a high time commitment. Most of the activities are done as part of the group project and are fun to do. This is a course in which after every lecture you will be mesmerized by the topics, case studies and innovation in the world.

We thank Samagra Jain for sharing the course review.

We thank the Chemical DAMP team for this review

Course review


Details

  • Year and semester taken: 2024-25, Autumn Sem
  • Professor: Anuradha Narasimhan, Rajkumar Hirwani,
  • Attendance criteria: Yes
  • Overall rating (on 10): 9


Prerequisites necessary (if any): None



Course difficulty

The tests were open-ended and were based on discussions in class. If we attend the classes, it will be an easy go. The grading during our semester was good, with around 65% students receiving an AA or AB.


Overview

The students learn the journey of building a startup through a project which is to build a startup from scratch, in a team of 6-7 students. Its main focus is customer discovery. Students will learn about pitching an idea, customer discovery, making a financial plan for a startup including business model canvas, P&L statement and Balance sheet. There will be lectures on finance, IP and 4-5 guest lectures by entrepreneurs from different domains.

Also this course is a prerequisite for most other ENT course.


Personal opinion: Overall, it was a great course. I would highly recommend attending all the classes to truly gain the full benefit—consistent participation is key to making the most of it.

title: Developing the Proof-of-Concept
description: The course is designed to enable students in converting their ideas with commercial potential into a tangible product/service.*Understanding the new product development process, *Learning to integrate the needs of the end-consumer into the process,*Acquiring the skill to apply the standard concepts and tools of the process, *Using the new product development process to conceiveown new product or service, *Developing the new product in a typical laboratory environmentand generating an effective go-tomarket strategy

reviews.body:
Instructor

Kums P Kumaresan

Semester

Autumn ‘21

Course Difficulty

Extremely easy

Time Commitment Required

Negligible, outside attending lectures

Grading Policy and Statistics

Extremely lenient, anyone who submitted all the assignments (regardless of the quality) and attended over 80% of the classes received an AA grade.
147 AAs out of 232

Attendance Policy

Compulsory, attendance directly reflected in the grade and was strictly monitored

Pre-requisites

None whatsoever

Evaluation Scheme

Fairly arbitrary, but the components were as follows (in no specific order): Attendance, 4 in-sem submissions and 1 final submission

Topics Covered in the Course

Understanding design principles and designing/building a few innovative prototypes
Presentation skills

Teaching Style

Classes were highly interactive, wherein the professor discussed the problem statements, offered feedback on submissions or showed random videos. A lot of activity happened (including submissions and announcements) exclusively on the official WhatsApp group of the course, so ensure you’re on there.

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

Assignments for this iteration of the course were all to be submitted in .ppt format, in the given template.
1) Introduce yourself to the world in full colour, bring an order to some stationery items, click a candid picture of your study table
2) Draw a single-line minimalistic sketch, explain 5 design principles out of 12 given in one slide each
3) Make an innovative product using a toilet plunger
4) Diya (Diwali lamp) design using household items
The final submission was to make a video and a presentation showcasing the above 4 assignments, as well as summarising the overall course experience

Feedback on Exams

None

Motivation for taking this course

Chill grade, as well as the unconventional content. Throwback to arts-and-crafts in school :-p

How strongly would I recommend this course?

One of the popular ‘chill courses’, so doesn’t specifically need my recommendation. Make sure you reg in time since there is a cap on the maximum size of the class.

When to take this course?

7th (placement) semester. Take it whenever you want an easy grade with minimal effort.

Going Forward

ENT608 - same prof, effectively the same course under a different name

Review By: Aditya Iyengar

We thank the Mechanical DAMP team for this review

Name: Sharvari
Roll Number: 200020123
Year of Study: to-be 4th Year
Section: S1
Course: ENT 606: Developing a Proof of Concept- Basic
Professor(s): P Kumaresan
Course Type: Institute Elective
Semester: Autumn (Odd)
Mode of Course: Online
Frequency of the Course: Every Year
Pre-Requisite: nan
Learning Outcome: Further course: ENT 608: Developing a Proof of Concept- Advanced
Pre-Midsem Course Content: There was no proper flow of content that was covered, it was a bit random and more of activity-based. We had classes on "How to make a ppt and unique templates" and design elements, perceptions etc. We also had a guest lecture on "Innovation of design" by Prof Chakravarthy Some of the assignments that were given were as follows:
1. Make the Paper disappear - we had to use origami to turn paper into something creative
2. Rorschach ink bolt - we had to make Rorschach ink blot and highlight what things we could see in that pattern
3. PPT- a presentation using unique template, elaborating a few words that were discussed in the class.
These assignments were related to the content taught in the classes.
Post-Midsem Course Content: There was no proper flow of content that was covered, it was a bit random and more of activity-based. We were taught to use the Inkspace and then as an assignment, we had to create some patterns using Inkspace and present it as a team. Content regarding various clock designs and art movements was covered in the classes. This was followed by group assignments on 1. Clock study
2. Concept generation and finalization (of clock design to be prepared by each team)
3. final design, drawing for prototyping
After this we started prototyping and making the clocks from scratch in the POC lab. Clock making was the end sem group project and there was no written end sem exam.
Slides [Ease of Understanding]: 5
Slides [Completeness]: 5
Slides [Depth]: 3
Slides [Usefulness for exams]: 5
Teaching method: It was a fun and activity-based course. Professor used some slides for explaining a few concepts but weren't provided. The assignments were related to the concepts taught in the class. (clarification: usefulness for exams : the only written exam was mid sem and the videos were provided which was the syllabus so it was very helpful, no slides were provided)
Grading Scheme: 1. Attendance: 10%
2. Midsem (MCQs): 25%
3. Assignments: 15%
4. End Sem Project : 50%

Feedback on Evaluation: Assignments were given almost after every class but very very easy and activity-based. Midsem was MCQs based on the video content shared by the Prof., was moderately difficult and required rote learning. End sem project was making a clock required efforts in design planning and execution
Nature of the Course: Time Needed: Very Light
Nature of the Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding: Very Light
Course Grading: Moderate
Course Grading Review: Very less AAs were given. Most of the people were on the same level as we had group projects and activity-based assignments. As long as you do all the assignments and project AB is easy to achieve. Midsem marks were determining factor to get AA as that was the only evaluation that could have varied as rest of the evaluations were open-ended and did not have very clear grading criteria.
Reference Texts: Midsem syllabus was "innovation by design case studies" YouTube Videos and questions were directly asked from the provided content.
Photo: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1nQWAh6XSRQ2sE7YDnBgA-krpLrOvoRcg
Comments: It is a fun course. Make sure you do all the assignments on time and attend classes to get clarity about the assignments and the content. It is an easy course and won't take a lot of your time. However, grading wasn't that great as only 1/6th of the class got AAs but 60% people got AAs+ABs

title: Developing a Proof of Concept - Advanced
description: This is a project based course and involves workshops in the Lab where you gain hands on experience in fundamental business skills required for venture creation. The expected output is a product designed for your own start-up idea. Teams will be evaluated based on the product development efforts during the course.

reviews.body:
Name: Prajwal Raut
Roll Number: 200020106
Year of Study: to-be 4th Year
Section: S2
Course: ENT 608
Professor(s): Kums P Kumaresan
Course Type: Institute Elective
Semester: Spring (Even)
Mode of Course: Hybrid
Frequency of the Course: Every Year
Pre-Requisite: ENT 606 (Developing a Proof-of-Concept Basic), not a hard pre-requisite but having done the course will enhance your chances to get the course as registrations are always greater than availability
Learning Outcome: The course explicitly will build your design thinking and how the small changes in form of design or structure can lead to profit or loss of the businesses. It will teach you to look at the product critically and analyze the aspects which other people mostly neglect. Prototype building is the best thing in this course I've came across.
Pre-Midsem Course Content: Pre-midsem was mostly online and we were taught some of the image combination ideas and after every class there was an assignment which was to be submitted, which indeed was very easy but required learning of various design software like Blender. We had to make a 3D structure of a finger ring which was the major assignment pre-midsem. Classes were very interesting and initially he focused on understanding of the product and it's design structure.
Post-Midsem Course Content: Post-midsem was mostly focused on Prototype development in teams, we had to work on an idea where we bring some design aspect into some already existing product. For our team we choose to convert plain white sneakers to some attractive funky sneakers and we got to learn a lot about how scaling up works and different resources required and teamwork most importantly, I still cherish the memories of the project.
Slides [Ease of Understanding]: 5
Slides [Completeness]: 4
Slides [Depth]: 5
Slides [Usefulness for exams]: 4
Teaching method: Best Prof for the Best Course Prof was more keen on our understanding of the material and keeping the class atmosphere light rather than teaching to pass some exam. The slides were in depth and clear explanations for all the things were being given with a light humor. You just can't miss this course.
Grading Scheme: There were no physical exams, some assignments were given in between. Typically the final grade was dependent mostly on the Final Prototype designing and then comes the assignment and then attendence. But exact weightage were never really remained the same, but going to classes regularly is enough for a good grade.
Feedback on Evaluation: Evaluation was on a lighter note only, Prof wanted us to try out things even if we are complete beginners, Prof was very chill and lenient throughout the course and in grading even.
Nature of the Course: Time Needed: Light
Nature of the Course: Level of Difficulty of Understanding: Light
Course Grading: Lenient
Course Grading Review: Most of class got either AA or AB, if you miss something without a genuine reason then you cannot expect a good grade because there are already very less assignments.
Reference Texts: NONE
Photo: nan
Comments: nan

title: Marketing and Finance for Entrepreneurs
description: Course teaches Entrepreneurial Marketing & Finance, from "Idea-to-IPO". 1. Personal branding, Team formation, Customer Discovery, Customer problem & persona, Market sizing 2. Customer Value Proposition, Rapid prototyping & Validation 3. Customer Branding, Lean advertising; Pricing; Go-to-Market & After-Market Plan, Supply Chain,Customer Channels 4. Sales strategy, skills & tools, Customer Relationship Management 5. Accounting: Assets, Liabilities, Revenue, Costs, Cash Flows etc. 6. Financial Statements: Balance sheet, Income - Cash flow - Equity statement, etc. 9. Corporate Finance: Objective of a firm; Time value of money; Financial Decision making 10. Startup Funding: Bootstrapping, Grants, Incubators & Accelerators, Angels, PE/VC, IPO Team Assignments : I. Business Plans II. Investor Pitch

reviews.body:
Instructor

Prof Devdip Purkayastha, Prof Rohan Chinchwadkar

Semester

Autumn ‘22

Course Difficulty

The course content wasn’t difficult but required religious efforts weekly. Assignment submissions were very important and you didn’t lose marks in them.

Time Commitment Required

Atleast 4.5 hrs a week including lectures

Grading Policy and Statistics

Lenient with 50% of class with 9+ and remaining with 7s and 8s

Attendance Policy

Expected 100% attendance

Pre-requisites

None required

Evaluation Scheme

25% Assignments
25% Mid Term Presentation
25% End Term Presentation
25% End Sem Exam

Topics Covered in the Course

Basics of building a start up - Customer Discovery, Customer problem & persona, Market sizing, Customer Value Proposition, Rapid prototyping, Go-to-Market & After-Market Plan, Supply Chain,Customer Channels, Accounting: Assets, Liabilities, Revenue, Costs, Cash Flows, Financial Statements: Balance sheet, Income - Cash flow - Equity statement, Time value of money; Financial Decision making, Startup Funding: Bootstrapping, Grants, Incubators & Accelerators, Angels, PE/VC, IPO Team

Teaching Style

We were to work on a startup in a diverse team of 5-6 members. All the assignments and presentations were to be done based on our start up idea. We were given sufficient time to finalize our idea with repeated discussions with the instructors.
The Mid term presentation was focussed on our idea, the feedback we received from customers, and also our future road map.
The End term presentation was mainly focussed on the company financials like revenue streams, 5 yr PnL, 5 yr Balance Sheet, etc.

Feedback on Exams

The EndSem was open book with ~40% of it based again on our startup. The rest was based on the case study discussed in class along with some MCQs.

Course Highlights

Entrepreneurship, Company Financials, Start Up Ideas, Start up journey

Review By: Keerthan Ramesh

We thank the Mechanical DAMP team for this review

title: Tech-Opportunities Identification
description: Recognizing Entrepreneurial Opportunities and Generating Ideas Types of Change (Technology, social and demographic factors, Economy and Industry factors) Transforming science into Business Technology, Industry and Competitor Analysis Feasibility Analysis Developing an Effective Business Model

reviews.body:
Course review


Details

  • Year and semester taken: 2nd year, 1st sem
  • Professor: Raj Hirwani
  • Attendance criteria: 80%
  • Time spent on course (h/week): Spending time over the class material doesn't matter in my opinion, because the exam we had was more situation based I'd say like here's the situation identify the opportunities or something else here. If you're good at it you'll do great otherwise average.
  • Overall rating (on 10): 5


Prerequisites necessary (if any)

No


Course difficulty

[i.e. how difficult the tests/assignments were and how strict the grading was?]

As I said the exam required us to think of various scenarios and identify the opportunities or threats it presents for example one question we were asked said, the government has launched a initiative to encourage the movement of fresh farm produce to all parts of the country, what technological opportunities does this present?

The professor required us to write in great detail about it like 6 or 7 ideas and opportunities but most of the people were only able to thin 3 or 4 and received 6 or 7 marks out of 10. So make of that what you will.


Overview

None as such



Personal opinion

The assignments and presentations became a bit annoying, coupled with the facts that the professor teaches in a manner where I was struggling to stay awake and quite a weird exam, I'd say I didn't enjoy this course.

title: Classical Mechanics I
description: Review of Newton`s law of motion, frames of reference, rotating frames, centrifugal and coriolis forces. Free and constrained motion, D`Alemberts principle and Lagrange`s equation of first kind. Lagrangian formulation, Hamilton342200231s equation of motion. Variational principles. Canonical transformation and Poisson Bracket. Hamilton Jacobi theory and action angle variables.Periodic motion, small oscillations, normal coordinates, Central force, Kepler`s Laws and Rutherford scattering.

reviews.body:
Year : 2014-15 Autumn

Professor : Prof. Dipan K. Ghosh (Physics)

Overview:

This is a core course for EP 2nd year students, and can be taken by any other student. Electrical Engg. students can (as of 2014)  tag it as one of the two non-EE courses out of five courses they require for Honors.

It is recommended if you are interested in Physics. It provides a basis for most physics courses, although it isn’t a strict pre-req for several Physics courses.

Pre-requisites:

None

Course Contents:

Disclaimer: This is how the course was run in 2014 Autumn. It can vary depending on the instructor.

The course aims on taking the student through the two mathematical formulations of classical mechanics that are equivalent to (but in many situations more tractable and insightful than) Newton’s Laws. Namely, the Lagrangian and Hamiltonian formulations. The first half of the course concentrates on the Lagrangian, and the second half mainly on the Hamiltonian.

The focus of the course is also on Using the tools you learn, and being able to use them well, rather than on being able to derive these tools.

Evaluation:

There were a few assignments (basically solving the tutorials and handing them in) which were graded only if you needed the marks to save yourself from failing, but were a really good way of ensuring you did well on the exams, since it provided practice.

Quizzes, Midsem and Endsem exams were conducted, and were all based on using the tools taught in classes. Hence “solving-oriented’.

Lectures:

The Professor is friendly and can be approached to clear doubts and for other discussions.

However, lectures can seem monotonous and slow, especially for the mathematically faint-hearted.

Overall, the course was moderate in difficulty, not too difficult. Attending classes and solving the problem sets and tutorials regularly is enough to get a decent grade. Missing a few lectures can leave you in a tricky position as material is extremely linked together. This problem is exacerbated by the fact that you may not know any EP students to help you out.

Study Material and Refernces:

Class notes are uploaded after each section is covered, and they are a good way to review the course material. Goldstein’s book on Classical Mechanics is the recommended book for this course if you want other sources to help consolidate your knowledge. However it is not a textbook and is not followed in class as much as it is a reference.

Grading:

Grading is such that not too many people get AA’s (It is difficult to get a 10) but it is also difficult to get low grades. So moderate grades is what the Professor tries to hand out.

Reviewed by Jay Mardia<jay.mardia19@gmail.com>

Link to the actual review

title: Quantum Mechanics I
description: Wave functions, superposition principle, wave packets. Schrodinger equation, probability and current densities. Expectation values and Ehrenfest"s theorem. General formalism. Linear vectors and operators in Hilbert space, observables, commuting operators, momentum representations and uncertainty principle. Unitary transformations, Schrodinger and Heisenberg representations, equa-tions of motion. Applications: One dimensional potential problems, linear harmonic oscillator, polynomial solutions, creation and annihilation operators. Central forces, angular momentum, spherical harmon-ics, spin, addition of angular momenta motion in a well, free and bound states in Coulomb potential.

Link to the actual review

Instructors:

(1) Munish Kumar Chandel (Solid Waste)
(2) Virendra Sethi (Air Waste)
(3) Amritanshu Shriwastav (Water Waste)

Session: 2019-2020

Prerequisites: N/A

Course content and structure:

The course comprised of 3 modules:
Module A: Air Pollution

Description: An introduction to understanding air quality management, fundamental processes of meteorology, Gaussian plume model. Air Pollutants – Gaseous and particulate, Criteria pollutants, ambient and source standards, Aerosols: Characterisation of aerosols, size distributions, measurement methods; Transport behaviour: diffusion, sedimentation, inertia; Visibility; principles of particulate control systems.

Goal: To help students understand the key steps in managing air quality

Module B: Water Treatment

Description: Discussion of water quality constituents and introduction to the design and operation of water and wastewater treatment processes.

Goal: To help students develop basic understanding of physical, chemical, and biological phenomena involved in design and operation of water and wastewater treatment facilities.

Module C: Solid Waste Management and Climate Change

Description: First part of the module would cover different aspects of solid and hazardous waste management. Second part would focus on the climate change and greenhouse gas emissions, and how different technologies would reduce the greenhouse gas emissions.

Goal: To help students develop basic understanding of solid and hazardous waste management, and the climate change and its possible causes.

Feedback on lectures:

All three profs followed ppt style delivery of lectures where slides were presented and explained in the class. Usual insti policy of attendance was enforced. Explanation given in class and lecture slides were sufficient from exam point of view.

Feedback on tutorials and exams: 

All three modules had assignments which contributed towards 1/3rd of the weight age. The assignments involved were of descriptive type and involved reading articles and case studies. The end sem exam consisted of 2/3rd weightage having three sections for three modules. Water module had all MCQs but had a very harsh 100% negative marking. Other two modules had a mix of short and long answer questions. Almost all questions were picked straight from the lecture slides.

Difficulty (on a scale of 1 being very easy to 5 being very hard): 2

Textbooks & References:

Profs give a list of books for reference at start of the course, but the best strategy is to focus solely on lecture slides and use google to get more details about the slide contents.

Grading Statistics: 

AA- 69
AB- 122
BB- 87
BC- 60
CC- 29
CD- 4
DD- 4
FR- 6
II- 2

Additional Comments & what you learnt from the course: 

Grading for assignments is usually very liberal so final exam becomes the grade decider. Be very through with the lecture slides and revise properly.

Review by – Anurag Kumar (aforanurag99@gmail.com)

Course Name: ES200
Review By: Anjali Yadav
Name of the Prof: K.V. Venkatesh
Year the course was taken in: 2
Attendance Criteria: 0
Difficulty of evaluation(How difficult the test and assignment were and how difficult was the grading): 3
Grading Scheme: Module A: Air Quality 34% ( (7+7) 2 Assignments + 20 Final exam) Module B: Solid Waste Management and Climate Change 33% (10 Assignment + 23 Final exam) Module C: Water Quality Management 33% (10 Assignment +23 Final exam) AA 58, AB 87, BB 74, BC 54, CC 36, CD 15, DD 3, FR 6, II 4, PP 298, W 1, Total 636
Time spent on course(hours per week): 4
Pre-requisites(if any): none
Overview(Expect to learn and future prospects): Module A: Air Quality Key concepts of air pollution and air quality management Module B: Solid Waste Management and Climate Change First part: Different aspects of municipal solid and biomedical waste management. Second part: Climate change and greenhouse gas emissions, and how different technologies could reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Module C: Water Quality Management Water as a resource, water issues, water quality, and health, conventional water treatment and wastewater treatment, Alternate methods for water/wastewater methods The course content mostly consisted of environmental topics. The content of the course is not very interesting or engaging. There are certain things which you will find very trivial and there would be things which you have to take it as they are. The content is not very intellectually stimulating and you may lose out interest in the classes.
Course Rating: 2

Instructor

Munish Kumar Chandel, Swatantra Pratap Singh, Virendra Sethi

Section

S1

Semester

Spring ‘20

Course Difficulty

The course is not very difficult, and easy to score well in by being regular with the classes. The professors discuss the question banks in class in great detail, and most of the questions are directly from them or are derivatives of them. For this course, there is definitely some mugging up to do, and it’s not a big deal if you have been following the course but is quite difficult and tedious if you are looking at the syllabus just before the examination.

Time Commitment Required

3-5 hours per week

Grading Policy and Statistics

The grading statistics were decent, but not too lenient.
The grading of the assignments was very erratic and non-transparent (no feedback). It also varied a lot as the TAs had not been given a detailed marking scheme for assignments, and some TAs were very lenient whereas others were very strict (especially in the Municipal Solid Waste module). This was rectified to an extent after a lot of complaints from the students.

Pre-requisites

No prerequisites at all

Evaluation Scheme

Module A - Air Quality (34%):
Homeworks (2) - 14%
Endsem - 20%

Module B - Municipal Solid Waste (33%):
Homework - 15%
Endsem - 18%

Module C - Pollution (33%):
Homework - 10%
Endsem - 23%

Topics Covered in the Course

Air Quality: Pollution standards, diseases, non-containment cities, dispersion models

Municipal Solid Waste management: composting, dumping methods, energy recovery, recycling, Biomedical Waste management, Climate Change, Green Technology

Pollution: Environmental problems, ecosystem and biodiversity, environmental awareness and sustainable development, water resources and quality, pollution, water treatment system

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

AQ assignments were stimulating and required research. The first assignment required us to watch two movies of opposing views on global warming, and we had to turn in a critical review / essay discuss our views and the movies. The second assignment required us to analyze an air quality situation in our hometown, and come up with a plan to deal with it using the allocated budget.

MSW assignment was a mix of research and opinion based questions, with a majority of the assignment focusing on MSW management in our hometown, and the rest focusing on MSWM in other countries, plastic usage, global warming, etc.

Pollution (module C) assignment required us to research, analyze and report the water quality and supply situation in our hometown.

Feedback on Exams

The final exams of all three modules were of moderate difficulty. The numerical questions from AQ module or the guesstimate question from the MSW module were fairly simple to answer. The other questions were mostly directly from the slide, or were result of direct class discussion from the question banks shared for each module.

Course Importance

The concepts taught in this course, raise awareness about important environmental issues, how they affect us, and the methods and shortcomings of the current management process. It is of great importance for the future generation of leaders and decision makers to be aware of such issues, and consider them as a significant factor of influence while taking actions that can affect the environment and the populace’s long-term well-being.

ES 200 Review By: Shubham Lohiya

We thank the Mechanical DAMP team for this review

Instructors:

(1) Munish Kumar Chandel (Solid Waste)
(2) Virendra Sethi (Air Waste)
(3) Amritanshu Shriwastav (Water Waste)

Session: 2019-2020

Prerequisites: N/A

Course content and structure:

Module A: Air Quality
34% ( (7+7) 2 Assignments + 20 Final exam)
Key concepts of air pollution and air quality management

Module B: Solid Waste Management and Climate Change
33% (10 Assignment + 23 Final exam)
First part: Different aspects of municipal solid and biomedical waste management.
Second part: Climate change and greenhouse gas emissions, and how different technologies could reduce greenhouse gas emissions.


Module C: Water Quality Management
33% (10 Assignment +23 Final exam)
Water as a resource, water issues, water quality, and health, conventional water treatment and wastewater treatment, Alternate methods for water/wastewater methods

Feedback on lectures:

All three of the instructors used slides in the lectures. These slides were made available on Moodle. Professor Virendra Sethi was really enthusiastic and made every possible effort to make the lectures more interesting and engaging.
The course content mostly consisted of environmental topics. The content of the course is not very interesting or engaging. There are certain things which you will find very trivial and there would be things which you have to take it as they are. The content is not very intellectually stimulating and you may lose out interest in the classes. Although, it’s good to attend the classes and listen properly as it will significantly reduce the time you need to invest in this course, just before exams, to achieve a good grade in this course.

Feedback on tutorials and exams: 

There were no tutorials for this course. However, for each of the modules, you had one assignment (two in the case of Air module) which carried some weightage in your final evaluation. The assignments were based on topics covered in class, some extra reading material uploaded on moodle (along with the lecture slides), and a few references from the internet. The assignments were pretty easy and required you to frame subjective answers to some of the problem statements.

There was only one final exam for this half-semester course. The exam consisted of three parts, one for each module. The questions were based on the lecture slides, reading material uploaded, and the assignments given. The Water module part was a bit harsh on the marking scheme, you had just a few MCQs of 3 or 4 marks each with a negative marking of -2 or -3. The grading wasn’t very lenient and they did not entertain a lot of cribs. Although, the Air module Instructor was a bit generous here and gave you the liberty to write your cribs on a sheet of paper and submit it to him.

Difficulty (on a scale of 1 being very easy to 5 being very hard): 1

Textbooks & References:

There was no specific textbook or reference with which any of the instructors strictly adhered. However, each of the instructors individually provided you with information about different references you can use.

Grading Statistics: 

AA 58
AB 87
BB 74
BC 54
CC 36
CD 15
DD 3
FR 6
II 4
PP 298
W 1
Total 636

Additional Comments & what you learnt from the course: 

This is a compulsory course for every student in our college. The course appears as a core course in the seventh semester in the Department of Electrical Engineering. I was not sure about a Minor and hence didn’t take one in my third semester. Thus, I picked up this course in my third semester to save my credits in the seventh semester for some advanced courses in my own department or any other department. I knew of some people who have done the same, picked it up as an Institute elective, and later retagged it.

Review by – Anjali Yadav (anjaliyadavay3008@gmail.com)
